  • The Dawes case (Book 2 flashback — Rudy's first King County case): Not long after buying the Inn, county official Executive Dawes came to him — his son and the son's fiancée had been kidnapped, ransomed at a clearing called Boot Knife. Rudy's identity/reputation as a hunter isn't explained on the page; Dawes simply says he "hear[d] you... hunt things." The ransom drop failed (police involvement, against Dawes's instructions, may have spooked whoever was meant to collect it), and a second envelope arrived demanding a solo retry — accompanied by a severed adult finger. The outcome isn't shown; it's recalled only as backstory once an identical MO resurfaces with Jeff Speno's son in the present day, strongly implying the case was never truly closed.

docs.title: Growth of a Network and New Role (Mid 2000s–Present)
docs.text: • Building a Hunter Network: As Rudy’s reputation spread, he began cultivating a network of like-minded problem solvers, drawing on contacts from Colorado’s Western Slope and parts of Utah. Recognizing that supernatural cases are sparse in any single county, he established a loose network of hunters and investigators who could respond to issues as needed, covering a broad region from King County to Green River, Utah.

docs.title: - Home Base at Shepherd’s Inn: Rudy now lives in a Winnebago parked behind Shepherd's Inn, with access to the Inn’s kitchen, bathroom, and shower. His old camper still stands beside the RV, but he no longer uses it for living. He maintains a low profile, generally leaving only for critical issues within Shepherd Junction, while primarily acting as “dispatch” for the hunters in his network. Deliberately not a permanent trailer home — it's something he can hitch up and leave with if necessary.
docs.text: • Role as Dispatch and “Superior”: Rudy coordinates jobs, hears rumors, and provides guidance to hunter cells, passing on tips when something unusual pops up. He serves as a mentor and “superior” for clients who request one, establishing himself as the go-to resource for anyone in need of help with paranormal issues. In many ways, he has become a legend in the local supernatural circles, a central hub for dealing with King County’s otherworldly dangers.

docs.title: Current Role in King County
docs.text:
• Relocation to King County: Rudy moved to King County, Colorado, Colorado, after his discharge from the United States Marine Corps. He came specifically to be near Phoebe, who is catatonic and confined to an asylum in King County. His sole mission is to protect her from the unknown forces he believes are still after them.

  • Personal Mission: Rudy has long suspected that the deaths and disappearances of the other The Monster Squad (group) members are directly related to the supernatural events they experienced. This suspicion keeps him vigilant and isolated, always anticipating that whatever targeted them before might come for him or Phoebe next.

  • The obsessed-hunter type, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): He became this well before the Marines — as a teenager, in the years right after the portal incident, not as a result of his military service. That obsession is what actually drove Horace and Sean away, gradually, over a spread-out stretch of time — not some external force hunting them down, whatever Rudy himself has come to believe. Horace wanted to be safe and pulled away entirely; Sean stayed invested longer but the two of them couldn't keep working together over personality conflict. Rudy's belief in a systematic threat may be, at least in part, a story he tells himself rather than the literal truth — see The Monster Squad (group).

  • Relationship with Local Law Enforcement: King County law enforcement discreetly acknowledges Rudy’s expertise, occasionally recommending him off the books for cases that seem beyond standard protocol.

docs.title: Current Reputation and Roleplaying Hints
docs.text:
• Local Legend: Among locals and those “in the know,” Rudy is the guy to contact for supernatural issues. Known for his reliability, discretion, and no-nonsense approach, he’s trusted by hunters, police, and wary townsfolk alike. He’s respected but feared, especially as he rarely reveals specifics, keeping details about supernatural threats close to his chest.
  • Combat Style and Approach: While Rudy doesn’t often go out in the field anymore, when he does, he’s known for his aggressive style—jumping into fights without weighing the odds. He’ll throw himself into a brawl if necessary, especially if something disrupts Shepherd Junction. For most cases, though, he prefers to act as the mentor, guide, and dispatcher, making sure others can do the work without his direct involvement.

docs.title: Key Relationships
docs.text:
• Jenn: His grounding force in What Wasn't and What Was. Where Rudy reads every hardship as a supernatural sign or consequence, Jenn refuses to mythologize pain — she's lived through real hardship with no curse behind any of it, and her non-magical read on events is played as correct, not naive. Confirmed: Jenn does not know about the supernatural, and Rudy intends to keep it that way — she sees his humanity, not his war; she cares for him because he's kind when he doesn't have to be, not because she knows what he's faced.
  • Caretaking behaviors (established via draft dialogue): Brings her peanut brittle and licorice when she's had a bad shift; once pulled her car out of a ditch at 2am and never told a soul; calls her "kid" — established as armor, not an insult.
  • Jealousy/protectiveness tell: Reacts badly to Nathan Hale saying her name "like he owned it" — pushes back even though Hale doesn't mean it that way.
  • The Hollow Creek rumor, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Hale tells Jenn a specific rumor about Rudy: that he shot and killed a man near "Hollow Creek" in the middle of the night — no body, no charges, just someone who stopped showing up and Rudy with blood on his boots and no explanation. This is confirmed as the young-generation vampire kill above — true, not exaggerated, and not a secret Rudy could ever comfortably explain even if he wanted to. Jenn's response is to trust Rudy anyway ("Rudy's never lied to me... it makes him real," "I'll take dangerous and honest over clean and fake any day").
  • The "bear" framing (brainstormed thematic dialogue, not confirmed as adopted lines): Riffing on the "would you rather be alone with a strange man or a bear" meme, the idea floated is that Jenn would choose Rudy over the bear specifically because his danger is honest and known, unlike most men's — candidate lines include "I've seen him angry — and I've seen him choose not to be" and "Neither is a bear. But at least a bear doesn't pretend to be my friend first."

  • Reciprocal protectiveness: Per a brainstormed future ("Book 4") beat, Rudy's protective instinct extends even to people he can't stand — including Hale — because "someone has to" stand between danger and people. See the Book 4 note on Book Order and Series Structure.

  • Phoebe: Once a bright and energetic child, Phoebe is now catatonic, likely due to the trauma from the events in California. Rudy feels a deep responsibility for her, viewing his presence in King County, Colorado as a way to protect her.

  • Scary German Guy: The mage who helped them during the California events. His role in Rudy’s life was brief but pivotal. If he’s alive, Rudy might still consider him an ally, though his whereabouts are currently unknown.

  • The Monster Squad (group) Survivors: Rudy suspects foul play tied to their shared past, but per Horace and Sean's own confirmed fates, the truth is closer to his own growing obsession pushing them away than anything hunting them down. Their fate still haunts him, and still keeps him vigilant against a threat that — for at least those two — was never really there.

  • Phoebe, dream-contact confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): At some point, Phoebe reaches Rudy through a dream — she's secretly capable of astral-wandering the Umbra despite being physically drugged and catatonic (see her own page). Exact scene/timing deliberately left open for now.

  • Detective Marcus Cole: Rudy's contact at Brindle PD in What Wasn't and What Was — Cole gets him access to reports, footage, and patrol logs off the books when Rudy needs eyes on something before it gets buried in paperwork.

  • Gil: A Shepherd's Inn regular twenty-ish years Rudy's senior — legally Robert Gilbert, a 22-year Navy diver and 20-plus-year long-haul trucker, biker (road name "Gillman"), devoted grandfather. An "armchair Hunter" who knows the lore and the talk but has never actually done the work, which Rudy is fine leaving alone. Rudy doesn't talk about his own service, so Gil almost certainly doesn't know about the dishonorable discharge — whether Rudy would ever risk explaining it to him is an open question.

  • Jamie: Rudy was leaving the trailer park at sunup, having quietly left Tina's place while she still slept, when he found young Jamie running from a Huntsman. He U-turned, pulled them into his truck, and rammed the Huntsman outright (a gun was never an option — he'd have been firing toward the trailer park) — which cemented Jamie's loyalty to him. Repeated attempts to place Jamie in foster care failed; Jamie kept running back to him. Jamie eventually disappeared into the wider world (hopping trains, living off-grid) but returns occasionally to Shepherd Junction. Rudy knows about and hates Jamie's drug use (used to suppress memories of Arcadia and force sleep) but understands it. Already loves Jamie and knows they're different well before he ever sees their true Mien in the Hedge — when he does, he doesn't reach for a weapon or flinch; his first reaction is practical ("Am I gonna come out looking like that?"), and the shock is less about the monstrousness than about how much of Jamie stays the same underneath it. Confirmed (2026-07-18, decided live): Jamie has a one-directional supernatural "imprint" on Rudy — they always know his rough direction and distance, Warder-bond style — but Rudy does not have the reverse sense for Jamie. See Jamie.

  • Tina: Involved with her roughly eight years before the events of What Wasn't and What Was — started as a no-strings casual thing, but Rudy caught real feelings and pulled away rather than risk dragging her into his dangerous, secret-keeping life. Left her place at sunup after she fell asleep, without the conversation, and found Jamie fleeing the Huntsman minutes later. Briefly wonders if her son Ben might be his; Tina confirms Ben isn't, and that she'd never have met Ben's father — still around, "a pretty awesome guy" — if Rudy hadn't left.

Keepsakes: Keeps a drawer at Shepherd's Inn with a Monster Squad photo and a crayon drawing Phoebe made at age six (a monster being banished by a girl with light in her hands). A flashback fragment tied to this: her voice, "Don't let go, Rudy!", swallowed by a void.

What spare money is for: Rudy doesn't hoard the cash he doesn't spend on himself — he keeps it ready for bailing someone out, or burying someone who doesn't have family left.

Gothic sensory detail (offered, not confirmed adopted): Rudy bleeds a little every time he reads a certain case file — just enough to stain the page.

"Good enough": Rudy's habit of rigging broken things at the Inn with duct tape and twine rather than properly fixing them (an early draft example: a broken railing), muttering "Good enough" — a recurring line/motif in What Wasn't and What Was that echoes later when Leanne must decide whether love and belief are "good enough" to hold Caleb in existence. Full phrase, confirmed while discussing author catchphrases: "Not perfect. Just good enough."

Phone manner: Answers the Shepherd's Inn landline with a clipped "Shepherd's."

Cigarettes as avoidance: Buying cigarettes at LeGrande Station, despite finding the place an eyesore, is established as routine avoidance behavior rather than just habit.

A blessing/curse: Absolute strangers tend to be too open and forward with him — people blurt things out, ask invasive questions, overshare. Established via a gas station clerk pressing him about Jamie's gender (see Jamie).

Weapons/equipment (per The Prodigal Son): Carries a Colt 1911 strapped at the small of his back, under his belt. For a stealthier stakeout/search (scaling the wall into Aspenview Estates), he opted for a flashlight, compound bow, and a full quiver of broadhead arrows instead of a firearm.

Confirmed military background and civilian gun choices (per "Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export): Was a Marine Scout Sniper during the Gulf War, trained on the M16A2 and the Barrett M82A1; carried an M1911 sidearm in service (consistent with the Colt 1911 above). Deliberately avoids anything that reads as tactical/military/"prepper" in civilian life — his rifles are chosen specifically to look at home in a truck-cab gun rack: a wood-stocked Ruger Mini-14 Ranch Rifle (.223/5.56, the closest civilian analogue to the M16A2) and a walnut-stocked Remington 700 BDL in .30-06 (standing in for the Barrett's long-range role — Dave's own reasoning: "In his role as a civilian monster-hunter, he probably has little to no need of the sniper rifle's range — 800+ meters of the Remington 700 is perfectly fine"). Defaults to a compound bow when he can't legally or practically carry a gun (age, price, jurisdiction) — consistent with his established bow use in The Prodigal Son.

Gun-drawing code, confirmed (2026-07-16, decided live — from "Westernizing What Wasn't"): Rudy never draws as intimidation or a resting hand — the gun stays concealed at the small of his back (see the Colt 1911 above) until he's already decided lethal force is the outcome, not before. His actual decision tree, in order: talk his way out → leave → contain the threat some other way → protect someone else some other way → only then draw. Drawing is the last item on the list, not a bargaining chip — consistent with the truck-not-gun choice in the Prologue confrontation with The Huntsman (see Jamie), where firing wasn't a safe option and ramming was.

Additional confirmed gear (same chat): Keeps his actual military-grade firearms out of the truck entirely. Repacks his own shotgun shells with shot cast from melted-down estate-sale silverware/jewelry silver (cheaper than buying silver outright) — marks the silver shells somehow (a notch, a Sharpie stripe, different-colored hulls) so he can tell them apart by feel in the dark. Keeps a toolbox behind his truck seat with an ordinary "truck tools" tray on top, and underneath: an extra barrel, specialty rounds, and similar hunting-adjacent contraband.

Rock salt shells and replaceable barrels, confirmed (per "Making What Wasn't Scooby-Doo," full data export): Alongside the silver (which he knows, not just believes, kills werewolves) and the later iron test shells (see below), Rudy also keeps a few shotgun shells packed with rock salt — heard it works on ghosts, or at worst it's still a usable non-lethal round on a home invader. His shotgun barrel is replaceable and he keeps a few spares of varying length on hand, whether or not he's actually swapped one recently. He's not going to buy a metal lathe or start hand-casting precision ammunition — his approach stays scrounged and practical, not engineered.

Confirmed limits, vs. "monster hunter" archetypes (per "Making What Wasn't Scooby-Doo," full data export — Dave's own comparison points, contrasting Rudy with Sam and Dean Winchester): Rudy isn't out looking for supernatural cases the way the Winchesters are — cases come to him, and only some turn out to be supernatural at all (the mundane ones just don't make it onto the page, unless they're genuinely interesting). He's never exorcised a demon in his life. He knows salt lines across a threshold sometimes work — not reliably, not as a guaranteed ward. He might use a magical talisman or a very rudimentary spell in a pinch, but that's never his go-to, and he'd never drink anything like demon blood for power. He has no weapon guaranteed to work against everything, and nothing that reliably drops a creature in one hit — a bullet to the brain or heart tends to work, within reason, assuming the thing in question has a brain or heart and can be affected by normal bullets in the first place. That uncertainty is the point: Rudy's toolkit is built from tested contingencies, not a standardized loadout. His military background isn't supernatural lore training either — it's mundane warfare doctrine (recon, observation, asymmetric-threat handling) that happens to get applied against supernatural targets, not knowledge of the targets themselves.

Meta/craft note — case selection (per "Making What Wasn't Scooby-Doo," full data export — Dave's own framing): The series follows the same logic as Rudy himself — it dramatizes the supernatural cases and only references the mundane ones in passing, unless a mundane case is interesting enough to earn page time on its own.

The cold iron dagger, confirmed build (per "Making What Wasn't Scooby-Doo," full data export — Dave's own design; not a pre-existing "war days" weapon, see the retirement note below): Built specifically for the Huntsman case, not carried beforehand. Rudy tracks down a pre-1890 railroad spike — demonstrably iron, not mild steel — which isn't hard given Shepherd Junction's old rail-siding/mining history. He cuts the whole spike lengthwise into a tapered blade with a deliberately thick spine for strength, grinds the edge in with an angle grinder rather than hand-hammering it, then finishes it with traditional sharpening. Roughly two inches of tang. The handle is a piece of pipe, vice-flattened (and further ovaled so the blade indexes properly by feel) to friction-fit the tang, held on with a nuts-and-bolts or rivet solution, wrapped in leather or paracord against sweat and blood, with a plumbing cap for a pommel and no guard. Ugly, asymmetrical, obviously homemade — "functional-made," not artisan-made, and Rudy never tries to make it anything more than that. Used to stab and slice, establishing both that the blade works and that the Huntsman is vulnerable to iron. After the Huntsman fight, Rudy packs a few shotgun shells with shot rough-cut from spare iron railroad spikes, labeled something like "OLD SPIKE SHOT — TEST" — hedging a hypothesis, not confirming a rule, the same way the silver shells above are known (not just believed) to work on werewolves while the iron shells stay experimental. See The Huntsman.

Confirmed investigative philosophy — "no more than two questions" (per "Making What Wasn't Scooby-Doo," full data export — Dave's own stated rule): Every case has six questions: who, what, when, where, why, how. The supernatural gets to answer at most two of them, never three, or the mystery starts feeling arbitrary rather than investigable. Rudy's actual method follows from this: assume a mundane explanation first, gather evidence, eliminate ordinary possibilities, and only expand the model to include the supernatural if the evidence still doesn't fit — then test that explanation just as rigorously as the mundane one. He isn't a lore expert and carries no encyclopedic bestiary of monster types; supernatural knowledge in King County is fragmented, secondhand, and often contradictory, so finding out who knows about a given threat, and whether they can be trusted, is itself part of the investigation. Rudy weighs any supernatural claim by rough tiers of confidence — rumor, trusted testimony, personal observation, repeated confirmation — and his notebook of tested field observations (which weapon or ward worked against which specific threat, not a general bestiary) is one of his most valuable tools, precisely because it never assumes yesterday's lesson automatically applies to tomorrow's case.

Per the Detective Noir alternate outline, now confirmed non-canon (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session): That outline characterized him as drinking too much and talking to people who aren't there — retired along with the outline; his established sober, controlled characterization stands (a similar "drunk" framing was explicitly rejected once before for a different scene; see Out of Scope Ideas). Dave has also directly confirmed the leg injury/plate and the cold iron knife "from his war days" are not canon — no notable wounds, plates, screws, or shrapnel from his Marine service or otherwise, and no established history with the fae that would give him a reason to keep cold iron on hand the way he keeps silver rounds "just in case" for lycanthropes. Growing up in/near a trailer park is confirmed canon — consistent with his established poor upbringing.

Per the Save the Cat alternate outline: Implies Rudy swore off using his old magic/hunter methods at some point before the story starts, and going into the Hedge means breaking that vow (undeveloped — what exactly he swore off, and why, isn't specified). In this outline's escalation, Rudy himself starts to forget Caleb's face as the Huntsman closes in — in tension with the established "Rudy is the only one who still says Caleb's name" beat on the main outline (see What Wasn't and What Was). In the "Stage 12: The Fallout" epilogue, he keeps a crayon drawing inscribed "To Mom" taped to his window (yet another version of the recurring ambiguous "drawing" object — see Caleb) and isn't fully sure he's really left the Hedge, but holds onto the fact that he remembers Caleb as proof he won.

Rudy's will and a mysterious successor (now substantially clarified by the full "Escalating Tensions Rewrite" transcript): Rudy keeps a will that isn't fixed — he updates it at 3am over cheap coffee, crosses names out with motor oil still on his hands, mutters "asshole doesn't deserve it," and keeps it in a drawer under his cleanest gun. In one framing (from the "Supernatural Horror Publishers" chat), Nathan Hale's name is in it by default/technicality, and Rudy wants to cross it out "with the thickest black line" if an unnamed "she" shows up and "earns it" — implied to inherit Shepherd's Inn instead of Hale.

"She" is Amber, confirmed final (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): from the "Ghosts of King County" actual-play campaign, now confirmed by Dave as Book 3 in the same numbered series as What Wasn't and What Was (Book 1) — one shared continuity, not a separate parallel use of the setting. The earlier Jenn theory (from a brainstormed "Book 4" letter) is superseded, rougher, earlier brainstorming, not a live alternative. The mechanism: the Clara McKenzie case exposes Amber to the supernatural directly and proves to Rudy she can handle it; that, combined with her existing bar experience, is why he hires her to cover shifts at Shepherd's Inn — solving the practical problem of never having to invent a story for coming home covered in blood. See Amber and Book Order and Series Structure.

Rudy's letter to Hale (drafted in full, brainstormed "Book 4" beat — see Book Order and Series Structure and Nathan Hale):

Fuck you. Seriously, fuck you. There's no human who has ever existed that I've hated more. I hate your eyesore of a gas station and your soggy cheese fries and that obnoxious truck. You are a blight on this earth and everyone is worse for knowing you. But you took care of Jenn when I couldn't. You love this goddamn town as much as I do. I fucking hate you, but I respect you. The Inn is yours. Fix it up, bulldoze it, whatever... I don't care. But now the only person you can complain about when it comes to property lines and land value is you. Again, fuck you. - Rudy PS. If you sell the place, give Jenn half. (DON'T READ THIS NEXT PART OUT LOUD) — keep Jenn out of [PLACE TBD]. You know why.

The secret cache (unnamed, undeveloped): The letter references a place — never named, candidate mundane-sounding cover names floated include "The Trapdoor," "Locker 9," or "Maggie's Shed" — where Rudy keeps undeniable proof of the supernatural world and his part in it, sealed (per one suggestion) with cold iron and sage. Hale is meant to understand instantly that Jenn must never go there, without needing an explanation. Never located or described further — open worldbuilding thread.

docs.title: Notable Traits / Skills
docs.text:
• Military Training: After serving time in juvenile detention, Rudy joined the United States Marine Corps, where he gained significant combat and survival skills. He was discharged as the sole survivor of a dangerous mission, deepening his survivor’s guilt.

  • Survivor’s Guilt: Having survived multiple traumatic events (both supernatural and military), Rudy carries a heavy sense of responsibility. His experiences have hardened him, making him resourceful but also distrustful and isolated.

  • Meta/craft note — modeled on a real film: Rudy and the Monster Squad material are explicitly and openly modeled on the 1987 movie The Monster Squad — see The Monster Squad (Movie) for the naming decisions, the in-universe propaganda-film convention, and the confirmed unconscious movie-line-callback device (draft lines include Rudy saying "I'm in this goddamn club, aren't I?" and "Hey asshole... You looked").

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025 ("Making What Wasn't Scooby-Doo," full data export): Confirmed investigative "no more than two questions" rule; confirmed the actual build of the cold iron dagger used on the Huntsman (railroad spike, ground not hand-hammered, pipe handle) and the post-fight experimental iron shotgun shells; confirmed rock salt shells and spare replaceable barrels; confirmed specific limits distinguishing him from "monster hunter" archetypes (never exorcised a demon, no guaranteed one-hit weapon, no demon blood/power-drinking); confirmed his military background is mundane warfare doctrine applied to supernatural targets, not supernatural knowledge itself; confirmed a meta/craft note that the series dramatizes supernatural cases and only references mundane ones in passing.
  • (pre-existing, date unknown): Background, military history, Shepherd's Inn establishment, hunter network, and existing Key Relationships (Phoebe, Scary German Guy, Monster Squad survivors) established.
  • 2025 ("Character Name Considerations," full data export): Confirmed the direct Monster Squad (1987 film) modeling and the movie-line-callback device; see The Monster Squad (Movie).
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed Rudy became the "obsessed hunter" type as a teenager, well before the Marines — and that this is what actually drove Horace and Sean away over time, not a confirmed external threat. See The Monster Squad (group).
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting," corrected on second pass): Added the bow-and-arrow weapon of choice from the original Dawes case (stands). The left-hand scar from the present-day climax is flagged unconfirmed, not dropped — that draft's fight staging conflicts with an already-locked version elsewhere. See The Three Generations Ritual.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation," decided live, grill-canon session): Floated (by Dave himself) that both his parents were Hunters, meant to surface as a reveal inside the "God's House" bottle episode. See God's House.
  • 2025 ("Rudy Jenn Age Gap," full data export): Confirmed age/birthdate, the first pregnancy scare at 16, and the Book 1 beat where he briefly (and incorrectly) suspects a child is his own.
  • 2025 ("Astrology Birthday Guess," full data export): Confirmed the AWOL-with-government-firearms escalation behind his dishonorable discharge, the Task Force VALKYRIE/Union backstory, and the medical-care-before-hospitals system he runs for Jamie.
  • 2025 ("Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export — major backstory session): Confirmed pre-military teenage delinquency and the jail-or-enlist ultimatum, the full Kuwait First Change/massacre sequence, the confirmed reason the AWOL hunt for Doc actually ended (had him in his sights, couldn't take the shot, turned himself in), the court-martial/prison/discharge stigma details, and additional gear (silver-shot shells, the hidden toolbox tray). Also where the vault's "personal hatred of vampires" note (added earlier this session) gets its full context: the werewolf Rudy killed as a teenager was Dominated by the vampire, not acting freely.
  • 2026-07-14 (The Prodigal Son manuscript shared): Added a conflicting Shepherd's Inn origin story (bought outright vs. locals pooling money), the Inn's legal code-enforcement immunity, the Dawes flashback case, a third military deployment detail (Saudi Arabia), and the "Ash"/Phoebe arrival detail.
  • 2025-06-06 (Pulpify your Story): Jenn added to Key Relationships as his grounding force for What Wasn't and What Was.
  • 2025-06-04 (Escalating Tensions Rewrite): Noted the Winnebago is deliberately kept hitchable rather than a permanent trailer home.
  • 2025-06-04 (Escalating Tensions Rewrite, full transcript): A much fuller version of this chat surfaced later, adding: 5'7" height; the Fallujah bar-fight anecdote; the "prison, or the war" origin quote; Jenn caretaking details (peanut brittle, licorice, the 2am ditch rescue, "kid" as armor); confirmed Jenn doesn't and won't know about the supernatural; the unconfirmed "Hollow Creek" killing rumor; the "Book 4" brainstormed ending where Rudy dies or nearly dies, hands Hale the Inn, and leaves a full letter (quoted above) that substantially clarifies — but doesn't fully reconcile with — the earlier "who is she" will thread from Supernatural Horror Publishers.
  • 2025-06-06 (Pulpify your Story, poverty pass): Established what his spare money is actually for (bail, burying the family-less); the case-file-bleeding Gothic detail offered in the same broader conversation's Gothic pass.
  • 2025-05-29 (Act Beat Refinement Help): Marcus Cole relationship, drawer keepsakes, the "Good enough" motif, and cigarette-buying-as-avoidance established via Act 1 outlining for What Wasn't and What Was; landline phone manner and broken-railing example added from an earlier draft pass of the same outline.
  • 2025-05-29 (Chronicle Series Title Advice): Full phrase "Not perfect. Just good enough." confirmed as Rudy's catchphrase.
  • 2025-05-28 (WoD CoD Hybrid Setting): Jamie added to Key Relationships — Rudy's rescue of young Jamie from a Huntsman, failed foster care placements, and his tolerance of Jamie's drug use established (per one unreconciled account of their history).
  • 2025-06-03 (Gas Station Gender Moment): The "strangers overshare with him" trait established; his reaction to seeing Jamie's true Mien in the Hedge confirmed.
  • 2025-06-03 (Detective Noir Setup): Leg injury, iron weapons, and possible trailer-park upbringing established via the alternate noir outline; flagged a characterization tension (drinking, hallucinating) with his established sober personality.
  • 2025-06-03 (Save the Cat Mapping): Sworn-off-magic vow, the forgets-Caleb's-face escalation, and the "To Mom" drawing detail added via a fifth/sixth alternate outline.
  • 2026-07-15 (decided live, grill-canon session): Jamie/Huntsman confrontation resolved (Rudy rams the Huntsman with the truck; a gun was ruled out as too dangerous to the trailer park); leg injury/iron knife retired as non-canon, trailer-park upbringing confirmed. Follow-up same day: full Tina relationship arc and her son Ben confirmed, resolving "Which Kid Does He Think Is His?" as Ben, not Caleb.
  • 2025-06-02/03 (Supernatural Horror Publishers): Rudy's will and the mysterious "she" successor thread established — context incomplete since the setup message wasn't captured in the source export.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live): Height reaffirmed as 5'7", final, with a real-world Scout Sniper concealment rationale added. Added Gil to Key Relationships with his full new backstory.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): "Who is she" resolved as Amber, with the hiring mechanism; Shepherd's Inn origin story conflict reconciled; "a human he killed" resolved as a young-gen vampire, decoupled from the Mickey/Passion scene.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, from "Westernizing What Wasn't"): Gun-drawing decision code confirmed — talk, leave, contain, protect another way, only then draw; never drawn as intimidation or a resting hand.
  • 2026-07-18 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed as the one who actually kills the Huntsman (not Jamie), and confirmed carrying real, unresolved internal doubt about whether his history of killing already defines him. Also confirmed Jamie's one-directional imprint bond (Rudy doesn't have the reverse sense).

docs.title: Special Trait
docs.text: THE CHARACTER HINDERS RESOLVING THIS PLOTLINE: This Character gets in the way of resolving the current Plotline in some way, likely serving as a complication to the Player Characters

docs.title: Identity
docs.text: (Roll for two) Wanderer + Entertainer

docs.title: Descriptors
docs.text: (Roll for two) Dirty + Rough

docs.title: ChatGPT to the rescue!
docs.text:
Description: Bramble, often called "Bram," is a wandering entertainer with a rugged charm. Standing at about 5'8", he has a wiry build that speaks of a life spent on the move. His scruffy, unkempt hair is a chaotic tangle of dark brown curls, often adorned with bits of twigs and leaves from his travels. His face is weathered and lined, with sunken cheeks and a perpetual five-o'clock shadow that hints at his rough lifestyle.

Bram’s clothing consists of mismatched layers—tattered, colorful fabrics that once belonged to a brighter life, now faded and dirt-stained from countless performances in the dirt and grime of back alleys and roadside gatherings. He carries a battered guitar slung over his shoulder, along with an assortment of odd trinkets and makeshift instruments. Despite his disheveled appearance, his bright blue eyes twinkle with mischief and a sense of adventure, and he possesses a natural talent for storytelling and song, captivating anyone who stops to listen.

docs.title: What Actually Happened (played scene, 2024-10-30)
docs.text:
Wandered into Shepherd's Inn mid-afternoon looking to busk for a meal; Rudy turned him down (no stage, wrong crowd) but offered a sandwich and a beer instead, and pointed him toward LeGrande Station across the street. Bram stayed anyway, trading road stories with Rudy — a hitchhiking encounter near the Nevada border with something inhuman crossing the road, and pressed Rudy for stories of his own, prompting Rudy to describe a local ghost-town legend (an Old West reenactment loop that traps travelers overnight, complete with a recurring hanging). Bram seemed genuinely fascinated rather than performing interest.

A tell that undercuts the "random drifter" framing: when Rudy noted that Bram knowing his name usually means someone's here to hire, not be hired, Bram's easy grin tightened for a moment before he recovered and brushed it off as just picking up names on the road. He left just before Amina arrived — and when Rudy passed the on-ramp to I-70 shortly after, he spotted Bram loading his guitar and pack into a newer Audi, not the kind of ride a broke drifter usually catches. Neither the "famous musician in disguise" idea floated during planning nor any other explanation has been confirmed — treat Bram's true nature as an open question. See Development Notes & Unresolved Questions.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2024-10-29 ("Ghosts of King County," character creation): Created via Adventure Crafter rolls (Wanderer + Entertainer, Dirty + Rough) as a Character Hinders-type NPC; initial description drafted.
  • 2024-10-30 (played scene): Introduced in person at Shepherd's Inn; left with an unexplained high-end ride, raising doubt about his cover story.

docs.title: Harv
docs.text:
• Type: Vampire Spirit (formerly of the Malkavian Clan)
  • Background:
    • Harv was Missy’s first progeny, embraced before Mickey. After being diablerized by Mickey, he became a disembodied spirit, joining Missy in haunting Mickey.

Appearance: Harv is a short, stout Caucasian man standing around 5'3" and weighing about 200 lbs, with a completely bald head and prominent jowls that give him a soft, round-faced look. His build and demeanor suggest a man who leads a largely sedentary life, with no hint of physical fitness or activity. Harv appears in his late 50s, with deep-set eyes behind thin wire-frame glasses that add a touch of intellect to his otherwise no-nonsense, weary expression. His attire—a worn jacket, high-collared shirt, and vest—reflects his working-class roots from the late 1800s, with a rugged, timeworn style fitting his tough, unrefined character. Visual reference cited: Danny DeVito or Bob Hoskins.

Personality: Harv exudes a practical, no-frills demeanor, often giving blunt, tough-love advice. Despite being a spectral presence following his diablerization by Mickey Two-Dice, he remains unaware of his disembodied state, considering himself very much an active part of Mickey’s life. Harv’s hardened, straightforward approach makes him a grounding influence in Mickey’s chaotic world, though he can be exasperated by Mickey’s memory lapses and unpredictable behavior. While he’s often critical, Harv is loyal to Mickey and views himself as a rough-around-the-edges mentor who doesn’t sugarcoat the truth.

Relationship with Mickey: Harv considers himself Mickey’s mentor and often treats him with an almost begrudging care, though he doesn’t hold back his criticism when he thinks Mickey’s being foolish. Despite his stern demeanor, Harv is loyal to Mickey, even if he’s unaware of his own spectral state.

Correction — he can't help with physical tasks, not that he won't (per Dave directly, 2026-07-14; refined 2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): In The Prodigal Son, Harv hangs back while Mickey does all the physical work alone — dragging an unconscious man's dead weight from a car to a nursing home room — and needles him afterward about sloppy soldering on the warehouse plumbing instead of pitching in. Harv and Missy have full free will and full autonomy — they aren't puppets or passive recordings — but they can't interact with the living world in any meaningful way, which is why they often come across as passive or lazy. Harv genuinely can't lift, carry, solder, or fix anything.

But Harv is also, separately, just lazy (2026-07-16): Nothing stops him and Missy from moving around independently, observing, eavesdropping, or following someone — that's well within what their autonomy allows, even without physical interaction. Harv has tailed someone before, when Mickey directly told him to. He'll never volunteer to do it unprompted, though — it's a level of effort he doesn't want to put in. This reframes the previously-flagged Prologue banter ("Sure, if you let me... I offered, but you insisted on doing it all yourself") — Harv likely means he offered to go watch/follow/scout, not that he offered to physically help drag a body, which he genuinely can't do.

Perceptibility, resolved (2026-07-16): Harv and Missy can perceive each other, not just Mickey — the "only Mickey can see them" framing was incomplete. Kayla was in this same disembodied, mutually-perceptible state too, for the period after Mickey diablerized her and before she was fleshcrafted a new body. As of the events of The Prodigal Son, no entity outside this small circle (Mickey, Harv, Missy, and Kayla during her disembodied window) has ever perceived Harv or Missy.

Harv isn't leashed to Mickey's physical proximity either — he can wander off freely. The only real tether is existential: if Mickey dies, Harv may cease to exist permanently along with him.

Current Influence:

  • Harv offers Mickey advice based on his past experiences, sometimes functioning as a voice of reason, albeit one that may not always align with Mickey’s understanding of reality.

Confirmed human background (per "Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): A client at Missy's brothel in Castle Dell, Georgia who fell in love with her; unable to have her, he became her bartender/security/protector instead, and she eventually embraced him. Mickey diablerized him the same night Missy embraced Mickey. Dave's own comparison for the Harv/Mickey dynamic: "Silent Bob" to Mickey's "Jay." His stance toward Mickey is explicitly pity, not condemnation — his words can still land with Mickey precisely because he never tries to control him.

Era, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Harv's own human years at the brothel are turn-of-the-century — he's embraced sometime after Missy's already running the place, which is what pins down Michael Turino's own corrected, later arrival at Castle Dell (see Mickey Two-Dice).

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Background, appearance, personality, and relationship with Mickey established.
  • 2026-07-14 (relayed directly by Dave): Corrected the "lazy/passive-aggressive" framing — Harv and Missy are disembodied and can't physically interact with the world at all, not choosing to withhold help.
  • 2025 ("Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Confirmed human background (Missy's brothel, the "Silent Bob" comparison) and the diablerie timing.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Refined the perceptibility/autonomy rule — Harv and Missy have full free will and can perceive each other (and could, per Kayla, perceive her too during her own disembodied window), just no meaningful physical interaction with the living world. Also confirmed Harv is genuinely lazy on top of that — capable of observation/following tasks, but he'll never volunteer for one.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed his human years as turn-of-the-century, pinning down Michael Turino's corrected arrival timeline. See Mickey Two-Dice.

docs.title: Kayla Fawston
docs.text:
Type: Vampire (Tzimisce Clan)

Background: Kayla is Mickey Two-Dice's “girlfriend” and a fellow vampire. She followed him to King County but has kept an even lower profile than Mickey, avoiding notice.

Current Activity: Kayla has not made an impression locally, preferring to remain in the background, likely as an enforcer or ally for Mickey if he encounters trouble.

Corrected/expanded background (per "Vampire Population Ratio," full data export — long-established in Dave's own head, just newly surfaced into this vault): Brazilian. Embraced by a Tzimisce sire named Alissa, who had already fleshcrafted Kayla (and two other, unnamed childer) into mirror images of herself.

Not actually Mickey's girlfriend — diablerized, not chosen: Mickey diablerized Kayla; her soul got "stuck" inside him the same way Missy's and Harv's did, per the soul-anchoring rule (a diablerie victim persists only if they had a coherent, strong identity to anchor to — see Mickey Two-Dice). Unlike with Harv and Missy, Mickey actually remembers diablerizing Kayla. Confirmed (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): for that window before her fleshcrafted body was ready, Kayla was in the exact same disembodied, mutually-perceptible state Harv and Missy live in permanently — full free will, no meaningful physical interaction with the living world, perceptible to Mickey and to Harv/Missy but no one else. With Alissa's help, he fleshcrafted a new body matching Kayla's original appearance, and her soul was moved into it — ending her time in that state.

Mickey's actual view of her: Not a childe, not prey, not an ally — "property he didn't choose but now possesses." Reluctant obligation and annoyance, not love or even like ("Mickey doesn't love... He doesn't even LIKE. But he tolerates. He feels some sort of parental obligation toward Kayla, even though she's not his childe" — Dave). The earlier "girlfriend" framing above should be read as the cover story she and Mickey project, not the real dynamic.

Trauma: The diablerie and forced reconstruction left her with PTSD and the Narcissist derangement.

The Dolls: Kayla compulsively fleshcrafts/bonecrafts victims into near-perfect copies of herself, refining the technique over time — no two sculptures ever come out exactly the same, which drives her ongoing derangement. Mickey disposes of most of these victims before they're embraced; he's embraced and diablerized a few himself. The Dolls are, in effect, things Mickey does to Kayla's stand-ins because he can't (yet) do them to her directly. See Spanish Dolls.

Confirmed drafted scene and dialogue (per "Bridging Story Gaps," full data export): While working on a new Doll — who looks like inert clay until she starts squirming and whimpering, prompting Mickey to numb her mind again — Kayla needles Mickey about his own fleshcraft limits: he's good at it but will never be a master, because he only ever works on "what will be seen" and never "paints all the way to the edge of the canvas." She asks to play with his face; when he declines, she needles him with "Angel lets me" (see Angel de la Vega) — to which Mickey retorts, "Yeah, but then I have to clean up after you when he leaves." Neither pushes it further.

The Angel arc and her death: After a Blood Hunt was called on Mickey (for an unrelated diablerie), Kayla helped fleshcraft him into a new identity, Angel de la Vega, to help him escape it. She wants Mickey forgotten forever, and ultimately conspires with Angel to destroy Mickey — accepting that this destroys Angel too. Mickey remembers the betrayal and kills her for it. Whether he diablerizes her when he does is undecided ("I don't know if he'll diablerize her, yet" — Dave).

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Introduced as Mickey's "girlfriend," a fellow vampire keeping a low profile in King County.
  • 2025 ("Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Full corrected backstory surfaced — the "girlfriend" framing revealed as cover for a diablerie-and-possession dynamic, plus her origin, the Dolls, her role in the Angel arc, and her eventual death at Mickey's hands.
  • 2025 ("Bridging Story Gaps," full data export): Confirmed drafted dialogue with Mickey over his fleshcraft limits and Angel's face-privileges, plus the "clay girl" Doll-in-progress scene beat.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed she shared Harv and Missy's disembodied, mutually-perceptible state during the window between her diablerization and her fleshcrafted new body.

docs.title: Missy Chamberlain
docs.text:
• Type: Vampire Spirit (formerly of the Malkavian Clan)
  • Background:
    • Missy, a former brothel owner in Castle Dell, GA, embraced both Harv and Mickey. After her diablerie, her spirit became tied to Mickey, now residing in his mind alongside Harv.

Appearance: Missy has a striking, slightly wild look, reminiscent of someone who’s both dangerous and alluring. She’s styled in a way that exudes glamour with a rough edge, often dressed in bold, old-fashioned attire that hints at her former life as a brothel owner in Castle Dell, Georgia. Her expression is sharp, with eyes that hold a hint of cunning and mischief, always assessing and ready to take advantage of any situation. Though spectral now, Missy maintains a vivid, almost tactile presence. Visual reference cited: Kaitlin Olson or Jaime Pressley. Confirmed in The Prodigal Son: bleach-blonde hair, voice like honey, wearing a clinging dark blue evening gown.

Personality note (per The Prodigal Son): Delights in directing Mickey's life and needling him — sarcastic, backseat-driving, quick to point out his blind spots (including his memory lapses, which she uses to needle him about having "been a doctor").

Personality: Missy is cunning, with a dark sense of humor and an edge that reflects her Malkavian lineage. As the one who Embraced both Harv and Mickey, she holds a maternal, needling influence over them.

Not actually leashed to him (per Dave directly, 2026-07-14): Missy and Harv can freely wander off — they aren't required to stay within any particular distance of Mickey. The real tether is existential, not physical: if Mickey dies, they may cease to exist permanently along with him. In practice they tend to stick close since he's the only one who can perceive or talk to them, but that's a choice born of loneliness/utility, not a hard constraint.

Correction — not a manipulative secret, but a truth she gave up telling (per Dave directly, 2026-07-14): Missy and Harv are disembodied spirits, and neither can physically interact with the real world in any meaningful way. Missy fully remembers her own diablerization by Mickey and knows exactly what she and Harv actually are — but this isn't leverage she's strategically withholding for personal advantage. She and Harv both genuinely tried to convince Mickey of the truth and gave up. Whether that's because his memory lapses erase the conversation every time, or he simply refuses to believe it, isn't stated — but the dynamic reads as resigned and a little sad rather than calculating.

Perceptibility, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Missy and Harv can perceive each other, not just Mickey — the "only Mickey can perceive them" framing above was incomplete. Both have full free will and autonomy; they just can't meaningfully affect the physical world (no lifting, carrying, fixing). Kayla shared this same disembodied, mutually-perceptible state for the window between her diablerization and her fleshcrafted new body. As of The Prodigal Son's events, no one outside that small circle has ever perceived either of them.

Relationship with Mickey: Knows more than she lets on and isn't shy about needling him over his memory lapses (see The Prodigal Son), but her silence about what she and Harv really are is born of futility, not manipulation.

Current Influence:

  • Missy has the ability to provide counsel to Mickey, using her past experiences to subtly guide him, though she also has her own agenda in mind.

Confirmed human background (per "Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): A former prostitute before becoming a brothel owner in Castle Dell, Georgia. Embraced Michael Turino — Mickey's human self, an internationally renowned neurologist/psychiatrist she sought out specifically for his knowledge of the mind, not realizing "there was evil inside of him." Was impartial/distant toward Mickey until he diablerized her — having since watched his cruelty from inside his own head, she now pities him and regrets ever creating him. Dave's own comparison: "she looks at him the same way Jeffrey Dahmer's mom must have looked at him." That pity has real teeth, though — Dave's own further line: "If Missy still had a body, she'd kill Mickey."

Era, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Missy's own human years as a prostitute-turned-brothel-owner are Victorian-era/Wild-West-era — this is what pins down Michael Turino's own corrected arrival at Castle Dell to around/shortly after WWI at the latest (see Mickey Two-Dice), since she has to have already run the brothel long enough to embrace Harv before Michael ever shows up.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Background, appearance, and personality established.
  • 2026-07-14 (relayed directly by Dave): Corrected the "manipulative secret" framing — she and Harv genuinely tried and gave up telling Mickey the truth, rather than withholding it strategically.
  • 2025 ("Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Confirmed human background (Castle Dell brothel, embracing Michael Turino) and her private pity/regret toward Mickey.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Refined the perceptibility rule — she and Harv can perceive each other, and could perceive Kayla during her own disembodied window, though no one outside that circle ever has.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed her human years as Victorian/Wild-West era, pinning down Michael Turino's corrected arrival timeline. See Mickey Two-Dice.

docs.title: Horace
docs.text:
A neighborhood kid who befriended Rudy over the summer before either of them started school together — round-faced, easygoing, never treated Rudy like the "dangerous new kid" everyone else assumed he was. Rudy didn't hear the other kids call him "Fat Kid" until weeks after they'd already become friends, and it pissed him off when he did.

Invited Rudy into the Monster Club right as the school year started — nervous about it, half-expecting to be laughed at.

Confirmed scenes: - Got in trouble with Sean in the principal's office for drawing monster pictures in science class — Rudy, despite never actually having been in trouble himself before moving, talked Horace down with an invented line about permanent records: "It doesn't matter. Your permanent record is a myth. I've been to the principal's office hundreds of times, and none of that stuff followed me here." - Present with Rudy in the Vampire's mansion when Wolfman was there — kicked Wolfman in the nards. - His level-up moment, fighting off one of the Vampire's brides: cocking a shotgun and declaring, "My name," he says, cocking the shotgun in his hands, "is Horace!"

Fate, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live — deliberate homage, not subverted): Not hunted, not killed by anything connected to the case. Horace wanted no part of what came after — he wanted to be safe, and ignorance was bliss, so he pulled away from Rudy as Rudy got more obsessive about the whole thing, well before Rudy ever joined the Marines. He moved away, lost touch with everyone from that life, and years later died of pneumonia as an adult — an ordinary, unconnected death. Rudy only learned about it much later, secondhand, the same distant way anyone who'd known of him might have — no one left who'd kept in touch to pass the news along directly. Deliberately mirrors the real fate of Brent Chalem, the actor who played Horace in The Monster Squad (1987), who died of pneumonia as an adult — Dave's own choice to honor that rather than write around it.

Estrangement from Rudy, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): Not a single dramatic falling-out — a gradual drift, spread out over time. Horace wanted normalcy; Rudy was becoming the "obsessed hunter" type even as a teenager, and that obsession is what actually pushed people away, not some external threat picking them off one by one.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025 ("California Incident Brainstorming," full data export): Backstory, the principal's-office scene, and the "My name is Horace!" moment established.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Fate confirmed — mundane, adult death from pneumonia, deliberately honoring the real actor's fate rather than subverting it with a supernatural cause. Estrangement from Rudy confirmed as a gradual drift driven by Rudy's own growing obsession, not an external threat.

docs.title: Phoebe
docs.text:
Alias/Nickname: Ashley (used in early brainstorming for What Wasn't and What Was — same character, treated as a naming inconsistency across sessions rather than a distinct person)

Naming preference, confirmed (2026-07-16, decided live) — vault bookkeeping only, not a finished-manuscript decision: Now that Ashley exists as her own, unrelated named character in the Clara McKenzie material, use "Phoebe" throughout the vault's own prose and cross-references going forward, even when a source chat used "Ashley." The alias itself stays documented above as real naming history; only the loose vault-wide shorthand changes. Direct quotes that use "Ashley" (Dave's own words, drafted dialogue) are left verbatim rather than rewritten.

What she's actually called in the published book: Sticking with "Phoebe" for now (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session) — not fully re-litigated, but no longer treated as urgently open either. The underlying tension is still on record: "Phoebe" risks being on-the-nose about the Monster Squad modeling (see The Monster Squad (Movie)), which is the same reason "Ashley" was floated as a rename in the first place — but "Ashley" collides with an unrelated character elsewhere in this vault. If a better final name surfaces later, it's still fair game to revisit.

Role in Setting: Catalyst of the original supernatural events, Rudy’s primary motivation for staying in King County

Based in: Asylum in King County, Colorado

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
• Early Life and Connection to the Monster Squad: Phoebe was a young child during the events in California that inspired The Monster Squad (Movie). She was the youngest member of the group and had a naturally high affinity for the supernatural, showing early signs of Awakening that made her critical in closing the portal.

  • Role in the Portal Ritual, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Phoebe sealed the portal herself via a spontaneous Awakening — Scary German Guy, an older mage, was genuinely present and involved, but not the source of the power. The earlier "used as his battery" framing is retired. Her raw magical potential at such a young age was the deciding factor, though the experience left deep, lasting trauma.

  • Confirmed family background (per "Astrology Birthday Guess," full data export): Awakened as a Mage in kindergarten — "way too damn early." Her parents divorced shortly after her Awakening. Had a brother who died when she was a teenager, under circumstances she still doesn't know. Confirmed Mage archetype: "more D&D Wild Mage than Technocracy" (Dave's own phrasing) — an uncontrolled/untrained caster, not an institutional or disciplined one.

  • Confirmed powers and asylum situation (per "Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export — Dave's own comp: "Wanda Maximov from Marvel meets Walter Bishop from Fringe"): Kept drugged in the asylum for two reasons — lucidity lets her warp reality, and she also just seems "crazy" to staff regardless. Critically, the drugs don't actually stop her mind: she's an unwitting master of Astral projection. Confirmed future plot beat: in a later book, she creates a pocket dimension for "Team Rudy" to hide from Mickey in. Confirmed real (2026-07-22, decided live): unknowing dream-contact with The Promethean, who's trapped in the Abyss alongside The Vampire — see the Lingering Psychic Connection note below for the mechanism.

  • An insider at the asylum, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): Someone on staff knows exactly what she is — a wild mage — and is the actual reason she's kept drugged. The institution itself isn't in on it; it just follows the care path already sitting in her chart, written by that person, without anyone else understanding why it's shaped the way it is. Still unnamed — see Development Notes & Unresolved Questions.

  • Physical limits vs. astral freedom, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): The drugs and her own catatonia genuinely stop her body — she's never overcome that. But they don't touch her mind. She's been astral projecting and wandering the Umbra's various layers freely, unnoticed by anyone at the institution, for who knows how long.

  • Astral projection's origin, confirmed (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): She learned it from being "locked in her mind" for so many years in her catatonic state — not an innate gift, a survival skill born of the trauma itself. It's also her established channel of communication with Angel; on the astral plane, Mickey and Angel are visually/perceptually distinct from each other, unlike in the flesh.

  • Magic-as-music metaphor, confirmed (same source, Dave's own idea): Phoebe explains her and Mickey's magic as two kinds of musician — some learn to play Bach note-for-note but can't adapt when something changes; some jazz musicians barely know what note they're playing but can play anything by ear. Mickey is the former, rigid and classically trained; Phoebe is the latter, improvisational. This is the thematic reason she can beat him where raw power alone wouldn't be enough.

  • Dream-contact with Rudy, confirmed concept, scene details deliberately open (2026-07-22, decided live): At some point, Phoebe reaches out to Rudy this way — almost certainly through a dream, using the same kind of logic as the Wheel of Time's World of Dreams (Tel'aran'rhiod): the dream space is both real and not, and things that happen there carry real weight. When and where this happens isn't pinned down yet.

  • Potential scene with Mickey on the Astral plane, floated (2026-07-22): Mickey is confirmed to eventually gain enough Auspex to Astral project and is already established as using it to harass/manipulate her one-directionally. A scene where the two of them actually meet and interact there is a real candidate, not locked. See Mickey Two-Dice.

  • Confirmed narrative function in What Wasn't and What Was (per the same chat): Never appears on-page — she's "a photograph. She's the kid Rudy couldn't protect... she's why he doesn't take kid cases and why he always does. She's a spectre and a memory, not someone who will see any screen time." Rudy visits her at the King County asylum, but less often than he feels he should — he'd "rather take a bullet than look Ashley in the eye knowing he can't help her." His move to King County was specifically to be near her, not incidental.

  • Post-Event Trauma: The intense supernatural exposure during the California events left Phoebe mentally scarred, unable to fully process what happened. This trauma has manifested as a largely catatonic state, punctuated by periods of awareness and occasional supernatural sensitivity.

  • Connection to The Promethean: During the Portal Incident, Phoebe formed an unexpected bond with The Vampire’s Promethean servant. Drawn to her innocence and latent magical potential, the Promethean saw in Phoebe a path toward understanding humanity. This bond may have anchored him to his Pilgrimage, inspiring his eventual turn against the Vampire.

  • Promethean’s Sacrifice: In a pivotal moment, The Promethean chose to sacrifice himself to save Phoebe and the Monster Squad, allowing himself to be drawn into the portal. This selfless act left a profound impact on Phoebe, who may retain a subconscious memory of his presence.

docs.title: Current Condition
docs.text:
• State of Mind: Phoebe is mostly catatonic, with her consciousness appearing to withdraw in self-defense. This withdrawal may be psychological, a reaction to trauma, or a result of her early Awakening. She experiences brief periods of lucidity, though these are rare.

  • Connection to King County's Supernatural Energy, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): Not coincidence, and not really conscious destiny either — resonance. King County's fracture is the same ancient wound the Vampire briefly tore open at a different, more recent point in California (see King County, Colorado), and Phoebe's own Awakening pulled her into direct contact with that same wound when she sealed the breach. Something in her still carries that contact. A place already thin like King County is exactly the kind of place that can hold someone touched the way she was — not because anyone planned it, but because like calls to like. Her periods of lucidity correlating with local supernatural fluctuations are the clearest evidence of this; nobody around her, including whoever placed her at that specific asylum, actually understands why it "works" for her when nothing else has.

  • Rudy’s Visits: Rudy Williams frequently visits her in the asylum, even though she rarely responds. His dedication seems to provide her with some measure of comfort, as her occasional reactions are most pronounced when he’s nearby.

  • Lingering Psychic Connection, confirmed real (2026-07-22, decided live): Kinda, per Dave's own cosmology logic — the Astral plane and the Abyss are both layers of the Umbra (see King County Cosmology Framework: Astral = High Umbra, Abyss = Low Umbra). Phoebe's an unwitting master of Astral projection; the Promethean is trapped in the Abyss. Different layers of the same structure, plus a real trauma bond formed during the ritual, is enough to keep a real link open between them — not just waiting for his return, an active, ongoing thing.

  • Residual Effects of Disquiet: The Promethean’s Disquiet may have influenced Phoebe’s perception of the world, amplifying her sensitivity to supernatural energies. This could explain her occasional lucid moments, especially if the environment around her temporarily shifts or aligns with the Promethean’s Wasteland-taint.

docs.title: Notable Traits / Abilities
docs.text:
• Potential for Awakening: Despite her catatonic state, Phoebe may still possess latent magical potential. Her early signs of Awakening hint that she could be capable of extraordinary feats if her consciousness were fully accessible.

  • Sensitivity to Supernatural Activity, confirmed: Phoebe senses changes in King County, Colorado's supernatural environment because it's the same wound she touched directly during the portal ritual (see above) — not a vague empathic gift, a specific resonance with that specific fracture. This could be an asset if tapped into, though her catatonic state makes it difficult to do so.

docs.title: Speculative "what if" — Monster Squad Sequel (What-If Idea)
docs.text:
A hypothetical, explicitly non-canon sequel idea imagines an adult Phoebe who recovered enough to have a daughter of her own, called back into action when The Vampire returns. This directly conflicts with her established catatonic, institutionalized state above — treat it purely as a "what if," not a fact about her current condition.

Meta/craft note — modeled on a real film: Phoebe is explicitly and openly modeled on the 1987 movie The Monster Squad, with "Ashley" (her established alias) chosen specifically after the actress who played the film's Phoebe. See The Monster Squad (Movie) for the in-universe propaganda-film convention and the confirmed callback-line device, including a candidate line for her: "He's not a monster."

docs.title: In What Wasn't and What Was (referenced as Ashley)
docs.text:
• Rudy keeps a photo of her and a crayon drawing she made at age six — the drawing shows a monster being banished by a girl with light in her hands — locked in a drawer at Shepherd's Inn, alongside a Monster Squad photo.
  • Flashback fragment: her voice, "Don't let go, Rudy!", swallowed by a void. (An earlier draft pass of the same scene used a different line instead: "You believe me, right?" — an alternate/discarded phrasing, not necessarily replaced for good.)
  • Rudy recognizes the pressure of broken reality (e.g. the name-flicker at Caleb's vigil) as the same feeling from "the tunnels under town, before the portal opened — before Ashley screamed and the world tore open."

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Core background — Monster Squad member, Awakening potential, portal-sealing role (originally framed as a ritual "battery"), catatonic aftermath, bond with the Promethean — established.
  • 2025-05-29 (Act Beat Refinement Help): Referenced under the name "Ashley" in Act 1 outlining for What Wasn't and What Was; drawer keepsakes and the "Don't let go, Rudy!" line established (an earlier draft pass used "You believe me, right?" instead).
  • 2025-06-03 (Monster Squad Sequel Idea): Speculative "what if" adult/recovered version noted — explicitly non-canon, conflicting with her established current condition.
  • 2025 ("Character Name Considerations," full data export): Confirmed the direct Monster Squad (1987 film) modeling and the "Ashley" naming origin; see The Monster Squad (Movie).
  • 2025 ("Astrology Birthday Guess," full data export): Confirmed family background (parents' divorce, brother's death), Wild Mage archetype, and her purely off-page/photograph narrative function in What Wasn't and What Was.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Portal-sealing role resolved as her own spontaneous Awakening, not a "battery" for Scary German Guy's ritual.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session, later same day): Sticking with "Phoebe" as her working name for now, without fully closing the question for the finished manuscript.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live): Dave confirmed "Phoebe" as the vault's preferred name in loose prose/cross-references going forward, to avoid confusion with the unrelated Ashley character in the Clara McKenzie material. Updated the loose "Ashley" references across ~12 other pages that weren't quoting source material or documenting the alias itself.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed her connection to King County's supernatural energy — not coincidence, resonance. King County's fracture is the same ancient wound the Vampire briefly tore open in California; her Awakening put her in direct contact with that wound, and something in her still carries it, which is why she's sensitive to fluctuations there specifically. See King County, Colorado and The Vampire.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session, later same day): Confirmed the psychic link with The Promethean is real — Astral (High Umbra) and Abyss (Low Umbra) are adjacent layers of the same Umbra structure, and the trauma bond from the ritual bridges the rest. See King County Cosmology Framework.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session, later same day): Confirmed an unnamed asylum insider knows she's a wild mage and is the real reason she's kept drugged, that the drugs only ever stopped her body and never her mind, and that she's been astral-wandering the Umbra undetected. Also confirmed she reaches out to Rudy through a dream at some point (Wheel of Time World-of-Dreams logic) — concept locked, exact scene left deliberately open.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation," later still): Confirmed astral projection's origin (learned from being locked in her own mind for years) and the Bach-vs-jazz magic metaphor explaining why she can beat Mickey. See Angel de la Vega and Mickey Two-Dice.

docs.title: Scary German Guy
docs.text:
Real Name: Unknown (referred to by the Monster Squad as “Scary German Guy”)

Role in Setting: Mage and mentor figure; helped the Monster Squad close the portal in California

Status: Current whereabouts unknown

Based in: Originally in California; potential to reappear in King County or a nearby location

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
• Introduction to the Monster Squad: The “Scary German Guy” was an older Mage who lived in the California town where the portal incident occurred. Known to the kids by his mysterious nickname, he was initially a reclusive figure, but his knowledge of the supernatural made him essential in stopping the vampire’s portal ritual.

  • Role in Closing the Portal: Scary German Guy recognized Phoebe’s potential for Awakening, despite her young age, and used her raw magical energy to power the ritual that closed the portal. This process left her traumatized, but it was the only way to prevent the vampire’s incursion.

  • Aftermath and Departure: Once the portal was sealed, Scary German Guy retreated from public view, leaving Rudy Williams and the others to deal with the aftermath alone. His disappearance is another mystery, as no one knows where he went or if he’s even alive.

docs.title: Abilities and Traits
docs.text:
• Mage of Significant Power: Scary German Guy possesses advanced knowledge of ritual magic, wards, and protective spells, especially regarding portals and inter-dimensional threats. His abilities might align with the Mysterium order or a similar faction focused on preserving dangerous knowledge.

  • How the kids cross paths with him, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): He was already independently investigating the Vampire's house (there's no German-language diary in this telling to force the connection) when the Monster Squad arrived there on their own. Dave's best reconstruction, not stated with full certainty: the kids didn't so much need his help as insist they were now part of it — from there, either he begrudgingly worked with them, or the two groups (his investigation and theirs) ended up working separately and getting in each other's way. See The California Incident.

  • Secretive Nature: Despite aiding The Monster Squad (group), Scary German Guy kept much of his personal life and knowledge hidden. He likely has a network of contacts in the magical community, but he keeps his distance, operating in secrecy.

  • Potential Ally or Mentor: Though his whereabouts are unknown, his experience could make him a valuable ally if Rudy Williams were to find him. He might hold additional knowledge about the portal or the supernatural forces involved, as well as insights into Phoebe’s condition.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Introduced as the mage who helped the Monster Squad close the portal.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Resolved how he and the kids actually crossed paths — he was already investigating the Vampire's house independently.

docs.title: Sean
docs.text:
Phoebe's older brother and the actual leader of the Monster Club — Rudy is emphatically not the one who ran things; that was always Sean.

Personality: Bold, always chasing the next big scheme (sneaking into the supposedly-haunted Silverstein house, watching bootleg horror movies in his basement), the type who drags everyone else along for the ride without a second thought. Protective of Phoebe in practice even while constantly telling her to go home and stop following them.

Cop's kid: His father is a police officer, which is how he's first to hear about the break-in shooting at the station and the missing body/dead ambulance driver — the group's first real sign that something in town is wrong (see The California Incident).

Fate, deliberately left open (2026-07-22, decided live): Not resolved on purpose — unlike Horace, whose fate is now confirmed mundane, Dave wants Sean's ending to stay undecided for now.

Falling out with Rudy, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): Sean stayed invested in what happened longer than Horace did, and may have gotten more involved in his own way over time — but he and Rudy couldn't keep working together, purely down to personality conflict, not any external force driving them apart. Part of the same gradual, spread-out drift that cost Rudy the rest of the group before he ever joined the Marines.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025 ("California Incident Brainstorming," full data export): Introduced as Phoebe's brother and the club's true leader; given the police-station foreshadowing beat.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Fate explicitly left open, unlike Horace's now-confirmed mundane death. Falling out with Rudy confirmed as personality conflict, not a hunted-down death — part of a gradual drift, not a single break.

docs.title: The Promethean
docs.text:
True Name: Unknown (referred to as “The Promethean” or “Frankenstein” by the Monster Squad)

Role in Setting: Servant of The Vampire, but ultimately allied with the Monster Squad; complex supernatural entity with potential for redemption

Status: Presumed lost; last seen entering the portal during The California Incident

Location of Origin: Created by the Vampire as an unnatural, soulless entity animated by The Divine Fire

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
• Creation and Nature: The Promethean was a flesh golem animated by the Divine Fire, giving him a semblance of life but lacking a human soul. As a creation of The Vampire, he was initially bound to destroy the Monster Squad, driven by the Disquiet his unnatural presence caused in others.

  • Creation method (brainstormed): The Vampire is theorized to have animated him with the same lightning strike depicted in the film, of the Frankenstein lineage of Prometheans specifically.

  • Confirmed series endpoint (per "Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Both he and The Vampire eventually return from the Abyss together, roughly 30 years after being pulled in. The Promethean joins Rudy's circle upon returning — exact circumstances of the return still undecided by Dave, but the fact of it, and his subsequent alliance with Rudy, is confirmed as a fixed future story beat, not just brainstormed. Deliberately left open (2026-07-22, grill-canon session): The actual return mechanism — not being pushed for an answer now.

  • Role in the series finale, confirmed (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation" — see Book Order and Series Structure): Joins Rudy, Amber, Phoebe, Moss, and Doc in the final battle against Mickey. He and the Abyss-touched vampire Mickey later diablerizes were in the Abyss together for the same ~30 years — a direct link between the two. He's unmade/torn into his component parts early in the fight; whether that permanently kills him isn't the point on the page, just that it removes him from the battle.

  • Psychic link with Phoebe, confirmed real (2026-07-22, decided live): Kinda, per Dave's own cosmology logic — the Astral plane and the Abyss are both layers of the Umbra (Astral = High Umbra, Abyss = Low Umbra — see King County Cosmology Framework). Phoebe's an unwitting master of Astral projection; he's trapped in the Abyss. Adjacent layers of the same structure, plus the real trauma bond from the ritual, keeps an active link open between them — not just dormant, waiting for his return.

  • Corrected mechanism — he pushed the Vampire in, not the other way around (per "Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export): Dave's own direct correction of an earlier framing: "the Abyssal tear didn't pull the vampire in — the vampire was pushed by a Frankenstein-type Promethean." Confirmed as sacrificing himself specifically to stop The Vampire, not simply being drawn in alongside him as an accident of the ritual.

  • Confirmed sentimental detail — still carries "Scraps": The stuffed dog Phoebe threw at him as he flew into the Abyss during the film's depicted climax. Dave's own words: "this Promethean probably still carries the little dog stuffy, Scraps, that Phoebe threw it as it flew into Limbo (in the movie)." This bond is confirmed as the same one depicted between the Promethean and Phoebe in the film.

  • Divine Fire and Disquiet: While the Divine Fire allowed him to mimic humanity from a distance, his proximity to humans (or any mortal creatures) led to Disquiet. His presence caused a subtle but growing sense of unease in those around him, which could escalate to fear or even rage if he stayed in one place too long. This effect extended to the environment, leaving behind areas of supernatural taint or “Wastelands” if he lingered.

  • Desire for Humanity: Upon encountering the Monster Squad, the Promethean was struck by their Humanity—their compassion, loyalty, and innocence in stark contrast to The Vampire’s cruelty. This encounter sparked a realization: through these children, he might understand his own path toward mortality. This connection deepened when he met Phoebe, whose youth and latent magical energy seemed to resonate with him, drawing him closer to the concept of humanity and hope.

  • Sacrifice to Protect the Monster Squad: Seeing an opportunity for redemption, the Promethean ultimately betrayed The Vampire, aligning himself with Phoebe and the children. In the final confrontation, he sacrificed himself to save them, allowing himself to be drawn into the portal in order to prevent the Vampire from succeeding. His last act was one of bravery and compassion, hinting that he may still be pursuing his Pilgrimage.

docs.title: Abilities and Traits
