docs.text:
• Resilience and Physical Power: As a Promethean, he possessed immense strength and durability, making him a formidable enforcer and guardian. This strength allowed him to serve as The Vampire’s weapon against the children initially.

  • Divine Fire and Disquiet: The Promethean’s presence generated an aura of Disquiet that made humans uneasy, often escalating to hostility. This Disquiet extended to the land, causing decay or corruption to manifest around him, a phenomenon that could linger even after his departure.

  • Potential for Humanity: The Promethean’s encounter with the children, particularly Phoebe, kindled his desire for humanity. He saw in their compassion a path toward the Pilgrimage—a journey of self-discovery that could one day make him mortal, provided he completed its steps.

docs.title: Influence and Legacy
docs.text:
• Connection to Phoebe and the Monster Squad: The Promethean’s alliance with Phoebe and the Monster Squad left a lasting impact, especially on Rudy, who remembers him as a tragic figure caught between monstrosity and humanity. This memory of their shared humanity inspires Rudy’s current drive to protect Phoebe.

  • Potential Survival in Another Realm: Though he sacrificed himself by entering the portal, it’s plausible that the Promethean is still alive within whatever realm The Vampire attempted to access. If so, he may still be seeking the Pilgrimage’s steps, striving for humanity even in this other dimension.

  • Lingering Disquiet and Taint: The Promethean’s presence in California left behind traces of supernatural corruption, subtly marking the land as a place of unease. It’s possible that the Disquiet he generated lingers near the portal’s location, leaving a Wasteland-like effect that attracts other supernatural entities or amplifies existing Wyrm-taint.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Core background, sacrifice, and legacy established.
  • 2025-06-03 (Monster Squad Sequel Idea): A speculative "what if" idea has him returning as an ally rather than a foe — "he remembers" — via a hypothetical sequel brainstorm, not confirmed as canon.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed a real, active psychic link with Phoebe via adjacent Umbra layers (Astral/Abyss) plus their trauma bond. Return mechanism deliberately left open for now.

docs.title: The Vampire
docs.text:
Real Name: Unknown (referred to as “the Vampire”)

Role in Setting: Antagonist in The Portal Incident, dark influence with lingering effects on the supernatural landscape

Status: Presumed destroyed (though some uncertainty may remain). Per a 2025-06-03 speculative brainstorm (see Monster Squad Sequel (What-If Idea)), being pulled into the Abyss was never a full death — it was a banishment, and the Abyss itself may have "gravity," potentially sending him back with a purpose rather than simply expelling him. Speculative clan: not actually Dracula, but a Ventrue or similarly powerful vampire, possibly with Lasombra traits or ambitions, obsessed with final immortality — none of this confirmed as canon, just brainstormed.

Firmed up further (per "Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Probably Lasombra, specifically. Wasn't trying to open a way to Limbo or the Underworld — the target was the Abyss, specifically. Has been trapped there roughly 30 years as of "present day" King County. Confirmed series endpoint: he and The Promethean both eventually return; Mickey Two-Dice diablerizes him afterward, inheriting some of his age/power/Abyssal taint and becoming the single most powerful vampire Rudy will ever face — ending with Mickey killing Rudy. See Mickey Two-Dice.

Corrected mechanism — pushed, not pulled (per "Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export): Dave's own direct correction of an earlier assistant framing: "the Abyssal tear didn't pull the vampire in — the vampire was pushed by a Frankenstein-type Promethean." The vampire's entry into the Abyss was an act of sacrifice/attack by The Promethean, not an accident of the ritual itself. The werewolf he'd bound (see Wolfman) was starving and near-feral by the time the kids encountered it — a further confirmation it was acting entirely under his domination, not its own will.

Full sequence, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): The Promethean shoves the Vampire into the tear — stopping the Abyss from fully breaching through into the town — and Phoebe seals the portal shut behind them via her spontaneous Awakening (see The California Incident). Read as "behind them," plural: this is why both the Vampire and the Promethean are confirmed to eventually return later in the series (see the confirmed series endpoint above) rather than just the Vampire alone — the Promethean went in after him, sealed in on the other side of the same closing door.

Confirmed movie framing: The film depicts him as Dracula purely as cover — Dave's words: "the movie just depicted the vampire as Dracula to better function as a propaganda film."

Location of Origin: California town (site of the Portal Incident)

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
• Goals and Motivation, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): Not power, not service to a greater entity, not ritual duty — the Vampire was simply obsessed with the Abyss itself, true Lasombra fashion. The goal was never to open a door and step through to visit it. He meant to bring the Abyss to Earth — to tear it open wide enough that it would engulf the town, and hopefully keep spreading past it. Not a personal-power play; closer to an act of annihilation he wanted to cause and be consumed by.

  • Not actually Dracula, brainstormed mechanics (per the "California Incident Brainstorming" pass): He'd made three thralls locally, all of whom were later killed publicly with wooden stakes — consistent with a Ventrue, or a vampire with the Selective Feeding flaw. He may also have been addicted to vampire blood himself, which would explain why he embraced (rather than just fed on) his thralls. He created Frankenstein with the same lightning depicted in the film.

  • Connection to the Wyrm: The Vampire’s power seemed infused with the corruptive essence of The Wyrm. This taint not only allowed him to manipulate supernatural energies but also attracted attention from a nearby werewolf pack, who attempted to stop him.

  • Influence over Others: The Vampire held sway over lesser supernatural beings, most notably a Promethean flesh golem he created and blood-bound werewolf minions forced into servitude. His presence exerted a powerful aura, commanding fear and obedience from weaker entities.

docs.title: Abilities and Traits
docs.text:
• Master of Ritual Magic: The Vampire was highly skilled in ritual magic, especially in opening inter-dimensional portals and manipulating dark energy. His knowledge of forbidden spells and Wyrm-infused rituals made him exceptionally dangerous.

  • Blood-Binding and Manipulation: He possessed the ability to blood-bind individuals, forcing them into loyalty or obedience. This trait extended to one of the werewolves , who served him unwillingly until the end. His mastery over manipulation made him not only powerful but also insidiously persuasive.

  • Durability and Cunning: The Vampire was not easily defeated. Even the combined efforts of the Werewolf Pack, the Monster Squad, and [[Scary German Guy] ]only managed to halt his ritual—not destroy him outright. This suggests he was both physically resilient and strategically evasive.

docs.title: Influence and Legacy
docs.text:
• Lingering Taint in California: The Vampire’s ritual, though interrupted, left a lasting Wyrm-taint on the town. This corruption may have residual effects, possibly drawing other supernatural entities to the area or impacting local human residents in subtle ways.

  • Mysterious Deaths of Monster Squad Members: Rudy suspects that the Vampire, or some influence he left behind, could be responsible for the deaths of the other Monster Squad members. Whether this is due to a lingering curse or the work of other followers is unclear.

  • Residual Connection to King County: The no-man’s-land aura surrounding King County might echo the Vampire’s original intentions. His work with portal magic and Wyrm Taint could theoretically extend to this region, influencing supernatural boundaries.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Core background, abilities, and legacy established.
  • 2025-06-03 (Monster Squad Sequel Idea): Speculative banishment clarification and clan identity added via a "what if" sequel brainstorm — not confirmed as canon.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): True motive confirmed — pure Abyss-obsession, true Lasombra fashion, not power/service/ritual. The goal was never to visit the Abyss but to drag it through to Earth and let it engulf the town (and hopefully further). Full sequence confirmed: the Promethean pushes him into the tear, then goes in after him, and Phoebe seals the portal behind both of them — which is why both return later in the series.

docs.title: Wolfman
docs.text:
The California Werewolf Pack member blood-bound to The Vampire during The California Incident — brainstormed mechanics for how a "shot and killed" werewolf from the film actually plays out under World of Darkness rules.

Nature: Tribeless — a Ghost Wolf — afflicted with a Flaw that forces an involuntary Change tied to the moon's phase.

The police-station incident: Rather than a random rampage, he went to the police specifically to be locked up for the night, trying not to hurt anyone. He Changed, was shot, and was presumed dead — but werewolves regenerate from anything short of aggravated damage, so he actually revived in the ambulance and escaped, killing the driver in the process. This is the "missing body, dead ambulance driver" incident Sean reports to the rest of the club as an early sign something's wrong.

Capture: The Vampire got to him after his escape — in this version, not drugged as in the film, but blood-bound outright. He never broke free of the bond.

Death: Present for the final battle largely as depicted in the film, including Rudy landing the killing blow — either Rudy kills him outright, or simply gets the last shot in with a silver bullet, which causes aggravated damage a werewolf can't shrug off.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text: • 2025 ("California Incident Brainstorming," full data export): Full arc established — Ghost Wolf nature, the police-station surrender/regeneration/escape, the blood bond (not drugging), and death by silver at Rudy's hand.

docs.title: Aaron Mabey
docs.text:
Aaron “Doc” Mabey

Type: Werewolf (Tribeless)

Background: Doc was part of Rudy’s elite military team and underwent his First Change in the field, slaughtering the rest of the unit. Rudy went AWOL to hunt him for six months before the military discharged him. Doc has recently come to King County, seeking Rudy’s help with an undisclosed matter.

Confirmed escalation (per "Astrology Birthday Guess," full data export): Rudy's command considered Doc a low-priority "rogue asset" and was ready to reassign Rudy to a new unit without chasing him down at all — the trigger for Rudy's AWOL wasn't just grief or vengeance, but the conviction that if Doc had to be put down, it should be by someone who still remembered him as a friend, not a stranger executing a kill order. Rudy took government firearms with him when he went AWOL, which is what actually escalated his discharge to dishonorable (and possibly a military prison stint) rather than a simple administrative separation.

Speculative Book 3+ material (per "Werewolves and Spirit Worlds," full data export — explicitly NOT locked in, see Book Order and Series Structure): A once-considered, later-discarded storyline had Doc trapped in a trauma-born decaying otherworld nicknamed "The Rusting Place," rescued by Rudy in a book that predates Dave's later direct confirmation that Book 3 is the Clara McKenzie campaign. A "Fetch" version of Doc living a normal life in King County, and a "Rudy is immune to memory-erasure" trait, were both explicitly rejected by Dave and should be treated as abandoned, not canon.

The revised version, after the Fetch was dropped (2026-07-16, fresh-eyes pass on the full export — this is the last version discussed before Dave tabled the whole book, so it supersedes the Fetch-era room list above if this material ever gets picked back up): Without the Fetch, the hook becomes a burnt, water-damaged notebook showing up for Rudy with fragmented entries and a line he recognizes: "It followed me out of the fire." Doc is trapped in The Rusting Place, not physically but as a trauma-solidified, purgatory-stained space Rudy has to enter to get him out — described as a maze/bottle-episode/broken-mind-palace with five rooms: The Armory (gear laid out for Rudy by something/someone unknown), The Burn Pit (Rudy relives the First Change on a loop that changes each time), The Wall of Names (names scratched into metal/stone/bone, one almost his), The Observation Room (docu-style footage of Doc's memories, some true, some lies), and The Quiet Room (Doc sitting still, present but not "there"). Three unresolved options for what the actual threat is: (1) a spirit/thoughtform that attached during the First Change and feeds on Doc's isolation and guilt, wearing familiar faces; (2) Doc himself, literally transformed/possessed and aware of it, begging Rudy not to kill him; (3) a "Department Wound" — a controlled narrative collapse left by a failed Department of Truth operation that Doc stumbled into. None of these were chosen before Dave tabled the book.

Naming note: This chat calls Rudy's old black-ops unit "Project Valkyrie" and floats it as a memory-conditioning/containment program — bundled with, and rejected alongside, the Fetch/memory-immunity material above. Don't treat "Project Valkyrie" itself as adopted; the separately-confirmed Task Force VALKYRIE (see Rudy Williams, per "Astrology Birthday Guess") is likely the same underlying idea explored in a different chat, but only that version — tied to [[The Union]]-style hunting, not memory control — is actually confirmed.

Confirmed werewolf-specific mechanics and backstory (per "Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export — a major, separate worldbuilding session): - Ahroun (Full Moon/warrior auspice), despite being the unit's medic — Dave's own reaction: "that tracks with the Full Moon." Homid — born fully human, unaware of his nature until his First Change. - See Lunacy for a folklore-grounded, belief-based reframing of how mortals fail to hold onto having seen him shift — floated, not yet adopted. - Was genuinely skilled at fieldcraft, camping, and hunting before enlisting — real wilderness competence, not just soldier training. - Explicitly lacks the social skills to survive in a city post-Change (doesn't know how to get a fake ID, for instance), so he's avoided civilization entirely ever since, sticking to wilderness and remote biomes. - Confirmed post-Kuwait travel route: East out of Kuwait, across into Alaska via Russia (possibly by fishing boat), down through Alaska toward his home region (Idaho/Montana), then south into Colorado to find Rudy. Crossed paths with other hunters along the way who never clocked what he actually was. - Tribe floated as Children of Gaia by the assistant — never explicitly confirmed or rejected by Dave; treat as plausible, not settled. - After his court-martial and discharge, Rudy tells himself Doc (or whatever Doc's become) is dead — a deliberate coping mechanism, not a factual belief he's ever tested. - Major confirmed future plot beat: one of the books — specifically the one that examines why King County is a werewolf no-man's-land, without packs policing the local spirit world — opens with Doc showing up at Shepherd's Inn, asking for Rudy's help. Dave's own framing ties this directly to the setting's "no coincidences" rule: it's not lost on Doc that Rudy just happens to be guarding the exact area where whatever Doc is now tracking is located.

  • Role in the series finale, confirmed (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation" — see Book Order and Series Structure): Fights alongside Rudy, Amber, Phoebe, Moss, and a Promethean in the final battle against Mickey. Gets caught up fighting off shadow minions; Rudy calls him off the fight specifically to treat Amber after Mickey overpowers her — his medic training taking priority over his werewolf strength at that moment. Rushes Mickey again later anyway and is battered aside, same as Amber.

Current Activity: Doc haunts the rural areas between Jackson Ridge, Jensen, Red Bridge, and Brindle, steering clear of populated areas and avoiding trouble. His presence has not attracted hunters, and he has maintained a low profile, likely wary of drawing Rudy’s attention.

Confirmed county-wide anomaly (per "Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): King County's Western Slope is deliberately a Werewolf: the Forsaken-style "no-man's-land" with effectively zero territorial werewolf presence, for reasons still undecided by Dave. Doc being there at all makes him "a huge anomaly" rather than one werewolf among several — see Supernatural Influence and Dynamics for the county's full confirmed population breakdown.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Core background established — First Change in the field, slaughtering Rudy's unit; Rudy's AWOL pursuit; Doc's current low-profile presence near King County.
  • 2026-07-14 (relayed directly by Dave): Corrected the Harv/Missy-style "strategic concealment" framing elsewhere in the vault doesn't apply here — see the AWOL escalation note above for the actual confirmed motive.
  • 2025 ("Astrology Birthday Guess," full data export): Confirmed the AWOL-with-government-firearms escalation and its real motive (wanting to be the one who remembered Doc as a person, not a stranger executing a kill order).
  • 2025 ("Werewolves and Spirit Worlds," full data export): Speculative, later-discarded "Rusting Place"/Fetch material floated and then explicitly rejected by Dave within the same chat.
  • 2026-07-16 (fresh-eyes pass on the full export): Added the revised, Fetch-free Rusting Place rescue structure (rooms and three threat options) that Dave was still considering when he tabled the book, plus the "Project Valkyrie" naming note cross-referencing the separately-confirmed Task Force VALKYRIE.
  • 2025 ("Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export): Major backstory session — Ahroun/Homid nature, pre-Change wilderness skill, post-Kuwait travel route, and the confirmed future book-opening beat (Doc arrives at Shepherd's Inn asking for Rudy's help).
  • 2025 ("Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Confirmed King County's Western Slope as a werewolf no-man's-land, making Doc's presence there a genuine anomaly.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): Confirmed his role in the series finale — fights, gets pulled off to treat Amber, rushes Mickey again regardless.

docs.title: Gil
docs.text:
Grey-bearded, leather-clad biker — roughly twenty years older than Rudy, with a similar depth of world-weary wisdom in his eyes. A regular at Shepherd's Inn, quiet by nature, communicates mostly in grunts and raised glasses.

A recurring "one more for the road" ritual with Rudy makes up most of the Inn's actual daily business. Recently became a grandfather. Trusted enough that Rudy leaves him watching the bar (with a shotgun under the counter, only half-joking about using it on anyone trying to steal a drink) when he needs to run an errand — Gil left a stack of bills on the counter and went home rather than actually cover for him, though, so how far that trust really extends is a little unclear.

Legal name (confirmed 2026-07-16, decided live): Robert Gilbert — "Gil" isn't short for a first name at all. Rudy only knows this because Gil paid with a check once. He went by "Gilbert" in the service, which other sailors shortened to "Gil," and it stuck for life.

Full backstory, confirmed (2026-07-16, decided live): A 22-year Navy veteran — a Navy diver — who then drove long-haul trucking for another 20-plus years until he could retire. Married during his Navy years, divorced at some point after; had a few kids, all of whom settled down in King County — which is why Gil always used King County as his home base even during his long-haul years, driving back specifically to see his kids and grandkids. He's never missed a birthday, graduation, or major holiday. A good guy.

Biker identity: Not a 1%er — riding was a weekender hobby during his working years, and only became a major part of his identity once he retired and had the time for it to be one. His road name in biker circles is "Gillman" — half a play on his Navy diver background, half a nod to "Gillman," the IP-safe name The Monster Squad used in its own credits for its Creature-from-the-Black-Lagoon analogue (consistent with this vault's established practice of borrowing that film's naming conventions — see The Monster Squad (Movie)). Rudy and the Inn regulars still just call him "Gil."

An "armchair Hunter": Knows the lore, knows the talk, has never actually done Hunter work — he's aware the supernatural world is out there without ever having engaged with it directly. Rudy doesn't know whether Gil has kept up his old military training at all, but suspects he has, given how active Gil still is in Hunter circles despite never having hunted anything himself.

What Gil doesn't know: Rudy doesn't talk about his own military service, so Gil almost certainly doesn't know about Rudy's dishonorable discharge. Dave's own read: Gil would probably understand and look past it if Rudy actually explained — but it's an open question whether Rudy would ever take that chance.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Introduced as a Shepherd's Inn regular — the "one more for the road" ritual, recent grandfatherhood, and the bar-watching/trust question established.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live): Full backstory confirmed — legal name Robert Gilbert, 22-year Navy diver, 20+ years long-haul trucking, divorce, kids/grandkids in King County, weekender-not-1%er biker identity with the road name "Gillman," "armchair Hunter" status, and the open question of whether he knows about Rudy's dishonorable discharge.

docs.title: Nathan Hale
docs.text:
Owns LeGrande Station in Shepherd Junction.

Physical: Over six feet tall, around 300 pounds, blonde hair in a boring cut. Most often seen in a red polo with "LeGrande Station" embroidered on the left breast, khakis "stiff from starch." A former state champion wrestler — his size and athletic background are played straight against Rudy's 5'7" frame, though Rudy is never the one who backs down. Confirmed independently in The Prodigal Son: "over six foot, over three hundred pounds, three chins on a good day, and a full head of dirty blond hair," red polo stretched over his gut like "a hammock for his girth," grease stains across his khakis.

A third variant of the public blowup scene (Book 2, LeGrande Station): In addition to the two drafted confrontations in "Escalating Tensions Rewrite," The Prodigal Son manuscript contains an actual written version of this same beat — Hale needling Rudy in front of Jenn ("Your very presence is losing me business"), Rudy firing back that Hale's business sense is worse than his own ("your shit-hole... sees less money in a month than I see in a single night" / "Oh, fuck you" / "I was here before you, and I'll be here long after your wife leaves you and takes the Station as her well-deserved alimony"). Hale immediately does damage control with the customers who overheard by offering free meals — a nice bit of business savvy layered onto the antagonism. Rudy calms down once back at the Inn, more bothered that Hale got to him than by what was actually said.

A recurring, toothless bit: Hale opens this scene with "I thought I said you weren't welcome here, Rudy" — implying he's tried to ban Rudy from LeGrande Station before, more than once, and it's never once stuck. Played as an ongoing, low-stakes ritual in the feud rather than a real threat.

Personality: Actually a pretty good guy — decent boss to Jenn, has a wife and a couple of kids, holds an MBA. Knows nothing about the supernatural; would probably piss himself if he found out, and then tell everyone about it — most notably, he'd tell everyone about Rudy. A gossip and a loose-lips risk, not a threat by strength or knowledge.

Sport discrepancy, flagged (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): That file has Hale getting his MBA on a football scholarship, playing defense specifically ("it justifies his size... and changes his role on the meta level" — Dave's own reasoning). This conflicts with the already-locked "former state champion wrestler" detail above, which is more deeply embedded (explains his size contrast with Rudy elsewhere). Not resolved — flagging rather than picking.

Political-arc material, floated (same source): Discussion of Hale eventually running for County Commissioner (not mayor — Shepherd Junction, based on Crescent Junction, UT, is unincorporated and has none), tentatively placed around Book 4, with a "sanitizing the battlefield" parallel Dave drew himself to the Camarilla's Guide to the Camarilla infrastructure-maintenance concept — Hale unknowingly stabilizes the town (streetlights, police funding, waste management) without knowing why it matters. Not committed to a specific book slot given the renumbering since.

Confirmed long-term series fate (per "Astrology Birthday Guess," full data export): Explicitly confirmed by Dave as one of three named characters — alongside Jamie/Moss and Jenn — who survive all the way through the confirmed series finale (Book 6), in which Mickey kills Rudy. See Book Order and Series Structure for the fuller confirmed 6-book arc this comes from.

Drafted future scene — Rudy finally levels with him, confirmed concept (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation," appears twice in the source, clearly load-bearing to Dave): Far future, well past this book — a moment where Rudy is finally exposed to Hale and enlists him specifically to keep Jenn safely in the dark rather than to fight alongside him:

"Get inside, Hale. Keep everyone inside. Give the patrons free dessert and all they can drink, and I'll pay for it. Get Jenn deep — inventory in the walk-in. Something safe." "What are you talking about, Rudy? You can't possibly face this on your own." "Of course I can. You told Jenn that you think I'm dangerous... You have no fucking clue. That thing out there, I'm going to kill it. This isn't the first thing I've killed, not by a long shot. I'm the rodeo champion who should have retired a long time ago..." "I can help you..." "No, you can protect them. Leave the wet work to me." Dave was explicit this is far off ("I'm nowhere near that point in the story") — not a Book 1-6 beat, just a confirmed direction for wherever Hale actually learns the truth.

Relationship with Jenn (surrogate-father dynamic, not romantic): Hired her at 16. Genuinely proud of her — she's put herself through nursing school (CNA track) while working his registers, and he's lined up a CNA job lead for her at a home in Jensen (possibly Western Slope Nursing Care — see the parallel note on Out of Scope Ideas). He warns her away from Rudy not out of jealousy but real (if overprotective) concern, and his private read on Rudy is more nuanced than his public complaints: "I think he's dangerous in ways he doesn't even see anymore. But no — not bad. Just broken." When Jenn once asked him outright whether Rudy used to be a cop or a criminal, he deflected with "Does it matter?" — protecting her curiosity rather than answering it. Publicly, when caught staring down Rudy in front of onlookers, he plays it off with "He's not dangerous. He's just lonely" — a performance that doesn't match his private read, and his hands are shaking as he says it. He's the one who tells Jenn the unconfirmed "Hollow Creek" rumor about Rudy (see Rudy Williams) — meant to protect her, though it reveals more about his own fear than about Rudy's guilt.

Feud with Rudy: Views Shepherd's Inn as a blight on Shepherd Junction bringing down property values, even though the Inn predates his gas station by at least 30 years. Wants to buy Shepherd's Inn and bulldoze it to expand LeGrande Station into something like a Buccee's — motivated in part by a county tourism grant for a "neon cowboy" welcome sign that Rudy's "blight" keeps jeopardizing. He wants Shepherd Junction to become a notable stop on I-70. Rudy wants the opposite: a little nowhere you grab gas and a drink before moving on. Rudy, for his part, sees LeGrande Station's chrome-and-neon 50s theme (and its milkshake machine/branded-hoodie merch) as an eyesore — but still buys his cigarettes there.

"Never escalates" vs. blowup drafts, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): The day-to-day feud really is mostly passive-aggressive — but Rudy does let his temper flare during a case sometimes, specifically because he knows it's safe: he's certain Hale would never physically retaliate to anything Rudy says, no matter how far he pushes. The three existing drafted blowup scenes (the two property-dispute versions from "Escalating Tensions Rewrite," plus The Prodigal Son's LeGrande Station version) are all this smaller, safer kind of flare-up — the Prodigal Son scene specifically needs a rewrite pass to actually read as lesser than currently drafted, since right now it plays too big. The real, one-time explosion is a future beat, not yet written: it happens later, during a case where things seem genuinely impossible, and it's bad enough that Jenn calls Rudy out on it afterward — telling him Hale is a good guy, a good boss, and a good friend, even if he doesn't know how to show it.

Roots of the feud, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): No deeper hidden history — roughly six years (see the timeline resolution on LeGrande Station) of property disputes really is mostly it, compounded by a genuine philosophical disagreement about what Shepherd Junction should even be: Hale wants a thriving I-70 tourist stop, Rudy wants a sleepy nowhere town people pass through without stopping. Deliberately mundane and boring on purpose, not a hidden secret being withheld. Added same day: a low-key layer of jealousy over Jenn — nothing specific or acted on, just that each man is her friend and it quietly bothers the other that she's close to him too.

The philosophical disagreement, quotable framing (2026-07-16, decided live — from "Westernizing What Wasn't"): The Shepherd Junction argument above boils down to one line each, and Dave confirmed it as a keeper: Rudy's "safety is something you maintain" against Hale's "safety is something you create." Rudy means it defensively — keep the town small, quiet, and unnoticed, and nothing has a reason to come looking. Hale means it constructively — grow the town, bring in visibility and commerce, and build something sturdy enough to withstand whatever comes. Neither is wrong within his own worldview, which is exactly why the feud never actually resolves into one side winning.

A specific early moment in the feud: Rudy once made Hale cry, back when Hale first bought and fixed up the station — Rudy told him his new chrome diner "looked like a jukebox threw up." Hale was proud of it at the time.

A future twist on the feud — the will, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Rudy's will apparently names Hale somehow — possibly a bitter technicality — and in one framing Rudy wants to replace it with an unnamed "she" if she ever "earns" Shepherd's Inn. That "she" is confirmed as Amber, from the "Ghosts of King County" actual-play campaign (now confirmed Book 3 in the same numbered series as this book) — the earlier Jenn theory below is superseded, rougher brainstorming, not a live alternative. The mechanism: the Clara McKenzie case proves Amber's capability to Rudy, and combined with her bar experience, he hires her on to cover Inn shifts. See Development Notes & Unresolved Questions and Amber. Separately, in a fully drafted brainstormed "Book 4" ending, Rudy actually dies or nearly dies protecting Hale and Jenn, and leaves Hale the Inn outright in a letter (full text on Rudy Williams) — with instructions to give Jenn half if he ever sells, and to keep her away from an unnamed cache of proof of the supernatural world. Hale doesn't fully understand what Rudy fought, but "he already knows what Rudy protected" — this is likely the moment (per the brainstormed triangle summary) where Hale's arc shifts from "disgust, respect, resentment" toward genuine understanding of Rudy. Whether this Book 4 letter-scenario and Amber's confirmed Book 3 succession both happen, or one supersedes the other, isn't decided — a question for a future session.

A glimpse of the supernatural (brainstormed, not confirmed adopted): In one drafted beat, Hale witnesses something unnamed and monstrous behind the diner at night, and Rudy steps between them ("Go back inside, Nathan" / "You want someone to gnaw on, you ugly sonofabitch, you go through me.") — in tension with the otherwise-stated intent that Jenn (and by extension, presumably, Hale) never learns about the supernatural. Unclear if this is meant to be a real future beat or just an illustration of how far Rudy's protectiveness extends, even to people he can't stand.

Note on a discarded earlier draft: An early rewrite attempt portrayed Hale as a hunter/Fed with a dark shared past with Rudy (a "Cairo" incident, threats about blood-bonds, having let a compromised child burn). This was explicitly rejected as wrong for this character — Hale has no connection to the supernatural at all. See Out of Scope Ideas.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025-06-04 (Escalating Tensions Rewrite): Established with full physical description, personality, family, and the property-dispute feud with Rudy — correcting an earlier mischaracterization as a hunter/Fed.
  • 2025-05-29 (Act Beat Refinement Help): "Scares off tourists" complaint and the passive-aggressive, never-escalates nature of the day-to-day feud established via Act 1 outlining for What Wasn't and What Was.
  • 2025-06-02/03 (Supernatural Horror Publishers): Rudy's will/mysterious-successor thread cross-linked — context incomplete.
  • 2025-06-04 (Escalating Tensions Rewrite, full transcript): A much fuller version of this chat surfaced later, adding: the MBA and wrestling background; the neon-cowboy grant motivation; two full drafted blowup scenes (directly contradicting the "never escalates" framing above); the surrogate-father dynamic with Jenn, including the CNA job lead and the Hollow Creek rumor he passes to her; and the brainstormed "Book 4" ending where Rudy leaves him the Inn and a letter.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): Flagged a football-vs-wrestling sport discrepancy for his scholarship/MBA origin. Floated a County Commissioner political arc and the "sanitizing the battlefield" thematic parallel, not committed to a specific book slot. Added a far-future drafted scene where Rudy finally levels with him and asks him to keep Jenn safely out of the fight.
  • 2025-06-04 (Escalating Tensions Rewrite, second pass): Added the "jukebox threw up" origin-of-the-feud anecdote, the "Does it matter?" deflection, and his public "he's not dangerous, he's just lonely" line (contrasted with his private, more sympathetic read on Rudy).
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): "Never escalates" vs. blowup drafts resolved — mostly passive-aggressive with safe temper-flares from Rudy, one real explosion reserved for a future case-desperation beat. Who is "she" resolved as Amber, not Jenn.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session, later same day): Roots of the feud resolved — property disputes plus a genuine disagreement over Shepherd Junction's future, no deeper hidden history. A low-key mutual jealousy over Jenn added as an additional layer.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, from "Westernizing What Wasn't"): Confirmed a quotable one-line framing of the feud's philosophical core — Rudy's "safety is something you maintain" against Hale's "safety is something you create."

docs.title: Avery Greene
docs.text:
Location: Stockton, Colorado

Abilities: Accounting, Business Law, Bureaucracy

Background: A sharp accountant with a background in legal matters. Avery has connections in the financial world and knows how to manipulate records when needed.

Setting: A quiet accounting firm in Stockton, where she manages local businesses' finances. Her office would have stacks of paperwork and a computer displaying financial software.

docs.title: Detective Marcus Cole
docs.text:
Location: Brindle, Colorado

Abilities: Forensics, Bureaucracy, Psychology

Background: A seasoned detective with a knack for solving cold cases. He has a deep understanding of forensic science and how to navigate police bureaucracy.

Setting: Based at the Brindle Police Department, he would have a modest office adorned with case files and photographs of past investigations. He might also frequent the local diner to chat with informants.

Relationship with Rudy: His contact at Brindle PD in What Wasn't and What Was — gets Rudy access to reports, footage, and patrol logs off the books when Rudy needs eyes on something before it's buried in paperwork. Rudy's line when asking: "Not pulling rank. Just need eyes on it before someone buries it in paperwork."

Personal life: Has a teenage daughter, Lena, going through a "rebellious phase." Restoring an old Indian motorcycle in his spare time — the carburetor has been giving him trouble for months. Rudy and Marcus are old, easy friends; their calls open with genuine catching-up, not just business.

A serious unresolved twist (Disappearance of Clara McKenzie case, 2024-11-07): At Zack Sawyer's ransacked trailer, Rudy found a handwritten warning note he immediately recognized as Marcus's handwriting: "This should've ended years ago, Rudy. You're not supposed to know any of this. Leave it alone, or someone else will end up in the ground." No signature — just the handwriting. This directly conflicts with Marcus's portrayal earlier the same day as a helpful, trustworthy ally actively helping Rudy investigate Clara's disappearance (he pulled Danny's file, identified Zack Sawyer by his tattoo, and sent Rudy Zack's address). Whether Marcus is genuinely compromised, being impersonated/framed, or something else entirely is completely unresolved — a major open question. See Development Notes & Unresolved Questions.

Confirmed (per GM notes generated during play): Marcus is indebted to The Broker through an "Old Deal" from the past, serious enough to have pushed him to act against his own moral compass — this is likely the actual mechanism behind the warning note, though it hasn't played out in a scene yet. Still actively working the Clara/Zack case with Rudy afterward (radioed Deputy Humboldt away from a confrontation with Rudy at a Brindle church), suggesting he isn't in "deep enough" to have skipped town or cut contact — whatever the debt is, he's still trying to help Rudy within its limits.

Confirmed earlier history (per "No-Tell Motel narration," full data export): Dave directly identified the Vice Detective from Rudy's mid-1990s Stanton City motel-clerk stint as this same Marcus Cole — predating, and presumably seeding, his later role as Rudy's ongoing contact at Brindle PD.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Established as a seasoned Brindle PD detective.
  • 2025-05-29 (Act Beat Refinement Help): Confirmed as Rudy's investigative contact/ally for What Wasn't and What Was.
  • 2024-11-07 (Ghosts of King County, actual-play session): Personal life (daughter Lena, the old Indian motorcycle) established; helped Rudy identify Zack Sawyer and pulled Danny's old file — then, later the same day, his handwriting turned up on a threatening note at Zack's ransacked trailer. Unresolved.

docs.title: Dr. Fiona Blackwood
docs.text:
Location: Stanton City, Colorado

Abilities: Biology, Medicine, Pharmacy, Chemistry

Background: A local physician with a particular interest in biochemistry. She’s well-versed in medicinal practices and pharmaceutical knowledge, often tinkering with new treatments.

Setting: A small medical clinic in Stanton City where she treats patients. Her office could be filled with medical journals and her workspace may have areas for pharmaceutical experiments.

docs.title: Elliot Johnson
docs.text:
Location: Brindle, Colorado

Abilities: Medicine, Biology, Chemistry

Background: Eliot Johnson is a local biochemist and former paramedic turned pharmacist with a deep passion for scientific exploration. After years in the back of an ambulance, he turned to research and currently runs a small laboratory in King County, where he experiments with various compounds and medicinal treatments. His expertise lies in the intersection of chemistry and healthcare, often focusing on innovative solutions for common ailments and exploring the effects of local flora on health.

Connections: His knowledge of biology and chemistry makes him a valuable resource for Rudy, especially when it comes to understanding unusual substances or creating remedies. While he may lack combat experience, his scientific approach provides a unique perspective on the strange occurrences in King County.

Setting: A small biochemistry lab near the [[Brindle Medical Center]], where he conducts experiments and research. His lab could have shelves of chemicals and scientific equipment.

docs.title: Nina Caldwell
docs.text:
Location: Jackson Ridge, Colorado

Abilities: Photography, Art History, Psychology

Background: A local artist and photographer who has a keen eye for details and an understanding of human emotions. She uses her art to explore deeper themes, often reflecting on the psychological states of her subjects.

Setting: A cozy art studio or gallery in Jackson Ridge where she displays her photography and paintings. The atmosphere would be creative, filled with artwork and inspiration from local culture.

docs.title: Old Man West
docs.text:
Location: Red Bridge, Colorado

Abilities: Inspiration, Language, History

Background: Known as Old Man West, he is a local historian and storyteller with a wealth of knowledge about King County. His vivid tales and anecdotes often reveal hidden truths about the area's past. Though he may appear eccentric and aloof, his insights are invaluable, and he possesses a unique ability to inspire those who listen to him. Old Man West is well-versed in local legends, folklore, and the linguistic nuances of the region, making him a rich source of information.

Setting: He's usually found in his RV near the river.

docs.title: Professor Lydia Hawthorne
docs.text:
Professor Lydia Hawthorne

  • Location: Jensen, Colorado
  • Abilities: Anthropology, Archaeology, History, Theology
  • Background: A retired professor specializing in ancient cultures and their religious practices. She’s known for her extensive knowledge of rituals and artifacts.
  • Setting: She could work at a local community college or university, where she teaches classes on anthropology and archaeology. A small office filled with books and artifacts would reflect her passion for ancient cultures.

docs.title: Professor Milton Larkin
docs.text:
Location: Stanton City, Colorado

Abilities: Astronomy, Physics, Cthulhu Mythos

Background: A university professor with a fascination for the cosmos and esoteric lore. He has researched strange phenomena and their potential ties to the supernatural.

Setting: An observatory or [[King County University]] campus, where he lectures about the cosmos. His office might be cluttered with astronomy equipment and strange artifacts related to the Cthulhu Mythos.

docs.title: Reverend Tobias Gray
docs.text:
Location: Stanton City, Colorado, Cathedral of Saint Paul

Abilities: Theology, Psychology, Reassurance

Background: A charismatic Catholic priest at Cathedral of Saint Paul in Stanton City. Reverend Gray is well-versed in Catholic doctrine, including various rituals and the church's teachings on the supernatural. He is known for his ability to comfort and counsel parishioners, often providing guidance in times of crisis.

Connections: His role within the Cathedral of Saint Paul gives him access to community knowledge and insights into local happenings. He may also have a deeper understanding of supernatural events or phenomena, given the Catholic Church’s historical involvement with such matters.

Setting: Cathedral of Saint Paul, where he holds services and provides counseling. The cathedral would be ornate and inviting, filled with parishioners seeking guidance.

docs.title: Allen
docs.text: Grizzled, gray-haired man in sweatpants — "barely overweight," per Mickey's noted effort dragging him. Drugged or otherwise rendered unconscious (breathing, not awake) and hauled by Mickey, Missy, and Harv in the trunk of Mickey's Cadillac to Western Slope Nursing Care, where he's propped into a chair in Room 35, shared with another elderly patient, Frank. Purpose unknown — see The Prodigal Son (A King County Chronicle).

docs.title: Executive Dawes
docs.text:
A county official — blond, receding widow's-peak hairline going grey at the temples. Came to Rudy years before the events of The Prodigal Son's present day, shortly after Rudy first bought Shepherd's Inn — the first case Rudy recalls taking as a hunter-for-hire in King County.

His son and the son's fiancée were kidnapped; first contact, enriched (per "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting"): an envelope containing his son's dog tags (a fellow Gulf War vet, like Rudy) and the fiancée's engagement ring, with instructions to bring money alone to a clearing called Boot Knife. He complied, but the police (who insisted on hidden backup despite his instructions) may have spooked whoever was supposed to collect it — no one showed. The next morning, a second envelope arrived at his office: "I said come alone. Try again," along with a severed adult finger (corrected 2026-07-14 — earlier notes mistakenly attributed a child's finger to this case; that detail actually belongs to Jeff Speno's envelope in the present day, per a careful re-read of the manuscript's confusingly-transitioned flashback).

Went to a psychic first, per the same prewriting doc: Before ever reaching Rudy, Dawes consulted an unnamed psychic, who told him Rudy was his only real chance. Not yet reconciled with how Rudy and Dawes actually connect elsewhere on the page.

Rudy told him he'd find his son. Outcome, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): This is The Three Generations Ritual's ~20-years-ago case — the kidnapper was a blood-mage pursuing a ritual requiring three firstborns across three generations of one bloodline. Rudy killed the mage and saved Dawes and his son, but not the son's fiancée or their unborn child. Dawes's son survived, but carries a vendetta against Rudy for failing to save them — now grown, he's the still-unnamed "kid" Mickey manipulates in the present day, using his grief as fuel to recreate the ritual and go after Rudy (see Mickey Two-Dice). Speno's present-day case is a new recurrence of the same pattern, using an unrelated family, not a reopening of Dawes's own case.

The "thank you gift": Rudy's Shepherd's Inn holds legal immunity from county code enforcement — described as a favor from a county official in gratitude for Rudy saving his son's life. Almost certainly refers to Dawes, though not stated explicitly on the page.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Introduced via The Prodigal Son manuscript as the source of Rudy's first King County hunter case.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed as the same case as The Three Generations Ritual; his son's fate and identity as "the kid" resolved.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting"): Enriched first-contact envelope (dog tags + engagement ring) and added that he consulted a psychic before Rudy — see The Three Generations Ritual.

docs.title: Frank
docs.text: Elderly patient at Western Slope Nursing Care, Room 35 — drooping jowls, forehead wrinkles, the loose double-chin common to the very old. Sleeps through Mickey, Missy, and Harv bringing Allen into his room and propping him in the chair, undisturbed even when the lights are switched on. Mickey stands the two old men up to visually compare them for an unstated purpose — see The Prodigal Son (A King County Chronicle).

docs.title: Jeff Speno
docs.text:
Source: Introduced in The Prodigal Son (Book 2) — Dave later shared the full manuscript directly, which supersedes the summary below (correcting it in several ways) and should be treated as the primary source going forward.

Corrected role (per the actual manuscript text, 2026-07-14): A lawyer, not a "local troublemaker." Meets Rudy for the first time at Shepherd's Inn — no established prior tense relationship. His twelve-year-old son (unnamed on the page) has been kidnapped; someone specifically told him to seek out Rudy rather than the police. Distraught, openly emotional in front of Rudy (tears, shaking voice), and defensive when Rudy presses him on whether he or his son could have had any inappropriate relationship with the boy's tutor/babysitter, Sarah (who has also gone missing, along with the boy). Rudy's investigation into Jeff's case triggers a flashback to an earlier, seemingly identical case — see Executive Dawes — implying Jeff's son's kidnapping is a recurrence of the same MO.

Physical description: Middle-aged, wearing an obviously expensive but slept-in blue tailored suit, hair cleanly parted despite everything else about him looking wrung-out. Five o'clock shadow, tired eyes.

Correction (careful re-read, 2026-07-14): The envelope Jeff hands Rudy at Shepherd's Inn contains a severed child's finger — his son is 12. Rudy's gut-punched reaction ("last time, the first time, it was an adult's finger, but even that experience couldn't prepare him for this") is him mentally comparing it to Dawes's older case, which involved an adult finger. Previously misattributed to Dawes' envelope on both pages — the manuscript's flashback transition back to the present day isn't clearly signposted, which caused the confusion on a first read.

Frank is his own father, confirmed (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting"): Completes the three-generations lineup for the present-day case — Frank (grandfather), Jeff (father), Jeff's kidnapped son (child). Jeff confirms it's really his father when he sees him at the nursing home. See Mickey Two-Dice.

- Role: Local resident and former troublemaker in King County - Background: Known for getting involved in questionable activities. His relationship with Rudy is tense, with hints of past conflicts and resentment. Jeff represents the type of resident that keeps Rudy on his toes—someone who knows enough to be dangerous but not enough to stay out of trouble. - Current Situation: Jeff is being hunted down by unknown forces or individuals. He initially seeks Rudy's help but ultimately falls victim to a trap, potentially set by the vampire Mickey or his associates. (Above struck through — this was a secondhand AI-generated summary of the manuscript from an earlier chat, and doesn't match the primary source. Kept for reference in case any of it turns out to be accurate for later chapters not yet shared.)

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2024 ("The Prodigal Son" manuscript, Chapter 2): Introduced in person at Shepherd's Inn.
  • 2024-10-29 ("Ghosts of King County," discussing the shared document): Summarized secondhand by ChatGPT — this summary turned out to be inaccurate once the primary text was reviewed directly.
  • 2026-07-14 (full manuscript shared directly): Characterization corrected to match the primary source.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting"): Confirmed Frank as his own father, completing the three-generations lineup. See Mickey Two-Dice.

docs.title: Mike Hastings
docs.text:
Works at the Body Shop by Meg's

Was a friend of Danny's

Tall and wiry, graying hair, grease-stained hands, worn denim jacket over his shop shirt. Guarded when Clara and Danny come up, but gave Rudy Danny's current number — with the condition that Rudy not mention it came from him.

docs.title: Sarah
docs.text:
Jeff Speno's son's tutor — picks him up after school and watches him until Jeff or his wife can collect him. Barely eighteen. Per Jeff (pressed hard on the point by Rudy, who's ruled out any inappropriate relationship with the boy): a lesbian, which Jeff offers as evidence nothing romantic was happening between her and either him or his son.

Missing along with the boy — no one had heard from her, and Jeff initially assumed she'd simply taken him to a movie and would drop him off after. When nobody showed by 9pm, Jeff let himself into her house with the spare key from a fake rock; no one was there.

Fate, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): A Doll candidate — one of Kayla's fleshcraft/bonecraft victims (see Spanish Dolls), tying her disappearance directly into Mickey and Kayla's ongoing scheme rather than leaving it a dead-end tragedy unconnected to the main plot.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2024 ("The Prodigal Son" manuscript, Chapter 2): Introduced as Jeff Speno's son's missing tutor.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Fate resolved — a Doll candidate.

docs.title: Silas Freeman
docs.text:
Silas "The Fat Man" Freeman

Type: Psychic (Human) Role: True psychic and a fixture in Stanton City, operating from a Chinese restaurant

Background: Silas operates as a true psychic with prophetic visions, taking over the “Fat Man” title from his predecessor, a genuinely large psychic. Silas’s abilities make him a subtle but powerful figure in Stanton City, where he operates out of Empire Dragon Chinese Cuisine.

Personality and Influence: Silas is a respected and somewhat feared figure due to his prophetic abilities. He fills a unique niche in King County as a human with supernatural insight, making him both valuable and dangerous to those who interact with him.

Current Situation: Silas’s role in the story highlights the subtle supernatural undercurrent of King County. His visions might provide guidance but could also come with a price, depending on the information shared and the person receiving it.

Physical description (confirmed in The Prodigal Son): An incredibly fat white man, draped in swap-meet samurai/kimono attire with a thinning black topknot — a stereotype-baiting first impression that Rudy corrects on a second look ("not Asian... just" the above). Speaks in a voice higher than expected. Holds court alone in a booth built for six, behind a bamboo-and-paper divider at the back of Empire Dragon Chinese Cuisine.

Contact method: Reaches out cryptically via fortune cookie — a specific delivery, paid for in advance, sent to a specific person, with a handwritten fortune slip replacing the usual generic one (e.g., "Midnight, tonight. Bring the finger," with the restaurant's address on the reverse). The delivery drivers and kitchen staff seem unbothered by/used to this arrangement.

Ability mechanic — needs a physical touchstone: Explicitly requires bringing a physical object connected to a case in order to read it — greeting Rudy with "I hope you brought the finger… I'm afraid I can't do much else without it." Implies a psychometry-style power (reading impressions off objects) rather than pure clairvoyance from nothing.

Continuity resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, per "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting"): Twenty years ago, during Dawes's case, the Chinese-restaurant contact was a different person — Silas's own predecessor as "the Fat Man" (see the pre-existing "taking over the title from his predecessor" line above), using an elaborate blood/Baijiu/hot-pot ritual to grant visions rather than the touchstone mechanic below. Rudy returns to the same restaurant in the present-day case hoping the same old man is still there, only to find Silas has since taken his place. This is exactly why Silas genuinely denies knowing anything about Dawes in Chapter 3 — he never met him; that was his predecessor's case, not his own.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2024 ("The Prodigal Son," Chapter 3): Physical description, contact method, and booth setup established when Rudy meets him in person for the first time (regarding Jeff Speno's case, and recalling Executive Dawes's earlier one).
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session, integrated from "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting"): Resolved the apparent continuity tension between that early draft and the manuscript — the "old man" from 20 years ago is confirmed as Silas's own predecessor as "the Fat Man," not Silas himself. Rudy returns hoping to find the same contact and finds Silas in his place instead.

docs.title: Trish
docs.text: Waitress at Meg's Diner Supervisor to Clara McKenzie and Danny

docs.title: Amber
docs.text:
Bartender at Lucky's Place, a dive bar in Jackson Ridge. Early 20s.

Confirmed by Dave directly: Amber is the "she" from Rudy's will/letter to Hale — the person who eventually takes over (or helps take a load off) Shepherd's Inn, whether because Rudy dies or just needs a break. This resolves the long-standing "who is she" open question — see Development Notes & Unresolved Questions, Rudy Williams, and Nathan Hale.

The mechanism, confirmed (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): The Clara McKenzie case is what actually gets her there. Investigating alongside Rudy exposes her to the supernatural directly and proves to him she can handle it — she doesn't flinch, doesn't back out even after being warned ("I'm in, Rudy. Weird or not."). Combined with her existing bar experience at Lucky's Place, Rudy hires her to cover shifts at Shepherd's Inn — practically useful on its own, but it also solves a real problem for him: he no longer has to invent a story for coming home covered in blood, his own or something else's, since she already knows what he actually does.

docs.title: What Actually Happened (played scene, "Ghosts of King County")
docs.text:
Found by Rudy at Zack Sawyer's ransacked trailer, also looking for Zack — she'd come to check on him after he stopped answering his phone and missing work. Wary at first (hand near a gun in her jacket pocket), she warms up once Rudy mentions Danny sent him. Practical, loyal, and not easily spooked: agrees to help investigate despite Rudy's warning that things might get supernatural and that her life "will never be the same" if she comes along. Her answer: "I'm in, Rudy. Weird or not."

Background with Zack: Close friends for years — shared a rave by the train yards in Stanton City (~2 years ago) and a drunk hike to Delicate Arch in Moab (last summer). His journal entries about these trips make clear he had unspoken feelings for her, which visibly catches her off guard when Rudy points it out.

What she knows about the party (the night Clara disappeared): She was at the party but didn't follow the group into the woods. Confirms it was Zack, Clara, Ashley (Zack's cousin, close as siblings), and an unnamed "goth girl" (with a crew that hung back at the party's edge, watching but not mixing) who went deeper into the woods together — Danny went partway too, but came back alone and pale, brushing off questions. Zack came back "real spooked" and stayed jumpy for years after, convinced someone or something was watching him. Says the site was out by Raven's Creek, reached via the Hollow Path trail off an old logging road, past a clearing — an area the county closed off years ago after the bridge over the creek rotted out.

Zack's journal (found in his room): Two entries stand out — "Saw her again tonight. She looked… wrong. But she knew things. Things no one should know about that night in the woods. Said they'd come for me if I didn't listen." and "It's not just whispers anymore. Shadows, moving where they shouldn't. I'm starting to wonder if Danny was right. Maybe we didn't leave all of them behind that night." No dates, but the second entry is more recent (references the Moab trip).

Current plan: Agreed to go with Rudy to talk to Johnny and then Ashley at Lucky's Place, and to lead Rudy out to the party site the next morning (with Johnny guiding partway) rather than go into the woods at night.

Carries a gun and says she'll use it if it comes to protecting Zack or herself — visibly nervous about that prospect but not backing down.

![[Amber.webp]]

Name sourced from Dave's own prior material, confirmed (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): "Amber" wasn't invented fresh for this — Dave pulled it from his own earlier play-by-post game material ("I just looked it up").

Origin scene, drafted (same source): Rudy tries to warn her off the investigation, cryptically ("You will learn things you can never unlearn, and you'll never look at him the same way again" — ostensibly about her missing friend, really about the supernatural at large). She makes clear she'd have found that world regardless of whether Rudy showed up — he decides it's safer to keep her close than leave her an uncontrolled variable. Dave's own stated theme for this: "Once you see the darkness, it sees you." Her own later line to Rudy, once she's proven herself: "You don't have to do this alone, you know. I'm here. I'm willing. This isn't some secret war only you can fight... If anything, you need more soldiers on your side."

Specific book placement, confirmed (2026-07-22, from "King County Chronicles Book Map.xlsx"): Introduced in Book 3 (Clara), hired on as Shepherd's Inn's bartender in Book 4 (Doc). See Book Order and Series Structure.

Employment mechanism, confirmed (same source): Once Shepherd's Inn loses its code-enforcement immunity (end of Book 2), Rudy can't keep closing for unexplained "family emergencies" — so he hires her as the Inn's actual first employee, since she already knows what he really does and won't panic when his mask slips.

Friendship with Jenn, floated (same source): Dave wants a real friendship built between Amber and Jenn before Jenn's Book 6 kidnapping, specifically so it has two books' worth of runway rather than feeling sprung on the reader.

Book 6, drafted (per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): By this point she's a skilled protégé Rudy won't let help directly — he's seen what Mickey does to people and won't risk losing another one he's responsible for. Mickey targets her along with Moss and Jenn to punish Rudy; Rudy eventually lets her fight, and she survives, but barely. See Book Order and Series Structure and Mickey Two-Dice.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2024-10-29/2024-11-07 ("Ghosts of King County"): Introduced in play at Zack's trailer; full backstory with Zack, the party-night account, and Zack's journal established.
  • 2026-07-14 (relayed directly by Dave, source chat not yet reviewed): Confirmed as the "she" who inherits/takes over Shepherd's Inn in Rudy's eventual will.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Reaffirmed as Amber, not Jenn, with the actual hiring mechanism confirmed — the Clara case proves her capability, and Rudy hires her on at the Inn.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): Drafted her role in Book 6 — sidelined by Rudy, targeted by Mickey, survives but barely. Also confirmed her name was sourced from Dave's own prior game material, her origin/recruitment scene, the Shepherd's Inn hiring mechanism, and a floated pre-kidnapping friendship with Jenn.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session, from "King County Chronicles Book Map.xlsx"): Confirmed specific book placements — introduced in Book 3, hired at Shepherd's Inn in Book 4.

docs.title: Ashley
docs.text:
Zack Sawyer's cousin — close as siblings, especially since Zack's mother wasn't around much. Lives in Jackson Ridge; Amber sees her at Lucky's Place occasionally, though they aren't close these days. More guarded than the rest of the group.

Was part of the group that followed Clara deeper into the woods the night of the party, along with Zack and an unnamed "goth girl" whose crew Ashley seemed to know from somewhere.

First meeting with Rudy (played scene, cut off mid-conversation): Found at Lucky's, nursing a drink. Guarded but not hostile — accepts a cigarette from Rudy and confirms she, Zack, and the goth-kid crew went back to the woods that night, framing it as just a good party at first. Was about to say more when the session log ends.

![[Ashley.webp]]

docs.title: Clara McKenzie
docs.text:
Went missing about two years before Rudy's investigation reopened the case.

Friends with Danny Close friends with Trish Worked at Meg's Diner

Short blonde hair with festive tattoos.

Personality (per Trish and Danny): Friendly, eager to help, "had that spark." Not usually one for parties.

Final known movements: Attended a party in the woods near Brindle not long before she disappeared, where she overheard talk of "a sacrifice... a ritual in the woods" and followed a small group (including Zack Sawyer and an unnamed woman) deeper into the trees against Danny's advice. Vanished for good after her next shift at Meg's — the same night captured on the cassette that reopened her case.

docs.title: Danny
docs.text:
Friend of Clara McKenzie Friend of Mike Hastings Used to work at Meg's Diner Moved to Denver, reportedly hit hard by Clara's disappearance and blaming himself for not stopping her.

Mike gave Rudy Danny's number.

Phone call with Rudy (2024-11-07): Confirmed "that night" was a party in the woods near Brindle (bonfire, beer, the usual crowd from Brindle/Jackson Ridge) shortly before Clara vanished. Clara wasn't normally one for parties but insisted on going. Danny saw her wandering off alone, looking over her shoulder like she was being followed; she told him she'd heard people talking about "dark shit — rituals and sacrifices." A small group split off from the party and headed deeper into the woods, and Clara followed them despite Danny trying to talk her out of it. He never saw her again after that night — she disappeared following her next diner shift (the one captured on the cassette). Danny started seeing shadows himself afterward and still feels watched.

Remembers two unfamiliar faces from the group that split off: a young man he thinks was named "Zack or Zane," with a snake/vine tattoo winding up his neck (see Zack Sawyer), and a quiet young woman in black who kept her distance from the rest of the party. Named Amber and Johnny as two locals who were also at the party and might remember exactly where it happened (near the Brindle trails or out by Red Bridge). Warned Rudy to be careful — "those woods... there's something wrong with them."

docs.title: Deputy Humboldt
docs.text:
Young King County Sheriff's deputy, recently transferred from Nevada.

Name, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Humboldt. The one-off "Holcomb" was most likely a misremembering — Dave's own or ChatGPT's — not a real alternate name.

Responded to Rudy's deer collision on the way to Stanton City. Knew Rudy's name on sight despite Rudy not recognizing him and Humboldt never having been to Shepherd's Inn — unexplained, and it unsettled Rudy enough to note his name for later. Marcus Cole confirmed Humboldt is new to the department and shouldn't have any obvious reason to know Rudy.

Mentioned recent strange noises in the woods (more than the usual coyotes) and a handful of unproven local disappearances, advising people to stick to main roads after dark.

Suspected (unconfirmed) of taking the cassette tape from Rudy's truck during the traffic stop — the tape vanished sometime during the encounter, and no other explanation has surfaced.

docs.title: Follow-up confrontation (played scene, church behind the Brindle service station)
docs.text:
Turned up again while Rudy and Amber waited for Johnny's smoke break, clearly needling Rudy about "showing up where trouble's brewing." Confirmed via Johnny that Humboldt has been loitering around that specific service station "more than usual" for a couple of weeks — watching for someone or something, not just idle. Got called away by a radio dispatch from Marcus Cole before the confrontation escalated; unclear whether Marcus's timing was deliberate cover for Rudy or a genuine coincidence.

Working theory (per the GM's framing during play, not yet confirmed in a scene): Humboldt's animosity toward Rudy is tied to Rudy's time in the Marines — the implication is that Rudy did something, in that period, that divided Humboldt's family. Not yet explained further.

![[Deputy Humboldt.webp]]

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2024-10-30 ("Ghosts of King County"): Introduced at the deer-collision traffic stop.
  • 2024-11-07 ("Ghosts of King County," continued): Follow-up confrontation at the church; loitering pattern and the Marines/family-grudge theory established.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Name settled as Humboldt; "Holcomb" logged as a misremembering, not a real alternate.

docs.title: Johnny
docs.text:
Works at the service station in Brindle. Dirty blond hair, usually grease-stained.

Was at the party in the woods the night Clara disappeared, per Danny — a possible lead on where exactly that party happened.

docs.title: What Actually Happened (played scene)
docs.text:
Wary but willing to talk once Rudy and Amber approach him at work — asked them to meet him on his smoke break behind the church next to the service station rather than talk on the lot, since Deputy Humboldt had been hanging around the area "more than usual" for a couple weeks, seemingly watching for something or someone.

Confirmed Zack and Clara were "glued at the hip" most of the party, and that Zack, Clara, and Ashley went off with a crew of "goth kids" Ashley seemed to know from somewhere. Recalled rumors from older kids about an overgrown ruin about half a mile from the campsite — supposedly an old meeting place for "cults or whatever," never taken seriously until now. Agreed to guide Rudy and Amber out to the site the next morning, but not go in himself.

![[Johnny.webp]]

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text: • 2024-10-29/2024-11-07 ("Ghosts of King County"): Introduced in play; Humboldt's suspicious loitering and the ruins lead established.

docs.title: The Broker
docs.text:
Unseen contact, saved in Zack Sawyer's phone as "The Broker." Deals in information, smuggling, and silence — for a price. Described by Rudy as more myth than known figure in the darker circles he's aware of.

Last message to Zack, sent the night his trailer was ransacked and he went missing: "If you still want out, tonight's the night. Bring the cash, and don't tell a soul."

Confirmed connection to Marcus Cole: Detective Marcus Cole is indebted to The Broker through an "Old Deal" struck sometime in the past — the exact nature of the debt is unknown, but it's serious enough that it's pushed Marcus to act against his own moral compass, which explains (at least in part) the warning note in his handwriting found at Zack's trailer. Whether The Broker is directly involved in Clara's or Zack's disappearance, or just adjacent to it through Marcus, is still unresolved. See Development Notes & Unresolved Questions.

Operates through debt and leverage rather than force — keeps a network of people obligated to them through secrets and old favors. Reputation alone is usually enough to keep people in line.

docs.title: Zack Sawyer
docs.text:
Local with a rep for petty theft, bar fights, and vandalism — a "two-bit shit-stirrer," per Marcus Cole, not a major player.

Neck tattoo — a snake or vine winding up his throat.

Lives in a single-wide trailer in Brindle.

Was at the party in the woods the night Clara disappeared, along with an unnamed girl in black — the two of them headed into the trees ahead of her, like they knew where they were going.

Status unclear (2024-11-07): Rudy arrived at his trailer to find it ransacked, a note in what looks like Marcus Cole's handwriting warning Rudy off ("This should've ended years ago, Rudy... Leave it alone, or someone else will end up in the ground"), and Zack's shattered phone showing a text thread with "The Broker" — the last message reading "If you still want out, tonight's the night. Bring the cash, and don't tell a soul." Zack himself wasn't there. A woman showed up moments later, also looking for him.

![[Zack Sawyer.webp]]

docs.title: Ben
docs.text:
Tina's son — roughly the age that made Rudy briefly wonder if Ben might be his own (see Rudy Williams and Tina). Tina confirmed he isn't; if Rudy hadn't left her, she'd never have met Ben's actual father, still around and "a pretty awesome guy."

One of the creek kids, resolved (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session — this is also the "Ben" previously flagged as possible ChatGPT contamination): Ben is the kid who meets Jamie at the tree line and explains the local kids' fear and rituals around the Huntsman, whom the creek kids call "the Hollowman." He's sneaking out early that morning specifically to nail his baby sister's baby tooth to the tree near the Hedge opening — a folk-charm meant to keep her safe from the Hollowman. Deliberately kept as the same character rather than introducing a new NPC to fill the role — Dave's own standing preference is to reuse an existing character over adding one when the story allows it.

Ben is also the vault's previously-unnamed "second missing kid," confirmed (2026-07-16, decided live — from "Westernizing What Wasn't"): After roughly eight years failing to catch Jamie directly, The Huntsman changes tactics and kidnaps Ben instead — exploiting the one thing Jamie won't run from, someone else in danger. This forces Rudy and Jamie into Arcadia (confirmed, not the Hedge — see The Huntsman) to get him back, which is where the Huntsman is finally killed. Timing, confirmed same session: this happens at the end of Act II, functioning as the book's red herring/false climax — Rudy and the reader are primed to assume the Huntsman took Ben the same way it's always pursued Jamie, when really it's an unrelated tragedy from Caleb's belief-based existence fading (see Caleb). Rescuing Ben is also the mechanism by which Rudy and Jamie learn Caleb was never really there (see What Wasn't and What Was) — the real, rescued child is proof of what Caleb's absence couldn't be. This ties Ben directly into both the A and B plots rather than leaving him a background character.

Confirmed sequencing (2026-07-18, decided live, grill-canon session): Ben meets Jamie at the tree line (above) → sometime later, the Huntsman's "Near Miss" beat happens (see What Wasn't and What Was) → after that, Ben goes missing. His disappearance is what moves "the Huntsman took Caleb" from a maybe to a near-certainty in Rudy's read of the case — deliberately, since the Huntsman wants Moss to know it was the one who took him. There's no ambiguity or misdirection on that specific point within the B plot itself; the misdirection is entirely about what it means for the A plot (Caleb). Near Miss location, confirmed (per "Making What Wasn't Scooby-Doo," full data export): the Near Miss beat needs its own location, separate from this tree-line meeting — deliberate narrative separation so it doesn't read as the same scene once Ben disappears. See The Huntsman.

Founding relic and timeline, confirmed (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): The tree's first nailed tooth was Jamie/Moss's own — knocked loose tripping on the way out of the Hedge, too old by then (~12) to still be losing baby teeth naturally. All of the kids' actual lore around the tree was invented after Jamie's return, 8 years ago — meaning there's only 8 years' worth of teeth actually nailed into the bark, not centuries of them despite how old the ritual might feel to the kids who grew up with it.

How the illusion holds up for adults, confirmed (same source): Older siblings quietly swap the tooth for a coin before or after the actual parents do the tooth-fairy routine — each parent assumes the other one did it, or forgot. Adults keep believing in an ordinary tooth fairy while the kids secretly maintain the real ward underneath it.

The tooth-nailing rule, confirmed (2026-07-18, decided live, grill-canon session): The rule of the charm isn't that a kid protects themself — someone else has to nail a kid's baby tooth to the tree on their behalf. Ben has a few of his own baby teeth left that haven't been placed yet, since he can't do it himself. Floated, not fully locked: Jamie/Moss might be the one who ends up nailing Ben's tooth to the tree for him. Confirmed same session: this rule isn't something Arcadia or the Huntsman designed — it's a binding the Huntsman picked up entirely from the creek kids' own superstition, and the Huntsman is genuinely, "weirdly" bound by it anyway. See The Huntsman.

The bloody molar clue, confirmed (2026-07-18, decided live, grill-canon session): When the Huntsman takes Ben, it leaves a bloody baby molar under his pillow — a fucked-up tooth fairy, deliberately. This is a real, plantable clue with two distinct readings that split exactly along the case's supernatural/mundane fault line (see the "a clue must lead somewhere" principle): Rudy's read is procedural — he wants to confirm whether Ben had actually lost that tooth recently, since kids commonly lie to their parents about losing teeth to collect tooth-fairy money, so it might just be a coincidence worth ruling out. Jamie's read is immediate and certain — Moss knows exactly what the tooth means the second they see it, tied directly to the tooth-nailing charm's rules (see above): the Huntsman isn't just taking Ben, it's mocking the very ward meant to stop it. Discovery mechanism, confirmed (per "Making What Wasn't Scooby-Doo," full data export — Dave's own reasoning): Dave was explicit that this can't be a clue Rudy or Jamie go digging for — Ben already told Moss directly that he had a loose molar he wanted nailed to the tree once it fell out, so the reader already has the information. The tooth under the pillow is found because Ben's mother, grieving and looking for anything of her son's to hold onto while Rudy questions her, lifts his pillow herself. Rudy then confirms with her that Ben hadn't mentioned losing that tooth. The clue isn't uncovered by investigative cleverness — it's stumbled into, which is what makes it land as devastating rather than procedural.

Confirmed climax beat (2026-07-18, decided live, grill-canon session): When Rudy and Jamie go into Arcadia looking for missing kids, Ben is the only one they actually find — and Ben confirms directly that he was the only child he ever saw there. The Huntsman may also confirm this, possibly mockingly, hinting that Caleb "wasn't his to take" (or something similarly suggestive of Caleb's belief-based nature) without giving the full reveal away. Exact integration with the pre-existing 2.6 "empty clearing" beat left deliberately unresolved for now — see the flag on What Wasn't and What Was.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
