They'd pull the box down and start looking AFTER Rudy got there."

The reveal: When Rudy finally presents the evidence that Caleb was never born, Leanne is already crying before he finishes. She's known. The moment is meant to land as acceptance, not exposition. Her side of it: she tried to forget the accident, only remembered coming home with a baby who smiled, liked books, wore a hoodie with ears; over time the memories fought back and doubt crept in, and when she checked his empty room, she knew. Her line: "I didn't want to believe I could un-believe someone."

The real accident (confirmed mechanism — see Caleb and What Wasn't and What Was): The "unregistered car" backstory beat is the actual accident. She borrowed the car to clear her head before the baby came, crashed it solo, and was admitted to a hospital as a Jane Doe — pregnant on arrival, no longer pregnant on discharge — before leaving without signing out. The real baby was cremated in an unmarked community plot. Caleb is the thoughtform her grief and belief built afterward.

Family: Has a husband (unnamed) who works a factory night shift or drives long-haul trucking — hard labor that pays just enough. They live in a trailer that's clean and modest despite being beyond the plow line. They're offering a reward for Caleb's return — not huge, but it's all they have, meant to show they're serious.

Same person, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): "Caleb's dad" and the husband above are the same man, not two different people — Dave's own reasoning: Leanne has enough going on already without adding "he's not his real dad" drama on top of it. Whether the "stable husband, night shift" framing or the "not currently together, scared fragile couple" framing better describes where they stand at any given point in the story is still open, but it's the same person either way.

Backstory beat: Borrowed an unregistered car about six years ago to get to work when hers broke down (earlier framing) / to clear her head before the baby came (later, confirmed framing — this is the actual accident, see above) — no judgment attached in-story, just texture establishing her as one of the area's low-to-middle-class, "used-to-be-the-bad-kid" type residents.

Social isolation (established during Rudy's investigation): Old friends say she quietly drifted away into motherhood after Caleb — not tragic, just a choice, as far as they knew. Neighbor quotes: "Cute kid. Kept to themselves." "I thought Leanne moved away, honestly." "We invited her once, but she never came."

Key lines: - On Caleb's disappearance: "He was just… gone. One minute I was reading, the next minute the silence was too loud." - To Rudy, asking for help: "I heard you help people when the cops can't." - A clue she doesn't realize she's dropped: "I just want to find him again. Even if it's not exactly the way I remember." - At the vigil, correcting someone who calls Caleb "Jacob," instantly and automatically, not confused or angry — just fast and practiced: "His name is Caleb."

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025-06-05 (Pulpify your Story): Introduced as Caleb's mother, trailer park beyond the plow line; nightly shoe ritual; brainstormed cursed-ground/well detail; townsfolk unease.
  • 2025-06-05 (Mystery Clues and Emotion): Soft-edged birth memories and her reaction to the reveal established as part of the confirmed Caleb-was-never-born twist.
  • 2025-05-29 (Act Beat Refinement Help): Husband, trailer, reward, unregistered-car backstory, social isolation, and key dialogue lines established via Act 1 outlining for What Wasn't and What Was.
  • 2025-06-02 (Project Chronicle Summary): Confirmed the unregistered car incident was the actual accident that caused the real pregnancy loss; her account of the slow-dawning realization established. Flagged a discrepancy between the earlier "husband" and this chat's "not together, scared fragile couple" framing of Caleb's dad.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed "Caleb's dad" and the husband are the same person — no paternity-doubt drama intended.

docs.title: Rye
docs.text:
A road companion Moss traveled with for a time. Age uncertain, likely in late 20s; Moss never asked. Road-worn, cocky, "too clever for his own damn good," talked like "a podcast host, all confidence and bullshit wisdom." Wore a neon orange hoodie with "SECURITY" ironed on the back (a joke), always inside out. Called Moss "Cavekid" — said like a compliment, like they were carved out of myth. Confirmed (per "Moss and the Huntsman," full data export): not just a hunch — Rye is another Changeling, and the nickname comes from genuinely recognizing something old and mythic in Moss, not idle flattery.

How Rye found Moss, confirmed (per "Moss and the Huntsman," full data export): Rye caught Moss "staring" — Moss's own term for shutting down and going unblinking-still for hours — more than a few times, on top of a handful of other tells, and put it together before Moss ever said a word about it. He told Moss his own story in turn: taken right after he started Kindergarten, subjectively not in Arcadia very long, but he came out a full-grown adult — and a replacement had been living with his family the whole time, for what was, to them, only a month or two. His family didn't believe him. They called the cops.

"Time went backwards," confirmed as Rye's own phrase, not literal (per "Moss and the Huntsman," full data export): Rye's own words for what happened to him. Not actual reversed causality — Arcadia's time and the mortal world's were never coupled to begin with. He lived out a childhood-to-adulthood's worth of years; his family lost a month or two. "Backwards" is just the only word he had for something that inverted what should've been true: he should be the one who lost the least time, and instead he's the one who lost everything.

Draft dialogue, not locked as final prose (per "Moss and the Huntsman," full data export): "You know you don't have to do that, right?" ... "The staring." A pause. "I'm not staring." "Yeah, you are." "No. I'm listening." That's the exchange where Rye realizes what Moss actually is.

The dawn rule and what happened, revised (2026-07-22, decided live): Moss and Rye caught out together in Nebraska, camped under a half-collapsed boxcar with a can of Sterno and a jar of peaches. Moss refuses to hop a train at dawn, same as always ("We wait. Dawn's bad luck" / "It's when I came back"). Rye's running line about it was always "That's your ghost, Cavekid. Not mine" — not disbelief, since he's lived his own version of Arcadia's cruelty, just genuine confidence the rule was Moss's alone, not a real danger to him. This time it wasn't. A "railroad bull" appears out of nowhere — a man in a hat, exactly the Huntsman's established disguise (see The Huntsman) — and Rye clocks what it actually is a half-second before Moss does. He shoves Moss toward the grainer, putting his own body between them and it. In the scramble — a shout, a flashlight, a bang that hit metal, not flesh — Rye's leg goes under the wheel. His last words to Moss: "You were right. Cavekid... you were right." Moss runs before the ambulance arrives, and never sees for certain what happens next.

Confirmed dead, killed protecting Moss (2026-07-22, decided live — resolves the previously open fate below): Dave's own call: Rye died from the injury. The Huntsman gets a real, confirmed kill on the page's backstory — not just a false lead for Rudy, but an already-proven killer for the reader too, which is exactly the weight the Huntsman-as-red-herring needs. Moss doesn't know this for certain, having fled before the ambulance arrived — that uncertainty is its own quiet wound, separate from the confirmed truth of what actually happened.

Aftermath: His hoodie was soaked in blood in the immediate aftermath. This is the origin of Moss's dawn curse — now doubly earned, since the rule was never superstition, it was a warning about a real, already-successful killer. Moss won't speak Rye's name aloud, and leaves orange scraps (torn hoodie bits, burnt caution tape) tied to fenceposts near trainyards — a warning for others, a shrine. At dawn, Moss sometimes hears his voice: "Just ride, Cavekid. Just go." Thematic closing line: "Dawn still means blood on the rails."

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025-06-02 (Train Hopping Farewell Scene): Introduced; full backstory, the dawn-rule incident, and the ongoing shrine ritual established. Fate left deliberately ambiguous. Bloodied-hoodie detail and closing thematic line added on a second pass.
  • 2025 ("Moss and the Huntsman," full data export — decided live, resolving a fork raised the same session): Confirmed as a fellow Changeling, not just someone who sensed something odd about Moss — he recognized Moss's staring episodes directly and shared his own "time went backwards" Kindergarten/Arcadia story, which is how the two first bonded. The dawn-rule mockery scene revised to fit this: Rye doesn't disbelieve the supernatural, he just doesn't think the rule applies to him specifically. Revised phrasing not yet locked as final prose. Clarified same session: "time went backwards" is Rye's own folk phrase, not literal — Arcadia and mortal time were simply never coupled.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Rye's previously-open fate resolved — he's dead, killed protecting Moss from the Huntsman. The train-yard "railroad bull" is confirmed as the Huntsman itself in its established man-in-a-hat disguise; Rye recognized it and shoved Moss clear, which is how his leg went under the wheel. Moss doesn't know this for certain (fled before the ambulance arrived), but the vault's canon does. Explicitly meant to give the Huntsman a real, felt kill for the reader, reinforcing it as a genuine threat and not just a red herring for Rudy alone.

docs.title: The Lawyer in the RV
docs.text:
The community's default suspect in Caleb's disappearance — a man living in an RV near the woods on the other side of the creek from Leanne's trailer park. A deliberate, fully-cleared red herring in What Wasn't and What Was, not a hidden villain.

Backstory: Formerly a defense attorney in a two-man firm — his partner handled custody cases, he handled DUI defense. Falsely accused of molesting his partner's own child (age 9 at the time of testimony). Per the partner's own deathbed confession letter, the partner bribed the child to testify against him to protect himself: "Said he panicked. Said his wife would've left if she thought it was him. So he bought the kid a dirt bike and said, 'Tell the court what I tell you to.'" Convicted on testimony alone, no physical evidence — sentenced to four years, served eighteen months. His appeal was initially denied. His former partner died of pancreatic cancer roughly three years later and confessed in writing; the victim, by then a teenager, recanted. His conviction was vacated via post-conviction relief — legally exonerated, not merely acquitted, pardoned, or expunged.

Prison left him permanently disabled — beaten/assaulted while incarcerated, now needs a four-point cane (a walker would be more appropriate but doesn't fit inside his RV). This is a deliberate, unstated clue: kids describe the real watcher in the woods as tall and thin; the lawyer is short and heavyset, confirming — without anyone in-story stating it outright — that he isn't who the kids have actually been seeing.

Present day: Town opinion never caught up to his legal exoneration. Someone slashed his tires the month before the story's events, "preemptively protecting" a five-year-old daughter. Kids dare each other to sneak up and touch his RV — notably, it's always the older kids who do it, never the younger ones. Payoff, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): a minor, non-case-critical clue, not a plot-important one. The younger kids are simply afraid of him; the older kids feed that fear by daring each other to touch the RV, since younger kids don't yet have the same rites-of-passage dynamic older kids do. Deliberately left abstract — no defined age or grade marks the "older/younger" line.

How Rudy actually clears him: Rudy physically walks the terrain and determines the lawyer could not have reached the creek where the kids have been seeing something — the two sides are separated by a "Detroit riprap" wall of five stacked junked cars, essentially unknown to adults, passable only via a garden-hose "rope" tied to a fence post and a dirt slide — kid-only infrastructure a man with a cane could never use. The police never verified this themselves; they built their case on "proximity, opportunity, history. Done." His own alibi for the night in question — that he was with a woman — turns out to be true, corroborated by undated (deliberately, jokingly timestamped "Jan 1, 2000 12:00 AM") kinky Polaroids/video, which is enough to satisfy Rudy without needing the terrain evidence at all.

Key line: "You think I'm your monster, detective? I'm not. I'm just what you find when you're looking too hard in the wrong places."

In the book's front matter ("Welcome to King County" blurb): Referenced without a name — "They say the lawyer in the RV used to be someone. Made the news. Made a list, too. Now he's not allowed near schools."

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025 ("Clue Evaluation Assistance" / "Jane Doe Investigation Plausibility," full data export): Full backstory, exoneration mechanics, disability/cane detail, and the terrain-based clearing method established, evolving from an earlier, less-developed "convicted child molester" version of the same red herring.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): "Older kids only" payoff resolved — a minor fear/rites-of-passage clue, not case-critical.

docs.title: Tina
docs.text:
A woman Rudy was involved with roughly eight years before the events of What Wasn't and What Was — she lived in the same trailer park as Leanne, near the barn where Jamie first came out of the Hedge. Confirmed to attend Caleb's candlelight vigil.

Confirmed reason Rudy was at the trailer park that night (per "Jane Doe Investigation Plausibility," full data export): Dave's own phrasing, deliberately loose: "Rudy was in that trailer park 8 years ago to get his CB radio fixed or maybe a booty call — maybe both." Not narrowed further than that.

Relationship arc, resolved (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session — supersedes the three previously-floated, never-narrowed options): Started as a no-strings casual thing. Rudy started catching real feelings, and pulled away rather than let her in — not wanting to endanger her by bringing his past and the world's secrets into her life. He left her place at sunup after they'd been up late together; he doesn't sleep, so he was awake when he made the decision, and left before she woke rather than have the conversation. That's the state things were in when he found Jamie fleeing the Huntsman in the field outside — see Jamie.

Ben, resolved (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session — this is also the answer to Rudy Williams's "Which Kid Does He Think Is His?" open question): Tina is the mother of a son, Ben, roughly the right age that Rudy briefly wonders if Ben is his own. Tina confirms Ben isn't Rudy's — and tells him that if he hadn't left, she'd never have met Ben's actual father, who's still around and "a pretty awesome guy." Ben is also the same kid who meets Jamie at the tree line — see his own page for that scene and the previously-flagged "orphaned name" contamination note it resolves.

Unresolved craft use: floated as someone who could offhandedly destabilize Rudy's own memory of the past ("I remember Leanne from back then. I don't remember her ever being pregnant.") — not confirmed as adopted dialogue.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025 ("Clue Evaluation Assistance," full data export): Introduced as a vigil attendee and a piece of Rudy's unresolved past in the same trailer park; backstory options floated, none chosen.
  • 2026-07-15 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed the full relationship arc (no-strings turned real feelings, Rudy pulling away to protect her, leaving at sunup after she fell asleep) and that he found Jamie immediately after leaving her place. Follow-up same day: confirmed her son Ben, roughly Rudy's-kid age, and that Ben isn't his.

docs.title: Timeline of Key Events
docs.text:
• 1986: The California Incident occurs. Rudy is 15 years old, and Phoebe is 5. the Monster Squad prevents The Vampire from completing a dark ritual with the help of Scary German Guy and The Promethean's sacrifice.

  • 1990–1991: Rudy enlists in the Marines and is deployed to Kuwait during the Gulf War (Operation Desert Storm). He spends years honing his survival skills, tactics, and resilience, which later shape his approach to supernatural threats.

  • 1999: After roughly 10 years of service, Rudy leaves the Marines at age 28. He has zero interest in reenlisting after 9/11 and instead finds himself drawn to King County, where Phoebe resides in The Asylum.

  • 2014 (Game Setting): Approximately 15 years after leaving the Marines, Rudy is around 43 years old. He’s spent years watching over Phoebe, haunted by the deaths of his former friends from the Monster Squad and the lingering threats he believes are still hunting them.

docs.title: The California Incident
docs.text:
The true events that inspired The Monster Squad (Movie)

Location: Small town in California (setting of The Monster Squad (Movie))

Date: 1986, confirmed — Rudy was 15 and Phoebe was 5.

Involved Parties: the Monster Squad, Scary German Guy, the Werewolf Pack, Vampire and Followers (including a Promethean)

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
• Background, motive confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): A vampire (not Dracula, confirmed Lasombra — see The Vampire) arrived in a small California town obsessed with the Abyss itself, true Lasombra fashion. The goal wasn't to open a door and step through — it was to tear it wide enough to drag the Abyss through into the town, and hopefully let it keep spreading past that.

  • Gathering Forces: The vampire’s presence attracted nearby supernatural forces, including the California Werewolf Pack, who sensed the Wyrm-taint and attempted to stop the vampire. The vampire’s followers included a flesh golem (following the rules of a Promethean), a twisted creation bound to serve him.

  • Monster Squad Intervention: The only ones who uncovered the full scope of the vampire’s plan were Rudy and his friends, later dubbed the Monster Squad With the help of Scary German Guy, an experienced mage, they pieced together the vampire’s ritual and prepared to intervene.

  • The Ritual and Phoebe's Awakening (resolved 2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session — full sequence confirmed 2026-07-22): With the Abyss tearing open and threatening to engulf the town, The Promethean shoves the Vampire into the breach — sacrificing himself by going in after him — and Phoebe seals the portal shut behind both of them via a spontaneous Awakening, with Scary German Guy genuinely present and involved but not the source of the power. Though successful, this act traumatized Phoebe, leading to her current catatonic state. The ritual stopped the Abyss from actually breaching through, but at the cost of leaving residual energy in the area — a lasting scar on the supernatural fabric of the town, closer to a near-catastrophe averted than a clean victory.

docs.title: Lingering Effects and Consequences
docs.text:
• Residual Wyrm-Taint: The ritual, combined with the vampire’s Wyrm-tainted energy, left a permanent “mark” on the town. This lingering effect may have attracted other supernatural beings over the years or created subtle disturbances in the area.

  • Deaths and Disappearances of Monster Squad Members: Following the incident, members of the Monster Squad began dying under mysterious circumstances. Rudy suspects these deaths were connected to the vampire’s ritual, either as revenge or due to a supernatural “curse” left behind.

  • Phoebe’s Current Condition: Phoebe’s trauma and early Awakening left her catatonic, trapped in a mental state that may still be linked to the portal’s energy. Her condition motivated Rudy’s move to King County, where she resides in The Asylum.

  • Unresolved Role of the Promethean: The vampire’s Promethean follower was destroyed, but traces of its energy or parts may remain. This creature’s twisted essence might still affect the location or even extend to King County.

  • Location detail: The portal ritual took place in tunnels under the town — the specific pressure/wrongness of that moment is what Rudy recognizes again years later during the events of What Wasn't and What Was.

  • Why that town specifically: Brainstormed (not fully confirmed) explanation for why the Vampire chose it — the town sits on a Locus, a ley-line crossing, or somewhere the Veil runs unusually thin, which is also why he needed physical protection (Frankenstein and the blood-bound Wolfman) rather than acting alone.

  • Setting name, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Staying with California — plain "a small town in California," not San Bruno, not Baton Rouge, and not renamed "The Louisiana Incident." Dave's reasoning: it makes more sense for Humboldt to have heard of an incident in California than one in Louisiana, simply on geographic proximity to Colorado — notable since why Humboldt knows anything about Rudy at all is still its own unresolved mystery (see The Disappearance of Clara McKenzie open threads). The San Bruno and Baton Rouge retcons, and the "Louisiana Incident" naming idea, are all set aside.

  • Vampire's target and clan, firmed up further: Probably Lasombra, not just a generic powerful vampire. Wasn't opening a way to Limbo or the Underworld specifically — the target was the Abyss. Confirmed still trapped there roughly 30 years later, as of King County's "present day" — see The Vampire for the confirmed series endpoint (he and The Promethean return, and Mickey Two-Dice's eventual diablerie of him is tied directly to Rudy's own fate).

  • Phoebe's role, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Spontaneous Awakening — Phoebe sealed the breach herself, at around age six. Scary German Guy was genuinely there and involved, but she did the actual work; the older "used as a battery for his ritual" framing is retired.

  • Narrative frame (from the same "Lore post" brainstorm): The story is told by an adult Rudy, late at night at Shepherd's Inn well after closing, to an unidentified listener sitting in deep shadow across the booth — someone whose drink empties over the course of the telling without Rudy ever seeing them take a sip. Never explained or flagged as intentionally supernatural, just an unresolved, faintly eerie detail baked into the frame. Conceived as "the first in a series of Lore posts" providing in-world background on King County's people, places, and events.

  • Named members beyond Rudy and Phoebe: Sean — Phoebe's older brother and the Monster Club's actual leader (see Sean). Horace — a summer neighborhood friend of Rudy's before either of them knew he'd be nicknamed "Fat Kid" at school; gets his own "level-up" moment fighting one of the Vampire's brides (see Horace). An earlier draft also included "Patrick" (plus a subplot about blackmailing Patrick's sister) and "Eugene," both deliberately cut — see Out of Scope Ideas.

  • Foreshadowing beat: Before the abandoned-house discovery, Sean — whose father is a cop — tells the others about a break-in shooting at the police station the night before: a man shot while breaking in, his body missing afterward, and the responding ambulance driver found dead with no explanation. This is the eventual Wolfman surrendering himself, being shot, and regenerating before vanishing.

  • No diary in this version, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Unlike the film, there's no German-language diary to translate. Instead, Scary German Guy was already independently investigating the Vampire's house when the kids arrived there on their own. Dave's best reconstruction, not stated with full certainty: the kids didn't so much need his help as insist they were now part of it — either he begrudgingly worked with them, or the two groups worked separately and got in each other's way.

  • Aftermath — Rudy's recruitment: After the incident, Rudy was specifically recruited into a Marine unit that deals with supernatural threats (brainstormed as similar to Dogs of War content or a Hunter: the Vigil Compact/Conspiracy) — directly because of what happened here, not as a coincidence of general military service. This unit is also the likeliest explanation, per this brainstorm, for why the Army showed up at the climax at all.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Core incident summary established.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Vampire's true motive confirmed — pure Abyss-obsession, not power/service/ritual; the goal was to drag the Abyss through into the town, not to visit it. Full ritual sequence confirmed: the Promethean pushes the Vampire into the breach and follows him in, and Phoebe seals the portal behind both of them — explaining why both are confirmed to return later in the series. See The Vampire.
  • 2025-05-29 (Act Beat Refinement Help): Tunnels-under-town location detail established, referenced as a sense-memory trigger for Rudy in What Wasn't and What Was.
  • 2024-10-29 ("Ghosts of King County," the original setting-building chat that seeded this vault): Date confirmed as 1986, with Rudy's and Phoebe's ages pinned down (15 and 5, respectively).
  • 2025 ("California Incident Brainstorming," full data export): Locus/leyline rationale, the narrative frame device, Sean and Horace as named members, the police-station foreshadowing beat, the no-diary recruitment gap, and the post-incident military recruitment all established. Also the point where two different real-world setting retcons (San Bruno, then Baton Rouge) were explored and never reconciled with the vault's plain "California town."
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Setting name resolved — staying with California, over both real-world retcons and the "Louisiana Incident" rename idea.

docs.title: The Three Generations Ritual
docs.text:
A standalone origin story for how Rudy and Mickey first met — brainstormed but not resolved (the conversation ends mid-brainstorm on an open question). Set roughly 20 years before the "present day" of The Prodigal Son/"Ghosts of King County," with period tech "roughly Y2K, 2005 at the most."

This is very likely the concrete mechanic behind the "generational ritual" theme merged into The Prodigal Son (see that page's Thematic Layer section) — a repeating ritual across a family line, grandfather/father/child, with "at least two prior, unseen occurrences" implied. This case may be one of those prior occurrences. It also strongly echoes the Dawes/Speno kidnapping MO in The Prodigal Son (a finger mailed as proof/warning, a "no cops"/"come alone" demand, escalation after the family involves police) — worth treating these as the same recurring pattern rather than coincidence.

docs.title: The Ritual
docs.text:
A blood-mage's method for extending life: drain and bathe in the blood of three firstborns across three generations of one bloodline (e.g. grandfather, father, and child). The mage gains three times the combined remaining lifespan the victims would have had. Dave's own math example: a 60-year-old (10 years left), a 40-year-old (30 years left), and a 10-year-old (60 years left) sum to 100 years — tripled to 300 years gained.

Likely connection to Mickey's current warehouse construct: The three suspended autopsy tables draining via copper piping into a single bathtub (see Mickey Two-Dice) match this ritual's mechanics almost exactly — three victims, one collecting basin. Not yet confirmed as the same ritual on the page, but the parallel is hard to miss.

docs.title: The Original Case (~20 years before "now")
docs.text:
The father is Executive Dawes, confirmed (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session) — a county official who came to Rudy years before the events of The Prodigal Son, shortly after Rudy first bought Shepherd's Inn. His teenage son and the son's pregnant girlfriend/fiancée had been kidnapped by the blood-mage. The mage's plan: use a money-for-victims ransom exchange as a ruse to kidnap the father too, then drain father, son, and the unborn child (the girlfriend's pregnancy providing the "child" generation) all at once.

  • First contact, enriched (per "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting"): The mage mailed Dawes his son's dog tags (the son's a Gulf War vet, like Rudy) and the fiancée's engagement ring, along with instructions to bring money alone.
  • Dawes initially involved police anyway; the ransom drop failed as a result.
  • A second envelope arrived: a finger, and the note "I said [no cops/come alone]."
  • Rudy was brought in only after this escalation — not from the start.
  • Outcome: Rudy saved the father and the son. He did not save the girlfriend or the unborn child — they were killed; the mage had already delivered the infant via crude C-section to use as the third generation. A partial victory with a permanent scar.
  • The mage's title, per the same prewriting doc: Referred to as "the Immortal" — implying this wasn't his first attempt at the ritual, or that he'd at least partially succeeded before. Killed by Rudy during this case: an arrow to the temple (not immediately fatal) followed by a knife across the throat.
  • The referring psychic, per the same doc: Dawes first went to an (unnamed, female) psychic, who told him his only real chance was Rudy. Rudy later returned to the same psychic when the trail went cold; she couldn't foretell anything herself and referred him onward to Empire Dragon Chinese Cuisine — where the actual ritual was performed not by Silas Freeman, but by his predecessor as "the Fat Man," using an elaborate blood/Baijiu/hot-pot mechanic (resolved 2026-07-22, see Silas Freeman).

docs.title: Mickey's Involvement (present day, unresolved)
docs.text:
Mickey seeks Golconda — or, failing that, immortality without risk of being consumed by his Beast. He somehow learned of this ritual and the failed attempt from 20 years ago — how exactly was an open "gap" when this specific conversation ended, with three options brainstormed and none chosen at the time: 1. Mickey found the dead mage's grimoire/notes (possibly cursed or warded), including a margin note with Rudy's name. 2. Mickey fed on someone connected to the case and absorbed impressions/memories of it. 3. A supernatural intermediary (a Seer, a Fae, an Ordo Dracul researcher) told him, for a price.

This gap is very likely resolved in a separate, later chat ("Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Dave states directly, "The way I figured Mickey would find out about it would be some esoteric murderbilia — the original blood-bather's notes" — i.e. option 1 above. The two chats never cross-reference each other, so this is treated as a strong resolution rather than an assumption, but worth double-checking with Dave since the connection is inferred across sessions.

The surviving son — Dawes's son — now has a vendetta against Rudy, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session) — not Mickey. He blames Rudy for failing to save his girlfriend and their unborn child. He's also confirmed as "the kid" — Mickey's unnamed present-day assistant in The Prodigal Son (see Mickey Two-Dice). Now grown, he's being manipulated by Mickey, who's using his grief as fuel — both to help recreate the ritual and to go after Rudy. The two have different motives in the same scheme: the kid genuinely wants revenge; Mickey personally doesn't care about Rudy at all at this point, the kid is simply useful to him. One still-unconfirmed idea for how their working relationship started: Mickey approaches the now-grown son looking for ritual info, the son refuses and attacks him, and that fight is how Rudy first hears of Mickey at all — no longer in conflict with anything, just not locked in.

Likely connected scene — Rudy's epilogue line to the father (per "Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): In the confirmed present-day case this ritual likely underlies (see Mickey Two-Dice for the reasoning connecting this to the Dawes/Speno kidnapping), Rudy tells the grandfather only a partial truth rather than the full story: "He got mixed up in something and I couldn't save him."

Climax choreography — correction (second-pass catch, 2026-07-22): The version pulled from "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting" on an earlier pass today (the kid forcing his own finger through the trigger guard in a hammerlock, effectively a forced execution) is the exact "cliché cop out" staging Dave already explicitly rejected on Mickey Two-Dice's own page, in favor of a different, already-locked version: Mickey uses The Passion to dial Rudy's emotions down rather than up, making him stop fearing his own death rather than making him more lethal; Rudy walks into the gun without flinching, takes a hit, grapples the kid down, and the gun discharges accidentally in the struggle — not a deliberate or forced kill. Mickey uncloaks in fury that his "blood key" is ruined (not a monologue about consuming the kid's immortality), and sunrise forces him to flee. That locked version stands; the forced-trigger staging is retired, not merged. See Mickey Two-Dice for the full, correct staging.

What might still carry over, unconfirmed: The kid's own belief that completing the ritual would make him personally immortal (rather than just avenging himself on Rudy) doesn't obviously conflict with the "blood key" framing — he could believe one thing while Mickey privately intends another. Whether Dave wants that detail folded in, along with the specific hand-injury framing (bullet graze between the first and second knuckle, left hand) as flavor consistent with "takes a hit," is open — not assumed here.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025 ("Bridging Story Gaps," full data export): Brainstormed as a standalone story explaining Rudy and Mickey's first meeting. Conversation ends before the "how Mickey learned of the ritual" gap is resolved.
  • 2025 ("Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Very likely resolves the "how Mickey learned of the ritual" gap (murderbilia/the mage's own notes) and adds a confirmed epilogue line — but also introduces a target inconsistency for the survivor's grudge (Mickey vs. Rudy) that isn't reconciled with this page's earlier framing.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Vendetta target resolved as Rudy. The surviving son is confirmed as "the kid" — Mickey's present-day assistant in The Prodigal Son, manipulated by Mickey using his grief as fuel.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session, later same day): The father identified as Executive Dawes — this is his case, referenced elsewhere in The Prodigal Son.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting," a Google Docs dump written when this book was planned as the series' first story): Enriched the original case (dog tags/ring as first contact, the mage's "the Immortal" title, exact kill method). Also resolved a continuity question this same document raised — the ritualist Dawes and Rudy consulted 20 years ago is Silas's predecessor as "the Fat Man," not Silas himself. See Rudy Williams and Silas Freeman.
  • 2026-07-22 (second-pass correction, same session): Caught that the prewriting doc's present-day climax staging (forced-trigger-pull execution) is the same version Dave already explicitly rejected as a "cliché cop out" on Mickey Two-Dice's page. Retired that staging in favor of the already-locked version there; flagged what might still carry over (the kid's personal-immortality belief, the hand-injury detail) as unconfirmed rather than assumed.

docs.title: California Werewolf Pack
docs.text:
Pack Name: Unknown (specific name not given in past notes)

Role in Setting: Failed to stop the vampire in California, resulting in one member being blood-bound; potential supernatural allies or wary observers of King County

Current Status: Presumed active, though their territory and members are unknown; may avoid King County due to its supernatural properties

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
• Role in The California Incident: A local werewolf pack caught wind of the vampire’s ritual to open a portal, likely due to the Wyrm-tainted energy emanating from his activities. They attempted to take down the vampire but were ultimately unsuccessful, resulting in one member being blood-bound to the vampire and forced to serve him. See Wolfman for the brainstormed specifics of that member's arc — surrendering to police to avoid hurting anyone, being shot and regenerating, the blood bond, and his eventual death by silver at Rudy's hand.

  • Failed Confrontation: This confrontation left the pack weakened and wary of high-risk supernatural threats. Their failure to prevent the portal ritual may have solidified their reluctance to intervene directly with powerful supernatural beings unless absolutely necessary.

  • Pack Structure: It’s unclear if the pack has traditional roles (such as alpha, beta, etc.) or operates with a more decentralized structure. They may be tied to a larger network of werewolf packs, which could impact their response to King County’s unusual reputation.

docs.title: Abilities and Traits
docs.text:
• Heightened Awareness of Wyrm Taint: The pack possesses a strong sensitivity to Wyrm-corrupted energy, which may make them aware of the residual effects of the vampire’s portal. This awareness could explain why they’ve avoided King County, as it may hold echoes of corruption that put them on edge.

  • Supernatural Combat Skills: As werewolves, the pack has experience with supernatural combat and the power dynamics of supernatural creatures. This would have made them formidable allies in California, had they succeeded, and could make them potential allies or adversaries for Rudy in the future.

  • Potential Knowledge of King County: Their knowledge of Wyrm corruption might give them insight into the no-man’s-land effect surrounding King County, even if they avoid it. They may have records or oral histories explaining why the area is unsafe for supernatural beings.

docs.title: Influence and Legacy
docs.text:
• Reputation for Survival: Though they failed in California, the werewolf pack likely carries a reputation among local supernatural circles for their resilience and knowledge of corrupted entities. This reputation might precede them if they enter King County, potentially creating tension with local entities.

  • Unresolved Grudge Against Vampires: The pack’s history with the vampire in California may leave them with a lingering distrust or even a vendetta against vampires, especially if they feel the blood-bound member was never freed from their influence. This could lead them to distrust Rudy initially or seek to test his allegiances if they cross paths.

docs.title: Hunter Network
docs.text:
Rudy's loose network of hunters and investigators, covering a broad region from King County to Green River, Utah (see Rudy Williams).

Sign system (unnamed): Members and supernatural-aware travelers mark warnings and information using a variant of traditional hobo code — chalk or coal symbols left in the open, but with esoteric additions specific to the supernatural (not the TV show Supernatural, though it uses a similar concept of hunter-marked signs). Likely used to flag things like safe houses, active threats, thin spots, or "someone's watching this place."

Occupational hazards of the profession (per The Prodigal Son): Alcoholism, depression, and lechery run rampant among hunters — suicide, overdoses, and untreated disease reportedly kill as many hunters as the actual job does. Rudy considers himself one of the lucky ones, being "well on his way to dying of old age," which he notes is practically unheard of in the profession. Reinforces why he set up shop in a bar specifically.

A rival/unaffiliated group (per The Prodigal Son): Rudy has heard a new group of hunters set up recently in Stanton City — distinct from his own network, unnamed, and not yet interacted with on the page.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Established as Rudy's hunter network.
  • 2025-06-02 (Project Chronicle Summary), corrected 2026-07-14: A chalked sign was initially logged under a cross-contaminated name ("skav," from a different, unrelated story ChatGPT pulled in). Corrected to this hunter/hobo-code sign concept per Dave.

docs.title: The Monster Squad (group)
docs.text:
This is for the Monster Squad faction. For the film, see The Monster Squad (Movie)

Founded By: Sean — the club's actual leader, not Rudy — with Rudy, Horace, and Phoebe (Sean's younger sister, who insists on tagging along) among its core members

Current Status: Disbanded; most members are presumed deceased or missing

Primary Purpose: Originally formed to investigate strange occurrences in their California hometown, culminating in a battle to prevent a vampire from opening a portal to a supernatural realm.

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
• Origin and Purpose: The Monster Squad began as a group of neighborhood kids interested in monsters and horror movies. They accidentally stumbled upon a real supernatural threat when a vampire attempted to open a portal in their town, using Wyrm-tainted magic and dark rituals.

  • The Portal Incident: Their investigation led them to confront a vampire (different from the Dracula depicted in the film) who sought to open a portal to a realm like Limbo, the Abyss, or the Underworld. Assisted by a nearby werewolf pack, the kids worked with an older mage (Scary German Guy) who used Phoebe’s latent magical power to close the portal.

  • Aftermath, revised (2026-07-22, decided live): While they succeeded, the experience had lasting effects. Phoebe was left traumatized and eventually became catatonic. The group's actual dissolution, though, wasn't primarily an external force picking them off — it was Rudy himself. He became the "obsessed hunter" type even as a teenager, well before the Marines, and that obsession is what drove the rest of the group away, gradually, over a spread-out stretch of time rather than one clean break. Horace wanted nothing to do with it — he wanted to be safe, and ignorance was bliss — and pulled away from Rudy specifically. Sean stayed invested longer and in his own way, but personality conflicts meant the two of them couldn't keep working together either. Rudy's belief that the group was being "systematically eliminated" is, at least in part, a story he tells himself — see each member's own page for their actual, individual fates.

docs.title: Notable Members
docs.text:
• Rudy Williams: Served as the group's leader during the portal incident. Now a hardened veteran and protector of Phoebe, Rudy has long believed the group's former members were systematically eliminated — though the truth, per each member's own page, is closer to his own growing obsession driving them away than any external force hunting them.

  • Phoebe: The youngest and most sensitive to supernatural forces, Phoebe served as a magical conduit to close the portal. Her current state leaves her unable to assist, but she remains a significant figure, especially for Rudy.

  • Scary German Guy (Mage): Assisted in closing the portal, using his knowledge of the arcane and ritual magic. His current whereabouts are unknown, though Rudy remembers him as an important ally.

  • Sean: Phoebe's older brother and the club's true leader — the one with the schemes, the one who brought the police-station shooting/missing-body rumor to the group's attention. Drifted from Rudy over personality conflict, not an external threat; fate deliberately left open.

  • Horace: A summer-friend-turned-club-member; has his own confirmed "level-up" moment fighting one of the Vampire's brides. Pulled away from Rudy's growing obsession wanting to stay safe; died of pneumonia as an adult, unconnected to the case — a deliberate homage to the real actor who played him.

docs.title: Influence and Legacy
docs.text:
• Unresolved Threats, revised (2026-07-22, decided live): Rudy has long believed the Monster Squad's activities put a target on the backs of the original members, that something's been slowly eliminating witnesses. Confirmed instead: at least for Horace and Sean, the real story is that Rudy's own obsession drove them away long before anything else could — no confirmed external force, no confirmed pattern. Whether that belief is pure guilt/grief talking, or whether something really is out there for the members whose fates remain genuinely unknown, is left open.

  • Potential for Resurrection: The legacy of the Monster Squad could make it a symbol for younger, local groups interested in paranormal events or investigating unusual occurrences in King County, Colorado, especially if the local supernatural undercurrents become more active.

  • Impact on Rudy: The traumatic events solidified Rudy’s dedication to defending the innocent and protecting Phoebe. His loyalty to the Monster Squad’s memory keeps him grounded but also haunted.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Core faction background, notable members, and legacy established.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Revised the group's dissolution — Rudy's own growing obsession, well before the Marines, is what actually drove Horace and Sean away, not a confirmed external threat systematically eliminating members. See Horace, Sean, and Rudy Williams.

docs.title: United States Marine Corps
docs.text:
Full Name: United States Marine Corps

Primary Role in Setting: Source of Rudy’s combat skills, survival training, and tactical mindset

Connection to Main Characters: Rudy Williams served in the Marines after his time in juvenile detention, gaining critical skills and experiences that still shape his approach to supernatural threats.

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
• Rudy’s Enlistment: Following his time in juvenile detention after the events in California, Rudy joined the Marines, seeking both a fresh start and a way to channel his lingering anger and trauma. His time in the service gave him structure and a hardened outlook, which has served him well in his encounters with the supernatural.

  • Experience and Skillset: Rudy underwent extensive training in survival, combat, and tactics. His skills range from weapon proficiency to guerrilla-style tactics, which he frequently applies to his ongoing efforts to protect himself and Phoebe. His Marine training also taught him to operate in isolated, high-stress environments, which has proven invaluable in King County, Colorado.

  • Involuntary Discharge: Rudy was discharged after a classified mission went disastrously wrong, making him the sole survivor. This incident deeply affected him, compounding his already complex survivor’s guilt from The Monster Squad (group) days. The details of the mission are classified, but Rudy carries both physical and emotional scars from it.

docs.title: Influence and Legacy
docs.text:
• Mental Resilience: Despite his traumas, Rudy’s military background has given him a resilience that helps him face both his inner demons and the very real supernatural threats in King County, Colorado. His training as a Marine helps him compartmentalize, allowing him to focus on protecting Phoebe and dealing with the dangers he believes are still out there.

  • Reputation as a Loner: Rudy’s time in the Marines reinforced his tendency to operate alone. Though he occasionally connects with others in King County, Colorado, he keeps himself at a distance, wary of forming attachments and risking others’ safety.

  • Practical Knowledge and Weapon Proficiency: Rudy’s expertise includes a variety of firearms, hand-to-hand combat, and survival techniques. These skills make him a formidable opponent and help him adapt quickly to the unusual threats he faces in the supernatural no-man’s-land.

docs.title: God's House
docs.text:
An old farmstead house standing alone in an open field — aged but loved, chipping paint, mismatched porch steps. Empty and dusty inside. Every wall is covered in handwritten prayer transcriptions ("Please help dad beat cancer," "please make sure Bosco finds a good home in heaven"). Opening any cupboard releases a whispered prayer, building to a cacophony if several are opened at once. Dave's own closing line for the concept: "This is God's house, but God isn't home."

Confirmed (per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): Reached by Rudy fleeing through a Hedge-adjacent dream/memory space, chased by buffalo the size of buses that knock down trees but won't enter the clearing around the house — they're driving him toward it, not hunting him. He's stuck there for a subjectively endless stretch (days, weeks), and nothing resolves until he writes a new prayer of his own — there's always paper but never a pen, so he ends up writing it in his own blood, drawn with a push-pin pulled from another note on the wall. He doesn't mail it (there's a mailbox, but that breaks the house's internal logic) — he pins it to the wall with the rest. The sequence doesn't end until he sleeps again after writing it.

Confirmed later use: Rudy was "fully gone" here once — genuinely lost/erased for a time, per his own history with the place. This is later why it works as a hiding spot from Mickey near the series finale (see Book Order and Series Structure) — Mickey has no way to perceive into a place where Rudy was once erased.

The bottle-episode this is built for, drafted (per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): Dave's own pitch for a Silent-Hill-flavored haunted-house episode (placed around Book 6, after Jenn's left Rudy and Phoebe's beginning to reawaken) — Rudy in a snowstorm sees a little girl in the road, crashes his truck swerving, follows her figure into the wilds calling "Cheryl... is that Cheryl?" (it's actually Phoebe). Chased through a Hedge-adjacent dream space by buffalo the size of buses that knock down trees but won't enter this clearing — driving him toward God's House, not hunting him. Confirmed beat: at some point Rudy "nuts up" and marches toward the danger with a bow in hand and a cigarette in his mouth, saying "I'm in this goddamn club, aren't I?" — a deliberate Monster Squad callback, the moment it's clear who Rudy and Phoebe really are. He eventually wakes in the old Monster Squad treehouse. Dave explicitly rejected a "child-Rudy" framing for this sequence ("I don't really like child-Rudy that much"), but confirmed he wants the teeth motif and the Huntsman to resurface here, and liked the idea of figures from earlier books showing up as memory-echoes in the space generally. Floated, not confirmed: a WandaVision-style reveal that Phoebe herself caused this space, compared by Dave to "Sam Winchester scratching the wall between him and his soulless past."

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session, integrated from "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): Introduced as Dave's own concept for a Book 6-ish haunted-house/bottle-episode sequence, and confirmed as a later hiding spot near the series finale.
  • 2026-07-22 (later same day): Added the full bottle-episode pitch — the buffalo chase, the "Cheryl"/Phoebe reveal, the "I'm in this goddamn club, aren't I?" Monster Squad callback, and the treehouse wake-up. Noted Dave's rejection of a child-Rudy framing and the still-floated WandaVision-style twist.

docs.title: King County, Colorado
docs.text:
Location: Western Slope, Colorado Rocky Mountains; based on real life cities such as Montrose, Delta, Grand Junction, Avon, Vail, and Crescent Junction (Utah). Per The Prodigal Son, Rudy's own working sense of the county's boundaries runs "effectively from Stanton City to Stockton, over to Jackson Ridge, down to Shepherd Junction, and back to Stanton City."

Town-to-real-world mapping, confirmed (2026-07-22, from a rediscovered Google Docs town-naming pass): - Stanton City — Grand Junction, CO (largest city in the region) - Shepherd Junction — Crescent Junction, UT - Jackson Ridge — Durango, CO - Hyatt City — Cedaredge, CO - Jensen — Montrose, CO - Red Bridge — Olathe, CO - Brindle — Delta, CO

Geography along the Shepherd-Jackson Highway, confirmed (2026-07-22): Shepherd Junction (north) → Red Bridge → Jackson Ridge (south), in that order. Jensen is a separate town east of Jackson Ridge, off this route entirely.

Reputation: Known as a supernatural no-man’s-land, with rumors and local legends suggesting that something dark and dangerous lingers within its boundaries.

Primary Inhabitants: Sparse population, mostly humans; minimal supernatural presence due to its reputation as an avoided area among supernatural creatures.

docs.title: Physical Description
docs.text:
• Geography and Terrain: King County encompasses rugged mountains, dense forests, isolated valleys, and the occasional abandoned town or mining site. The area’s natural beauty is juxtaposed with an eerie sense of isolation, especially in its more remote areas.

  • Atmosphere: There’s a pervasive feeling of dread in King County, even among its human residents. Locals speak of the “silent places” where strange sounds and shadows appear without warning, and most avoid the wilderness after dark.

  • Core visual/moral design rule: Everything in King County is a little darker and a little dirtier than real life — both literally (dimmer light, long shadows, dust on every surface, grime in every corner) and morally (trust is scarce, choices carry heavy consequences). Because of this baseline, places that are actually bright, clean, and shiny — like LeGrande Station — read as unsettling rather than welcoming: an almost ethereal glow that feels alien against the county's decay, as if the cleanliness is hiding something.

  • Confirmed existence mechanics (per "Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export): King County "doesn't exactly EXIST" in-universe — findable if you look, overlapping roughly where Mesa, Delta, and Montrose counties sit in real Colorado. More precise phrasing Dave settled on: the county sits IN a metaphysical fracture, not merely ON one. Its borders are inconsistent between the maps that do show it. I-70 and rail lines physically run through it regardless. Truckers delivering into its towns know it exists but can't reliably pin down where; the same stretch of highway sometimes has exits for Shepherd Junction and Stanton City and sometimes doesn't. Residents can leave and return freely; the county receives both intentional and unintentional visitors; none of this registers as strange to an average person living there. Dave deliberately does NOT want the mechanism over-codified ("it just does") — floated, without committing, that it might work something like Werewolf Lunacy (selective forgetting/unnoticing).

  • Setting technology level (per the original "Ghosts of King County" solo game, set ~2014 in-fiction): Deliberately behind real-world 2014 — closer to an early-2000s feel. No smartphones exist at all (the setting predates the 2007 iPhone). The Motorola Razr V3 is cutting-edge; Rudy carries an old Nokia 3310. Landlines are still the most reliable form of communication, especially in rural areas. Home internet is roughly a decade behind (dial-up/slow DSL common). Physical media (VHS, DVDs, CDs) remains standard; streaming doesn't exist yet in-setting.

  • Local Landmarks:

    • The Asylum: A mental health facility where Phoebe resides, located near a forested area that only deepens the sense of mystery.
    • Abandoned Mines and Caves: Remnants from the region’s mining days, some of which are rumored to connect to hidden supernatural sites.
    • Cursed Canyons and Ravines: Unmapped regions that locals claim are haunted or cursed, often avoided due to dangerous terrain and strange occurrences.

docs.title: Supernatural Significance
docs.text:
• No-Man’s-Land Status: King County’s supernatural reputation is one of avoidance and mystery. Even powerful supernatural creatures, including werewolves and vampires, keep their distance, leaving the area with an unspoken aura of corruption or danger.

  • Residual Wyrm Taint: There are whispers among supernatural circles that King County holds traces of Wyrm-tainted energy, a contamination often tied to corruption and decay. Some believe that this energy dates back to a failed ritual or an ancient curse, giving rise to the region’s “cursed” status.

  • Same wound as California, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): King County's fracture and the tear The Vampire opened in California are the same underlying wound into the Abyss — not two separate curses, two weak points along one much older crack. King County's point is ancient, predating the 1986 incident by far longer than the county's own recorded history; the Vampire simply found and forced open a different weak point along that same tear, closer to the surface and easier to widen. This is also why the two places share the same flavor of "wrongness" rather than two unrelated cosmic horrors.

  • Interdimensional Qualities: The strange energy in King County may act as a thin veil between realms, contributing to its avoided status. Supernatural beings who venture too close describe sensations of vertigo, as though they’re teetering on the edge of a chasm or another dimension.

docs.title: Human Population and Local Lore
docs.text:
• Human Residents: The human population is sparse, consisting of resilient families, old miners, and a few isolated settlements. Most locals are unaware of the supernatural, though they pass down vague stories and cautionary tales about the dangers of the area.

    • Local Law Enforcement: King County law enforcement discreetly acknowledges Rudy's expertise, occasionally recommending him off the books for cases that seem beyond standard protocol.

  • Local Legends and Superstitions:

    • The Phantom Lights: Ghostly lights that appear in the mountains, leading travelers astray or vanishing when approached.
    • The Hollow Peaks: Sections of the mountain range where people have reported hearing voices or experiencing intense unease, as if being watched.
    • The Whispers in the Pines: Locals claim that certain groves carry whispers that only appear during twilight, warning people away.

docs.title: Notable Locations:
docs.text:
• Shepherd Junction
    • Shepherd's Inn
      • Owner / Operator: Rudy Williams

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Core setting established — no-man's-land reputation, physical description, supernatural significance, local legends.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed the county's fracture is the same wound as the one The Vampire tore open in California — an ancient crack in the Abyss with multiple weak points, King County's being far older than the 1986 incident. See The Vampire and Phoebe.
  • 2024-10-29 ("Ghosts of King County," the original setting-building chat that seeded this vault): Core visual/moral design rule (darker and dirtier than real life, clean places feel alien) and the setting's technology level (no smartphones, Nokia 3310, dial-up internet, ~2014 in-fiction) established.
  • 2025 ("Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export): Confirmed the county's specific existence mechanics — sitting in rather than on the fracture, inconsistent map borders, the intermittent Shepherd Junction/Stanton City highway exits, and the deliberate choice not to over-codify why the county is hard to pin down.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session, from a rediscovered Google Docs town-naming pass): Consolidated the full town-to-real-world mapping in one place; corrected a Jackson Ridge/Hyatt City mixup (Jackson Ridge is Durango, not Cedaredge); resolved Red Bridge as Moss's drug-run town from The Huntsman. Pinned down the actual geography: Shepherd Junction → Red Bridge → Jackson Ridge along the Shepherd-Jackson Highway, with Jensen a separate town east of Jackson Ridge, off that route.

docs.title: Boot Knife
docs.text:
Old fur-trapper nickname for a remote clearing deep in the King County woods — "the largest clearing for miles in any direction," so dangerous "you'd better bring a boot-knife." Rumored territory for both Sasquatch and Wendigo sightings.

Fur trappers gave way to homesteaders, whose abandoned homesteads later became squats for teenagers hiding from local law (many of whom, ironically, had done the same thing themselves years earlier).

Site of Executive Dawes's ransom drop for his kidnapped son, years before the present-day events of The Prodigal Son — the drop failed, and things escalated from there.

Real-world origin, confirmed (2026-07-22): The farmhouse itself is reused from Dave's own "Poker Night" tabletop group's actual play-through of the published Hunter: the Vigil adventure The Harvesters — after the fence Christie Reese sent them after David D'Angelo, the party found him at this farmhouse and beat him to death there (with a sledgehammer, no less). A corrupt local sheriff, Ostler, was knocked out and dragged along by the party earlier, then handcuffed inside the same farmhouse and left behind with D'Angelo's body when the party moved on. Dave's plan for that old campaign was to bring Ostler back later as a Slasher — that never happened; the players simply never resolved him, and Poker Night ended with him still cuffed there. Not adopted into King County canon (2026-07-22, decided live) — Dave's open to him resurfacing eventually, but he's nothing important right now. Dormant, not a live thread.

Now redeveloped as Aspenview Estates — a gated luxury housing development ("lots now available to build, 2+ acres"), six-foot stucco wall, wrought-iron keypad gate, private security patrol. Rudy barely recognized the turnoff on his return years later; the old rocky, switchback-heavy back road was replaced with a freshly paved two-lane, and the mile markers no longer matched his memory. Went in armed with a flashlight, compound bow, and a full quiver of broadhead arrows (favoring quiet over a firearm) after scaling the wall to avoid the gate — the mostly-built lots, thick with young quaking aspens blocking sightlines between houses, took longer to search on foot than expected, and a slow-cruising private security patrol forced him to move carefully. Turned up nothing before he gave up around 3am and moved on to other leads. The irony of the transformation — cursed wilderness rebranded as yuppie real estate — is played for its own quiet dread: whatever made the place dangerous presumably didn't go anywhere just because the road got paved.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Fur-trapper origin, Sasquatch/Wendigo rumors, and the homesteader-to-teenage-squat history established.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed as the site of Dawes's ransom drop; Aspenview Estates redevelopment and Rudy's search of it detailed.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting," decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed the farmhouse's real-world origin — Dave's "Poker Night" group's actual play-through of The Harvesters, including the corrupt Sheriff Ostler left behind with D'Angelo's body, intended at the time as a future Slasher but never resolved by the players. Not adopted into King County canon — a dormant thread Dave wouldn't mind revisiting, not a live one.

docs.title: Brindle, Colorado
docs.text: Based on Delta, CO

docs.title: Hyatt City, Colorado
docs.text:
Based on Cedaredge, CO

Named in an early Google Docs town-naming pass alongside the rest of King County's settlements — not yet used in any drafted scene.

docs.title: Jackson Ridge, Colorado
docs.text:
Based on Durango, Colorado

Correction (2026-07-22): A Google Docs town-naming reference Dave rediscovered has Jackson Ridge based on Durango, not Cedaredge as this page previously said — Cedaredge is actually Hyatt City's basis. The two got swapped somewhere along the way; corrected here to match the source doc.

Confirmed (2026-07-22): Shepherd Junction sits on a street headed toward Jackson Ridge — the real-world basis for the already-established "Shepherd-Jackson Highway" naming. You pass through Red Bridge along the way. Jensen is a separate town to the east, off this route.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Named and given a real-world basis in an early Google Docs town-naming pass — recorded here at the time as Cedaredge.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Corrected to Durango, CO, after rediscovering the source doc and finding Cedaredge actually belongs to Hyatt City. Confirmed as the basis for the Shepherd-Jackson Highway naming.

docs.title: Jensen, Colorado
docs.text:
Based on Montrose, CO

Small town — per The Prodigal Son's Prologue, only a few streetlights mark the intersections of its major roads, and a thick cloud cover can black the place out almost entirely at night. Spelled "Jenson" in that manuscript's prose — not reconciled with the "Jensen" spelling used elsewhere in this vault.

Notable Locations: * Love and Sunshine Daycare * Western Slope Nursing Care

Not the drug-run town, corrected (2026-07-22, decided live): Briefly considered as the site of Moss's drug-run motel (see The Huntsman), but retired — Jensen's based on Montrose, which is too big for what that scene needs. The actual town is meant to be small and run-down, in the vein of Olathe, CO or Wellington, UT — the kind of place you pass through on the way to somewhere that matters. Not yet named; Dave's checking his old Obsidian Portal campaign notes to see if he already logged one. See Development Notes & Unresolved Questions.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Established as a small Montrose-based town within King County, with the daycare and nursing home as notable locations.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Briefly used, then retired, as a candidate for Moss's drug-run town — too big for the intended small, pass-through feel.

docs.title: King County Library
docs.text:
Location: Somewhere in King County (specific town unconfirmed).

Built in 1904, flooded in 1931, burned in 1952, reopened in 1970 — and it still holds all the original books.

Brainstormed for What Wasn't and What Was as a setting-as-character location for the Gothic tone direction.

Discrepancy to reconcile: an earlier pulp-draft brainstorm claimed the library's occult section burned in 1972 ("or so they say") rather than the whole building burning in 1952 per the Gothic draft above.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025-06-05 (Pulpify your Story, pulp pass): Occult-section-burned-1972 detail floated.
  • 2025-06-05 (Pulpify your Story, Gothic pass): Full building history established (built 1904, flooded 1931, burned 1952, reopened 1970), creating the date discrepancy above.
  • 2025-06-05 (Mystery Clues and Emotion): Established as home to Caleb's favorite book, donated by Leanne years ago and never checked out — one of the evidence clues for his true nature.

docs.title: Lucky's Place
docs.text:
Location: Jackson Ridge, Colorado

A dive bar on Main Street. Amber tends bar there.

docs.title: Raven's Creek
docs.text:
Reached via the Hollow Path trail, off an old logging road near Brindle, past a clearing. The county closed the area off years ago after the bridge over the creek rotted out — it's not a place people wander into by accident anymore.

About half a mile past the usual party clearing sits an overgrown ruin — older kids used to tell stories about it being some kind of cult meeting place, never taken seriously until Clara's disappearance.

This is where Clara, Zack, and Ashley followed an unnamed "goth girl" and her crew deeper into the woods the night of the party — the last place Clara was seen before she vanished for good. Not yet investigated by Rudy — planned for the morning after the Humboldt/church confrontation, with Johnny guiding partway and Amber going all the way in.

docs.title: Red Bridge, Colorado
docs.text:
Based on Olathe, CO

A small, run-down pass-through town on the Shepherd-Jackson Highway, between Shepherd Junction (north) and Jackson Ridge (south) — the kind of place nobody stops in on purpose, just somewhere you pass through on the way to Jackson Ridge. Jensen is a separate town east of Jackson Ridge, off this route.

Confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): This is the town from The Huntsman's Near Miss — Christie Reese operates out of an old motel here, and it's where Moss sources drugs, hitching a ride out via LeGrande Station. Resolves the previously-open "drug-run town" question — see Development Notes & Unresolved Questions.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Named and given its real-world basis (Olathe, CO) in an early Google Docs town-naming pass.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed as Moss's drug-run town and Christie Reese's base of operations, resolving a fork left open earlier the same session. See The Huntsman.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session, later same day): Geography pinned down — sits between Shepherd Junction and Jackson Ridge on the Shepherd-Jackson Highway; Jensen is a separate town east of Jackson Ridge, not on this route (an earlier "Shepherd-Jensen Highway" mention was a misremembering, never actually used in the vault).

docs.title: Shepherd Junction, Colorado
docs.text:
Based off Crescent Junction, UT

Basically a truck stop with a street headed toward Jackson Ridge

Geography (confirmed in The Prodigal Son): Sits at the intersection of US Interstate 70 and Colorado Highway 336, locally known as the Shepherd-Jackson Highway. Consists of a handful of single- and double-wide mobile homes, a small railroad siding with its own warehouse, Shepherd's Inn, and LeGrande Station directly across the highway.

Notable Locations: * LeGrande Station * Shepherd's Inn

Notable Inhabitants: * Rudy Williams

docs.title: Stanton City, Colorado
docs.text:
Based on Grand Junction, CO

Sitting comfortably on the Colorado Western Slope, Stanton City is the largest and most populous city of King County, Colorado According to the 2007 King County estimates, the population of the city is 52,662, with the surrounding area housing a population of roughly 103,000 people.

The racial makeup of the city is 91.78% White, 0.60% African American, 0.94% Native American, 0.76% Asian, 0.12% Pacific Islander, 3.81% from other races, and 1.99% from two or more races. Hispanic or Latino of any race were 10.86% of the population.

Atmosphere at night (per The Prodigal Son, Chapter 3): Outside of a 24-hour convenience store on each end of town, a single motel, and Meg's Diner, most businesses close before 10pm — the roads are completely empty by 11pm. Home to a "modern rock" radio station Rudy picks up on his truck's boom-box.

The Four Kings (per "No-Tell Motel narration," full data export): A run-down motel from the 1950s just off I-70 at Stanton City's first exit heading northeast — kept up only to the bare minimum the Health Department requires. Rudy worked its graveyard shift as night clerk in the mid-1990s, freshly (and dishonorably) discharged and living out of a truck-bed camper. A solo-play murder mystery set here (Bannerless Games' No-Tell Motel) generated a cast of noir archetype guests — not treated as confirmed King County canon, just the game's own content.

docs.title: Notable Locations
docs.text:
• Meg's Diner
  • Our Lady of Hope Chapel
  • Cathedral of Saint Paul
  • Empire Dragon Chinese Cuisine

docs.title: Notable Inhabitants
docs.text:
• Mickey Two-Dice
    • Kayla Fawston
  • Silas Freeman

docs.title: Stockton, Colorado
docs.text: Based on Avon / Vail Colorado

docs.title: Love and Sunshine Daycare
docs.text:
Location: Jensen, Colorado

Called "Sunshine and Rainbows Day Care" in The Prodigal Son's Prologue text — presumably the same place, name not reconciled. Western Slope Nursing Care sits in the same lot/right next door; Mickey parks his Cadillac in the unlit space behind the daycare (dark enough, on the night in question, to be "darker than the inside of a coal miner's asshole") before dragging Allen next door into the nursing home.

docs.title: Western Slope Nursing Care
docs.text:
Location: Jensen, Colorado (spelled "Jenson" in The Prodigal Son's manuscript text — not reconciled)

Has a no-locked-doors policy after hours; a bored security guard mans the front desk, more interested in his phone than his job. Nurses do rounds through the night trying to talk patients back to sleep.

The Prologue scene (Book 2): Mickey, Missy, and Harv drag an unconscious man named Allen in from the trunk of Mickey's Cadillac, past the front desk and the rounding nurses — none of whom react, whether through supernatural influence or simple late-night indifference isn't stated. They bring him to Room 35, shared with another patient, Frank, and prop Allen into the room's chair. A young nurse who walks in on them (interrupting mid-task) has her demeanor visibly, supernaturally flattened from alarm to placid the moment Mickey looks at her — he then recruits her: "I've got a job for you." Purpose unstated on the page, but likely connected to the autopsy-table/bathtub construct Mickey and Harv are building in a separate warehouse (see Mickey Two-Dice).

docs.title: LeGrande Station
docs.text:
Location: Shepherd Junction, Colorado Owner: Nathan Hale Employees: Jenn-with-two-Ns

Old train station turned 50s throwback gas station and diner, complete with all the gaudy neon lights and chrome you can imagine. Red-and-chrome glass entrance doors, black-and-white tiled floor, glass-fronted fridges, Beach Boys usually playing over the speakers — bright and clean enough that Rudy finds the sensory overload gives him a headache. Sells sandwiches, burgers, and shakes through a faux-vintage '50s deli/soda counter in addition to gas and convenience-store basics.

Nathan Hale wants to expand it into something like a Buccee's — a notable stop on I-70 — which puts him at odds with Rudy, who'd rather Shepherd Junction stay a little nowhere you grab gas and a drink before moving on. Part of that plan involves buying and bulldozing Shepherd's Inn across the street.

Social mechanic — anonymous vs. nosy, confirmed (2026-07-16, decided live — from "Westernizing What Wasn't"): LeGrande Station is deliberately the anonymous and exploitable pole of Shepherd Junction's social geography — clean, bright, corporate-feeling, the kind of place a stranger passes through without anyone remembering their face. This is a direct contrast with Shepherd's Inn across the street, which is deliberately the nosy and protective pole — regulars notice when something's off and look out for each other. The physical layout (two roadside stops facing each other) does double duty as a thematic one: commerce vs. community, anonymous vs. watched-over.

Trucker showers, confirmed (2026-07-22, decided live): Has shower facilities, standard for a highway truck stop. This is also the anonymous departure point Moss uses to hitch a ride from a trucker heading toward Red Bridge on drug runs (see The Huntsman) — nobody at LeGrande Station remembers faces, which is exactly why it works for that and not Shepherd's Inn.

Timeline, resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Hale purchased and renovated the station six years before the present-day events of The Prodigal Son — a deliberate compromise between that manuscript's "three years" and the "roughly ten years" feud framing established elsewhere (see Nathan Hale). Dave's own call: the exact figure isn't important enough to chase down further.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Established as owned by Nathan Hale, employing Jenn-with-two-Ns.
  • 2025-06-04 (Escalating Tensions Rewrite): Hale's expansion ambitions and the feud with Rudy established.
  • 2026-07-14 (The Prodigal Son manuscript shared): Interior/atmosphere details added; the "three years prior" purchase timeline surfaced, in tension with the "ten years" feud framing elsewhere.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Timeline conflict resolved — six years, splitting the difference.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, from "Westernizing What Wasn't"): Confirmed as the anonymous/exploitable pole of Shepherd Junction's social geography, deliberately contrasted with Shepherd's Inn's nosy/protective pole.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed shower facilities, and confirmed as Moss's anonymous departure point for hitching rides to Red Bridge on drug runs. See Jamie and The Huntsman.

docs.title: Shepherd's Inn
docs.text:
Based on the Roadhouse from Supernatural, and The Garage on Beck Street

Located in Shepherd Junction, Colorado Owned by Rudy Williams

Shepherd's Inn is a rundown roadhouse, serving as both Rudy's home base and unofficial hub for anyone seeking supernatural help.

There is an old Winnebago and old cab-over camper parked out back.

Despite the name, the Inn has no rooms for rent.

Due to the appearance of its clientele and its beat up nature, the road house doesn't get much business from people that don't already know what it is.

Predates LeGrande Station across the street by at least 30 years. Nathan Hale sees it as a blight bringing down property values and wants to buy and bulldoze it to expand his truck stop; Rudy sees LeGrande Station's chrome-and-neon theme as an eyesore right back — but still buys his cigarettes there. Truth be told, Hale's not entirely wrong — it genuinely is a bit of a blight, not just NIMBY bias on his part. Specific complaints Hale has raised: scrap metal piled up behind the bar, two broken windows, and Rudy's truck hasn't moved in six months. Hale's stake in it isn't purely aesthetic — a county tourism grant for a "neon cowboy" welcome sign he wants to build is jeopardized every time the county inspector sees the Inn's condition.

Legal immunity from code enforcement (per The Prodigal Son): The Inn has never been inspected and never will be — a personal favor from a county official (almost certainly Executive Dawes) grateful to Rudy for saving his son's life. This explains a mechanic in the Hale feud: Hale can't just report the place and have the county force a shutdown or fine, which is presumably part of why he's stuck trying to buy Rudy out instead.

The immunity is temporary, confirmed (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): Rudy loses it at the end of Book 2 — by Book 6 he's back on the grid, dealing with cops, a health inspector, and other bureaucratic distractions Mickey either engineers or exploits. See Book Order and Series Structure.

Interior (per The Prodigal Son): Small — a single bar with room for two or three, two standing tables in the middle, four more against the walls. A Twilight Zone pinball machine sits in the corner next to a jukebox stocked with outlaw country and classic rock ("Pretend I Never Happened" by Waylon Jennings gets a direct mention). A grey plastic bus tub for dirty glasses.
