Regulars include local oil-field workers stopping for one drink after their shift, and Gil, an old biker who closes out most nights with "one more for the road."

Mounted antlers hang above the front door.

The door bell (undecided, per editorial notes on The Prodigal Son manuscript): Dave was actively deciding between two options when drafting — a tacky old spring-mounted bell above the door, or just a handful of jingle bells tied on with baling twine. Not settled; either fits the "cheap, worn, functional" character of the place.

Unconfirmed detail (from a discarded draft scene, not settled canon): a rug inside, made by Rudy's mother.

Succession thread (context incomplete): Rudy's will apparently addresses who inherits the Inn — see the "mysterious successor" note on Rudy Williams and Nathan Hale.

Social mechanic — nosy vs. anonymous, confirmed (2026-07-16, decided live — from "Westernizing What Wasn't"): Shepherd's Inn is deliberately the nosy and protective pole of Shepherd Junction's social geography — regulars notice when something's off, ask about it, and look out for each other, which is exactly why it works as a hub for people who need supernatural help. This is a direct contrast with LeGrande Station across the street, which is deliberately the anonymous and exploitable pole — clean, bright, corporate-feeling, the kind of place a stranger can pass through without anyone remembering their face. The same physical layout (two roadside stops facing each other) does double duty as a thematic one: watched-over vs. anonymous, community vs. commerce.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Established as Rudy's home base and roadhouse.
  • 2025-06-04 (Escalating Tensions Rewrite): Feud with Nathan Hale over the property established; antlers over the door and the "genuinely a bit of a blight" admission added; unconfirmed mother's-rug detail logged from a discarded draft scene.
  • 2025-06-04 (Escalating Tensions Rewrite, full transcript): Specific blight complaints (scrap metal, broken windows, unmoved truck) and Hale's neon-cowboy tourism-grant motivation added.
  • 2025-06-02/03 (Supernatural Horror Publishers): Succession/inheritance thread cross-linked — context incomplete.
  • 2026-07-14 (The Prodigal Son manuscript shared): Legal code-enforcement immunity and interior/atmosphere details (pinball machine, jukebox, regulars) established.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, from "Westernizing What Wasn't"): Confirmed as the nosy/protective pole of Shepherd Junction's social geography, deliberately contrasted with LeGrande Station's anonymous/exploitable pole.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed the code-enforcement immunity is lost at the end of Book 2, and that the cab-over camper out back is specifically Moss's, not Rudy's. See Book Order and Series Structure and Jamie.

docs.title: Empire Dragon Chinese Cuisine
docs.text:
Run down Chinese food restaurant.

Also where The Fat Man does business.

Detail confirmed in The Prodigal Son, Chapter 3: Closes before 10pm most nights, neon "Open" sign usually dark by the time anyone with real business shows up. The back alley shared with neighboring restaurants reeks of rancid deep-fryer grease and old leftovers — cordoned-off waste barrels, overflowing dumpsters, split garbage bags. Kitchen staffed by at least two young cooks (unnamed, one notably short-tempered) who speak Mandarin to each other and don't react much to strange requests. The dining room is dark hardwood, decorated with wall scrolls depicting the Chinese zodiac and warriors battling serpentine dragons, thick with smoke and fryer smell drifting from the kitchen. Silas holds court alone in a booth built for six, behind a bamboo-and-paper divider at the back — see Silas Freeman for his contact method (a fortune cookie with a custom, handwritten fortune).

docs.title: Meg's Diner
docs.text:
Location: Stanton City, Colorado

The greasiest of greasy spoons, Meg’s serves exactly what you’d expect — 24 hour breakfast, bottomless coffee, and portion sizes big enough to make your eyes water.

Meg’s was founded by Meg Townsley near the end of World War 2 with life insurance money she received after her late husband, Mel, was gunned down by Germans. Meg ran the place until her own death in 1982, doing everything from cooking to serving to bussing tables.

After her passing, Meg’s closed for about a year while several interested parties tried to work out the details of purchasing the location, and in November of ’83, Chance Dayton – a lifelong regular of Megs – reopened the doors, leaving absolutely nothing unchanged from the way Meg left it.

Over the years, the kitchen has been upgraded, and the building has been retrofitted with many modern amenities, but the recipes and overall feel of the diner hasn’t changed.

docs.title: Our Lady of Hope Chapel
docs.text:
Located: Stanton City, Colorado

Our Lady of Hope Chapel was once the primary religious location of Stanton City, where it housed a Catholic priest, but also allowed for many other denominations of Christian faiths to practice and to hold services. Over the years, the other faiths built or bought their own churches, or simply fell away, while the Catholic population grew.

In 1985, the Cathedral of Saint Paul was built to provide a location for the Catholic masses to be held, and Our Lady of Hope’s congregation slowly dwindled, until it officially closed it’s doors in 1986.

The Catholic Church still owns the building and land, though it has gone unused for a quarter-century. It’s windows have been boarded up, and the doors chained, to prevent squatters, but anyone that has been in Stanton City for more than a few months can tell you that not even vagabonds will enter that building.

docs.title: Spanish Dolls
docs.text:
A cross-country hunter-network nickname for a decades-long string of forensically-inconsistent look-alike bodies — Kayla's fleshcraft/bonecraft attempts at sculpting other people into perfect copies of herself. Identical in gross appearance (scars, moles, general shape reproduced as closely as possible) but always forensically inconsistent on close inspection — different fingerprints, dental records, mismatched implant serial numbers.

The name itself: Dave's own deliberate detail, flagged explicitly as an in-world ethnic-stereotype naming convention among hunters, not an authorial slip — Kayla is Brazilian, and the hunter network calling the pattern "Spanish" anyway is meant to read as characteristic hunter-culture carelessness: "brown is brown, Mexican is Spanish, and so stereotypes go into naming things."

Why the pattern stayed hidden so long: Wide geographic and temporal dispersion — Mickey and Kayla have historically kept moving, so no two Dolls have ever turned up close together in place or time.

Origin location: The earliest known Doll cases were near a fictional city called Castle Dell, Georgia — the same Castle Dell where Mickey's human self, Michael Turino, was later committed to an asylum (see Mickey Two-Dice). Likely not a coincidence.

Why Dolls don't become "passengers" like Missy, Harv, and Kayla: The identity-erasure and torture the Dolls undergo leaves them psychologically shattered ("Gnawed") by the time they're embraced — no coherent self left for a soul to anchor to. When Mickey diablerizes a Doll, the soul dissolves completely, unlike victims (Missy, Harv, Kayla) who retained enough identity to persist inside him.

Same mechanism used once defensively, confirmed (2026-07-22, per "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): During a Blood Hunt called on him at Castle Dell, Georgia, Mickey used this exact "Gnawed" process on an innocent bystander instead of a Kayla-lookalike victim — breaking their mind with Dementation, fleshcrafting them into a physical copy of himself, then Embracing and releasing them as a feral decoy. The Camarilla hunted and killed the decoy, ending the Blood Hunt. Same underlying process as the Dolls, just aimed at making a copy of Mickey instead of Kayla. See Mickey Two-Dice.

Book 1 seeding, confirmed as the established Gil (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Gil mentions the Dolls to Rudy in passing at Shepherd's Inn, as one of many odd hunter-network rumors — Rudy dismisses it without following up. This is the same Gil already established as a Shepherd's Inn regular — his "armchair Hunter" characterization (knows the lore and the talk, has never done the work himself, but stays plugged into that world) fits perfectly with passing along a rumor like this one.

Confirmed future plot beat (not yet placed in a specific book): Dave wants two Dolls eventually created and killed by Mickey in the same county — one diablerized, one killed mundanely — with the significant detail being proximity: the first time two Dolls turn up clustered together, possibly the first time in the same state. That clustering would signal to the wider hunter network that "the Dollmaker settled down," putting King County on outside hunters' radar for the first time.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025 ("Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Full concept introduced — the naming convention, Castle Dell origin, the soul-anchoring mechanic explaining why Dolls don't persist as passengers, and the confirmed future two-Dolls-in-one-county plot beat.
  • 2025 ("Bridging Story Gaps," full data export): Origin chat for the Book 1 seeding beat — "a hunter at Shepherd's Inn" mentions the Boulder Doll to Rudy. Also the source of Kayla and Mickey's confirmed dialogue about fleshcraft mastery ("you never paint all the way to the edge of the canvas") and the "Angel lets me [play with your face]" exchange.
  • 2026-07-16 (decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed the seeding hunter is the established Gil, not a separate unnamed contact.
  • 2026-07-22 (integrated from "King County Chronicles ChatGPT consolidatation"): Confirmed the same "Gnawed" mechanism was used once defensively, to fleshcraft/Embrace a decoy copy of Mickey himself during a Blood Hunt at Castle Dell. See Mickey Two-Dice.

docs.title: Supernatural Influence and Dynamics
docs.text:
• Quiet Presence: Unlike more active supernatural hotbeds, King County’s ecosystem remains relatively subdued. Entities like Mickey and Kayla seem to recognize the danger of drawing attention, and others like Doc maintain a transient existence, keeping to the fringes.

  • Confirmed county-wide population breakdown (per "Vampire Population Ratio," full data export — Dave's own numbers, not just assistant estimates):

  • Humans: ~200,000 total across the county (Stanton City-analog ~160,000, Montrose-analog ~20,000, Delta-analog ~15,000, Olathe-analog ~5,000).
  • Vampires: 7 total — 5 pre-existing and unnamed, plus Mickey and Kayla.
  • Werewolves: Effectively zero territorial pack presence — the Western Slope is deliberately a Werewolf: the Forsaken-style "no-man's-land," inhospitable to werewolves for reasons still undecided, which is why Doc being there at all makes him "a huge anomaly." This supersedes any earlier "1–3 werewolves" style estimate.
  • Changelings: Moss confirmed. One additional drifter changeling was floated but explicitly hedged by Dave as maybe not part of the ecosystem — not firmly adopted, treat as an open possibility only.
  • Mages: Phoebe confirmed as a full Mage, but institutionalized and functionally removed from the power structure — the county has no active arcane oversight as a result.
  • Hunters: Rudy is the one aware, active hunter in the county.
  • Department of Truth: Explicitly has no meaningful presence within the county (per Dave's direct instruction), though it still exists in the wider setting and may resurface later.

  • Unconfirmed, brainstorm-only material not adopted: an assistant-proposed King County vampire court structure (Prince/Sheriff/Harpy roles, domain-by-highway-corridor territories) was offered at length but never named, endorsed, or engaged with by Dave beyond the headcount above — treat all of it as raw option-menu material, not canon, unless Dave revisits it. Note: the "Madness Network" analog concept was actually raised by Dave himself, conditionally ("especially if King County has a Madness Network analog") — a hypothetical he floated, not something the assistant invented and Dave ignored, though it's still not a firm adoption either way.

  • Confirmed dramatic irony — Rudy believes King County has no vampires: Dave's own framing: "I'm sure the local vampires are aware of Rudy, and do enough to stay off his radar. Every so often one doesn't succeed at that and Rudy offs them, and that's enough to keep him believing there are no vampires in King County." Mechanically, local vampires treat Rudy as a known hazard and avoid his notice; the rare vampire who doesn't (usually a nomadic drifter off I-70/rail) gets killed by Rudy, and each such kill reinforces his false belief rather than undermining it — he reads outliers as proof of absence. His hatred of vampires specifically (as opposed to general tolerance of other supernatural weirdness in the county) is rooted in The California Incident, not general monster-hunting zeal. The confirmed payoff: King County actually harbors one of the most dangerous vampires Rudy will ever face (Mickey, post-diablerie of the returned Abyss-touched vampire — see Mickey Two-Dice), and the reveal is meant to land hard precisely because of how long Rudy's spent believing otherwise.

  • Unresolved Conflicts: Doc’s arrival and his past with Rudy create potential for confrontation. His reason for seeking Rudy’s help is unknown, but their shared history and the trauma of their last encounter add tension. Similarly, Mickey’s presence in Stanton City raises the possibility of conflict, as a Blood Hunt might attract unwanted attention.

  • Silas’s Prophecies: Silas’s psychic abilities place him in a unique position of influence, allowing him to see glimpses of King County’s future. Though human, his powers make him a valuable resource for Rudy or others aware of the supernatural, provided they earn his trust.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• (pre-existing, date unknown): Core "quiet ecosystem" framing, Doc/Rudy tension, and Silas's role established.
  • 2025 ("Vampire Population Ratio," full data export): Confirmed county-wide population breakdown, the "no werewolves" Western Slope rule, and the "Rudy believes King County has no vampires" dramatic-irony mechanic and its payoff.

docs.title: Hybrid Setting Framework
docs.text:
King County deliberately blends World of Darkness (WoD, classic lines like Vampire: The Masquerade, Werewolf: The Apocalypse, Mage: The Ascension) with Chronicles of Darkness (CoD, the modernized lines: Requiem, Forsaken, Awakening) rather than picking one. This explains why the vault already mixes terminology from both across Lore/World of Darkness Concepts (e.g. Malkavian Clan and Blood Hunt from classic Vampire, alongside Awakening and Mysterium from Mage: The Awakening).

Base approach: Use CoD as the mechanical backbone (streamlined rules, Conditions/Beats/Integrity) while plugging in classic WoD lore and cosmology as needed.

Fusion pillars (brainstormed correspondences, not all confirmed as canon): - God(s): The God-Machine (CoD) as a shell/mechanism left behind or hijacked by greater entities like the Wyrm or Weaver (WoD). - Underworld: Layered — CoD's bureaucratic Underworld (Geist) on top, WoD's Oblivion/Tempests below, with old Spectres seeping upward. - Spirit Worlds: Hisil (CoD) as a localized spirit reflection, with the Umbra (WoD) as a broader metaphysical cosmos beyond it, accessible via deeper Hisil zones. - Vampires: Requiem as the default (no Caine, local myths), with Masquerade's Cainite mythos (Caine, Jyhad, Gehenna) reduced to just one powerful Blood God's cult rather than universal truth. - Mages: Awakening's Watchtowers/Lie/Exarchs as default, with the Exarchs framed as what became of the Ascension-era Technocracy, and the Watchtowers as rewritten Spheres. - Werewolves: Forsaken as default ("the ones who stayed behind"), with Apocalypse-style Garou framed as returning after a banishment (or vice versa). - Ghosts/Spirits/Demons: Wraith: The Oblivion as "deep" ghosts, Geist: Sin-Eaters for surface-level present-day ghostly concerns; Demon: The Fallen and Demon: The Descent as two aspects of the same metaphysical rebellion (the Fallen are old Descenters who stayed behind).

Candidate in-world justifications for why both cosmologies coexist (none confirmed as chosen for King County specifically): 1. The Shatter — a recent cosmological event, or the slow failure of the God-Machine, lets deep Umbra leak into the Hisil; the Gauntlet weakens, old gods return, Cainean vampires wake, and the Ascension War was only the beginning. 2. The Lie is Fraying — Mage: Awakening's "Lie" starts collapsing, and old-WoD truths (Caine, Antediluvians, Apocalypse prophecies) seep into the vacuum; the Exarchs and the Wyrm turn out not to be so different. 3. Multiple Realities — the Chronicles world is a rebuilt copy of the original WoD world (the God-Machine's reconstruction after a "Time of Judgment"), and something got loose from the old version.

Character creation: Use CoD mechanics, but let players pick Origins and Belief Systems from either line.

Supernatural template blending, additional specifics: - Vampires: Requiem Clans/Covenants as default, with Masquerade's Camarilla/Sabbat included as rival political orders — possibly remnants from a previous age. Most vampires follow local origin stories rather than the Cainite myth. - Werewolves: Forsaken mechanics as base, with Apocalypse tribes and spiritual enemies folded in as legacy spirits or "old blood" — e.g. the Silent Striders reimagined as exiled Forsaken with deeper umbral ties. - Mages: Mechanically Awakening, with the Ascension War as setting history and the Technocracy reframed as an ancient, failed attempt to control the Lie.

Implementation notes: Start local (a single region where the bleed-through is strongest — presumably King County itself, given its established "no-man's-land" reputation) and build localized myths for why a given faction is there (e.g. "the Camarilla exists here because of something specific to the area"); write with mystery rather than over-explaining; draw themes from both lines (WoD: epic tragedy, cyclical destruction, cosmic horror; CoD: intimate horror, surveillance, entropy, moral decay); consider cross-line factions (a Banisher/Wyrm-corrupted pack, a Kindred cult of the Abyss tied to old Tremere, a Sin-Eater who hears Oblivion's voice).

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text: • 2025-05-28 (WoD CoD Hybrid Setting): Framework and fusion pillars established as a general design philosophy; specific in-world justification (Shatter/Lie/Multiple Realities) not yet chosen. Additional per-template specifics (Camarilla/Sabbat, Silent Striders, Technocracy) and the character-creation note added on a second pass.

docs.title: King County Cosmology Framework
docs.text:
Confirmed by Dave (per "Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export) as the loose, deliberately-unofficial mapping this setting uses to unify concepts across different World of Darkness game lines, since there's no White Wolf license: "the same, but different," loosely unified rather than mechanically precise.

Confirmed mappings: - Astral plane = High Umbra (Werewolf: the Apocalypse/Forsaken terms borrowed for a Mage-flavored "high" spirit realm). - The Abyss and the Underworld = Low Umbra. - Arcadia is its own separate thing, but also functions as the dream realm in this setting's cosmology — not folded into the Umbra structure at all.

Confirmed tone guardrail (Dave's own words, worth preserving verbatim rather than paraphrasing): The wound/scar/tear underneath King County is cosmic in scale, but the series is explicitly not meant to become Cosmic Horror in tone as a result for the earlier books — a deliberate genre guardrail, not an oversight. Correction (2026-07-16, fresh-eyes pass on the full "Werewolves and Spirit Worlds" export): this guardrail applies to the early books, not the whole series — Dave separately and explicitly confirmed Book 5 itself is cosmic horror ("Book 5 is definitely cosmic horror," see Book Order and Series Structure) in the same chat. What actually got discarded from that chat was the Book 3 Fetch/"Rusting Place"-rescue plot and Rudy's memory-immunity trait (see Aaron Mabey) — not the Book 5 cosmic-horror escalation itself. The previous version of this note conflated the two and linked to a nonexistent "[[Werewolves and Spirit Worlds]]" page; fixed to point at where that material actually lives.

Confirmed county-specific application: Mickey is aware of the Astral plane (and is confirmed to eventually gain enough Auspex to project into it, using this against Phoebe) despite having zero awareness of the Hedge — the two realms are treated as genuinely separate tracks of supernatural knowledge, not a single unified "the supernatural" a character either knows about or doesn't.

Standing rule — mechanics are bookkeeping, not lore (reaffirmed 2026-07-22, decided live): Dave tracks game-mechanical detail (Discipline names and dot ratings for Mickey, Contract/power roles for Moss in The Huntsman, etc.) purely to keep character capability internally honest and consistent — none of it is ever meant to surface on the page by name. The books read as a Hunter/noir story on the surface; the underlying game logic stays entirely invisible to the reader.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025 ("Civilian Rifle Equivalents," full data export): Framework established in full — the Astral/Umbra/Abyss/Arcadia mapping, the explicit cosmic-scale-but-not-cosmic-horror tone rule, and Mickey's specific Astral-vs.-Hedge awareness split.
  • 2026-07-16 (fresh-eyes pass, "Werewolves and Spirit Worlds," full data export): Corrected the tone-guardrail note above — it had conflated the discarded Book 3 Fetch/Rusting-Place plot with Book 5's cosmic horror identity, which Dave confirmed rather than discarded. Also fixed a dangling wikilink.
  • 2026-07-22 (decided live, grill-canon session): Reaffirmed the standing "mechanics are bookkeeping, not lore" rule explicitly, after it came up again for Mickey's Discipline levels.

docs.title: Arcadia
docs.text:
The fae realm. Not a vacuum — it's obsessed with mirroring, mocking, and manipulating the real world. The Gentry steal people, ideas, language, objects, and culture, not to preserve them, but to repurpose them for their own alien aesthetics.

"Arcadia is like Hell with a scrapbook fetish: it doesn't just steal from the real world, it remixes it in the worst way possible."

Time is warped there — a Changeling can spend centuries in Arcadia while barely aging, and glimpses of the real world can filter in asynchronously (a cracked mirror, a bleeding television set, secondhand stories filtered through fae nonsense).

Jamie was taken into Arcadia early enough in life to not remember life before, and was held there for centuries.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text: • 2025-06-05 (Omniscient POV vs Alienation): Established while working out how Jamie could recognize mundane real-world objects despite centuries away.

docs.title: Keeper
docs.text:
The fae captor/owner of a Changeling in Arcadia.

Jamie's Keeper is not yet named or detailed — only that their domain included twisted reflections of modern towns, which may explain how Jamie recognizes real-world things despite centuries away. Open thread for future development.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text: • 2025-06-05 (Omniscient POV vs Alienation): Concept introduced in passing while explaining how Jamie might recognize mundane objects; no specifics established yet.

docs.title: The Hedge
docs.text:
The thorny boundary between Arcadia and the mortal world. Changelings cross it to escape/return.

Contains the crook-path, a tangle of time-rotted hedge-thorns wrapped in glinting iron — where The Huntsman comes from.

Brainstormed as a metaphor for grief in What Wasn't and What Was — tangled, consuming, filled with memories that aren't yours anymore.

Naming note, resolved (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session): The Mystery Crime Thriller alternate outline called this same kind of place "the Hollow" instead — that outline is now confirmed non-canon (see Development Notes & Unresolved Questions), so "the Hedge" is the sole established name; "the Hollow" is retired terminology, not a distinct sub-location.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text:
• 2025-06-05 (Pulpify your Story): Introduced as what Jamie fled through, pursued by The Huntsman.
  • 2025-06-05 (Omniscient POV vs Alienation): Crook-path detail established.

docs.title: Lunacy
docs.text:
The standard Werewolf: the Apocalypse/Forsaken concept that mortals can't hold onto the truth of having seen a werewolf shift — this setting's version leans on real-world folklore rather than pure game mechanics.

Belief-based reframing (per "Werewolves and Spirit Worlds," full data export): Folkloric precedent exists outside White Wolf's own cosmology — classical "lunatic" derives from lunar-madness beliefs (Roman/Greek); Celtic and Slavic tales hold that witnessing a shapeshifter's true transformation can cause memory loss, illness, or spiritual damage; Slavic Rusalka-adjacent taboos say speaking of what you saw invites forgetting or a curse, and you're only vulnerable if you believed in it long enough to remember it in the first place. The reframe this suggests, not yet adopted as settled canon: Lunacy might not be a werewolf power at all — it could be a side effect of belief systems colliding, which would fold it directly into this setting's established collective-belief mechanics (see What Wasn't and What Was for the clearest existing example of belief governing what persists and what fades).

Status: Offered as a thematically clean option during open Book 3+ brainstorming that Dave later set aside without picking it up ("I don't need to figure Book 3 out until Book 2 is done") — not rejected outright the way the Fetch/Rusting-Place plot was, just untouched. Worth revisiting if a future book needs Lunacy to actually function on the page.

docs.title: Timeline
docs.text: • 2025 ("Werewolves and Spirit Worlds," full data export): Folklore sourcing and the belief-based reframing floated during speculative Book 3+ brainstorming; not picked up or rejected, just left on the table.

docs.title: Rudy Williams
docs.text:
• Mystery of the Vampire's Portal: Does the portal the vampire attempted to open in California have any lingering connection to King County, Colorado? Could it tie into the region's reputation as a supernatural no-man's-land? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Yes — King County's fracture is the same ancient wound the Vampire's ritual briefly tore open at a different, more recent point in California. Not caused by the 1986 incident; King County's weak point is much older. See King County, Colorado and The Vampire.
  • Fate of the Monster Squad Members: Are there clues in King County, Colorado about what really happened to the other members? Were their deaths connected, and if so, how? Partially resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Horace's fate is confirmed — mundane, adult death from pneumonia, unconnected to the case, deliberately honoring the actor who played him. Sean's fate is deliberately left open. Neither was actually hunted; both drifted from Rudy over his own growing obsession, not an external threat. See The Monster Squad (group).
  • Phoebe’s Condition: Is Phoebe's catatonic state purely psychological, or could it have supernatural causes? Does her early Awakening hint at a hidden power that could still be accessed?
  • Rudy’s Role in King County’s Supernatural Dynamics: How much does Rudy know about the supernatural “ecosystem” of King County, Colorado, and is he considered a respected hunter among others in the know? Could his presence be drawing attention, either from allies or enemies?
  • Unseen Forces: Who or what might still be hunting Rudy? Is there a specific supernatural force (a vampire faction, cult, or creature) tracking him?
  • Reputation Among the Supernatural: How much do supernatural beings know or care about Rudy’s presence? While he has a strong reputation among locals and hunters, supernatural creatures may have a different perception or even recognize him as a potential threat.
  • Secrets of Shepherd’s Inn: Are there any unexplored supernatural phenomena within Shepherd's Inn or the area surrounding it? This could potentially tie into the area’s general no-man’s-land status, hinting at lingering energy or a dormant power.
  • Network Expansion and Future Alliances: Could Rudy expand his network further, perhaps involving supernatural allies or factions who would benefit from his connections? His contacts are mostly human, but the county’s nature might open doors to surprising new alliances.

docs.title: Phoebe
docs.text:
• Her actual published name: Sticking with "Phoebe" (reaffirmed 2026-07-22, grill-canon session — still not necessarily the final locked answer for the finished manuscript, just not worth re-litigating again right now). "Ashley" remains retired from working use (see Ashley naming collision).
  • Cause of Catatonia: Is Phoebe’s condition solely due to psychological trauma, or could a supernatural force be suppressing her consciousness? Could the ritual with the portal have left a lingering effect that holds her in this state?
  • Lucid Episodes: What triggers her rare moments of lucidity? Are they connected to King County, Colorado’s supernatural no-man’s-land status, and could they contain clues about her potential Awakening?
  • Possible Future Role: If she were to regain her full abilities, would Phoebe become a powerful ally, or might her traumatic experiences make her a threat to herself and others?
  • Connection to the No-Man's-Land: Is Phoebe's condition, or her presence in King County, Colorado, linked to the reason why supernatural creatures avoid the area? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Yes — resonance, not coincidence. Her Awakening put her in direct contact with the same ancient wound that runs under King County; something in her still carries that contact, which is why her lucidity correlates with local supernatural fluctuations. See Phoebe. Still open: Could she play a central role in resolving or revealing the area's mystery down the line?
  • Impact of the Promethean's Return: If The Promethean is still alive and returns, could this reunion with Phoebe trigger a release of her consciousness or aid in her recovery? Return mechanism/timing deliberately left open (2026-07-22) — revisit once that's decided.
  • Promethean's Influence on Phoebe's Awakening: Did The Promethean's presence accelerate or influence her magical Awakening? Still open — the bond itself is confirmed real (see below), but not this specific causal question.
  • Subconscious Memory and Manifestations: Does Phoebe retain subconscious memories of The Promethean? Her brief moments of lucidity might be connected to echoes of their bond, manifesting as vague feelings or glimpses of his protective presence. Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Real, active psychic link, not just subconscious echo — Astral (High Umbra) and Abyss (Low Umbra) are adjacent layers of the Umbra, and the trauma bond from the ritual bridges the rest. See Phoebe and The Promethean.

docs.title: The Monster Squad (group)
docs.text:
• True Purpose of the Portal: Was the vampire's goal simply to open a portal to the Underworld, or was there a specific entity or purpose for summoning this dark energy? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Pure Abyss-obsession, true Lasombra fashion — not power, not service to a greater entity, not ritual duty. He meant to drag the Abyss through into the town to engulf it, not to open a door and step through. See The Vampire. Still open: Does it have any connection to King County, Colorado's supernatural aura? — see the still-open item below.
  • Cause of the Squad Members' Deaths: Were the deaths of the other Squad members orchestrated by a supernatural force, or could other factions be involved? Partially resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Not orchestrated, at least for Horace (mundane pneumonia) and Sean (fate deliberately left open, but the estrangement from Rudy was personality conflict, not a threat). See The Monster Squad (group).
  • Scary German Guy's Whereabouts: What happened to him, and could he still be an ally? Deliberately left open (2026-07-22, grill-canon session) — not being pushed for an answer now.
  • Legacy and Influence: Are there other individuals or factions aware of the Monster Squad’s original activities? Could there be others who wish to either uphold or destroy the legacy of what the group did?

docs.title: United States Marine Corps
docs.text:
• Classified Mission Incident: The mission that led to Rudy Williams's discharge remains a point of mystery. Could this incident be connected to supernatural forces, perhaps even tied to what he experienced as part of The Monster Squad (group)? Resolved (pre-existing, cross-referenced 2026-07-22, grill-canon session): Fully detailed on Rudy Williams's own page — Task Force VALKYRIE recruited him specifically because of his California-incident exposure. His unit was slaughtered by Doc Mabey's First Change; Rudy went AWOL hunting him for six months, had him in his sights, couldn't take the shot, and turned himself in. Dishonorably discharged. Doc is confirmed still alive/at large for a future book — that's the one genuinely open thread here, not a contradiction.
  • Ties to Military and Other Hunters: Rudy’s Marine background might draw the attention of other hunters or ex-military personnel in the supernatural world. Are there other former Marines or government operatives aware of the supernatural who could cross his path?
  • Military Training Adaptations: Has Rudy made specific adaptations to his Marine training to handle supernatural threats? This could include unconventional weapons, knowledge of supernatural vulnerabilities, or traps designed for creatures rather than humans.

docs.title: Scary German Guy
docs.text:
• Current Whereabouts: Where did Scary German Guy go after the events in California? Is he still alive, and if so, does he remain connected to the supernatural world? Deliberately left open (2026-07-22, grill-canon session) — not being pushed for an answer now.
  • Knowledge of the Portal: He may know more about the vampire’s motivations and the nature of the portal, possibly even understanding why King County, Colorado is a no-man’s-land. If contacted, he could shed light on the area’s supernatural properties.
  • Interest in Phoebe: As the person who helped harness Phoebe’s magical potential, he may have a personal interest in her Awakening or a sense of responsibility for her current state. If he’s aware of her condition, he might be willing to assist Rudy Williams, but his approach would likely be pragmatic rather than sentimental.
  • Potential Links to Other Factions: Scary German Guy could have connections to groups like the Mysterium, a werewolf pack, or even Prometheans, given his knowledge of flesh golems. These ties could prove useful to Rudy if he learns how to leverage them.

docs.title: What Wasn't and What Was — Which Outline Is Primary?
docs.text: Five outlines now exist for this book: the original Act 1.1–3.6 outline (Rudy living in a Winnebago, sober and controlled, Leanne comes to the Inn, a fuller and warmer resolution); the Detective Noir alternate outline; the Character-Driven alternate outline; the Mystery Crime Thriller alternate outline; and the Save the Cat alternate outline. Which (or what blend) is the real plan for the book? Resolved (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session): The original Act 1.1–3.6 outline is the primary, canonical plan for the book. The four alternates (Detective Noir, Character-Driven, Mystery Crime Thriller, Save the Cat) were a drafting exercise — running the story through the Novel Factory tool's genre-specific templates — not competing creative visions or parallel drafts. They're retired to non-canon status except for the pieces Dave separately and explicitly confirmed as real regardless of outline (the second-missing-kid addition from the Mystery Crime Thriller pass, now on the main What Wasn't and What Was page; the "belief/memory/truth, not violence, neutralizes the Huntsman" mechanic — see the cascading resolution below). Where an alternate's specific mechanism (motel room, kidnapping, a given ending) conflicts with the main outline, the main outline wins.

docs.title: Rye / Jamie
docs.text:
• Rye's Fate: Did Rye survive the train accident, die from it, or come back changed (Hollowed or partway Lost, per Changeling: The Lost terms)? Deliberately left unanswered — the user declined to develop it further when offered. Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Dead — killed protecting Moss from the Huntsman, which was disguised as the "railroad bull" that caused the train-yard accident. Deliberately confirmed to give the Huntsman a real, proven kill before the book starts, reinforcing it as a genuine threat and not just Rudy's red herring. See Rye and The Huntsman. Prologue Discrepancy (now three accounts): Jamie/Moss's Hedge-exit has been described three different ways across three chats: (1) a quiet, solo field/barn/truck escape; (2) a chaotic dawn arrival alone, with sirens and someone bleeding; (3) escaping the Hedge around age 12 with a group of fellow Changelings (the only survivor), then found bleeding on a King County back road and rescued by Rudy himself from a Huntsman. Same event told differently across sessions, different moments in Jamie's life, or genuine contradictions to reconcile — not resolved. Resolved (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session): All three are the same event, and Dave filled in the full mechanism directly. Jamie was stolen as an infant/toddler, held in Arcadia for subjectively hundreds of years, aged only as far as needed for labor — other Changelings passing through eventually named the abuse for what it was, and it took decades more to find and commit to a Hedge opening. The escape was solo, not with a group. On exiting, disoriented by real sensation for the first time, Jamie hid in a barn (visible along with the trailer park from the exit point), was working up the nerve to run for the trailer park when the Huntsman blocked that path, and ran the opposite way instead. Rudy — leaving Tina's place at sunup while she still slept — didn't clock the danger until he caught the Huntsman's "man in a hat" form in his rearview mirror; he U-turned, threw open the passenger door, and shouted "Get in!," with the dust and sunrise backlighting revealing the Huntsman's true bear-and-antlers shadow. Confirmed same-day follow-up: Rudy rams the Huntsman with the truck — a gun was ruled out as too dangerous to trailer-park residents. This is both the book's Prologue and the origin of Jamie and Rudy's relationship. See Jamie.
  • Aftermath of Rudy Seeing Jamie's Mein: How does witnessing Jamie's true Mien in the Hedge reshape Rudy's perspective on his past cases? What might it cost him later? Raised but not developed. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): It doesn't reshape his view of past cases. Rudy's never dealt with fae/Changelings in any meaningful way before, and he already knew Jamie was different long before seeing the Mien — the reveal confirms what he'd already accepted, rather than recontextualizing anything from his history. See Jamie.

docs.title: Rudy Williams — Who Is "She"? (resolved: Amber)
docs.text: A chat referenced Rudy updating his will so that an unnamed "she" — someone who "earns it" — would inherit Shepherd's Inn instead of Nathan Hale. The full "Escalating Tensions Rewrite" transcript surfaced a separate, fully drafted letter from Rudy to Hale (a brainstormed future/"Book 4" beat) that gives the Inn to Hale outright, with instructions to give Jenn half if he ever sells — strongly suggesting Jenn is "she." A second candidate, Amber, and a likely-redundant third (an unnamed protégé, per "Supernatural Horror Publishers Guide") were also in play. Not fully resolved. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Amber — Dave confirmed directly ("I believe I meant Amber"). The mechanism: the Clara McKenzie case exposes her to the supernatural and proves to Rudy she's fully capable; combined with her existing bar experience, he hires her to cover shifts at Shepherd's Inn, which also solves the practical problem of not having to explain coming home covered in blood. The Jenn letter is superseded, rougher, earlier brainstorming, not an alternate answer. The "unnamed protégé" Book 7 candidate is just another reference to Amber, not a third option. See Amber.

docs.title: Rudy Williams — The Hollow Creek Rumor
docs.text: Hale tells Jenn an unconfirmed rumor that Rudy shot and killed a man near "Hollow Creek" years ago in the middle of the night — no body, no charges, just someone who stopped showing up and Rudy with blood on his boots. Never confirmed, denied, or explained anywhere in the source material — real crime, supernatural encounter Rudy can't/won't explain, or town gossip with no basis? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): True — the same event as "A Human He Killed": a young-generation vampire, killed proactively, whose body didn't decay on final death and so read as an ordinary human kill. See Rudy Williams.

docs.title: The Prodigal Son (Book 2) — New Open Questions
docs.text:
• How did Rudy actually get Shepherd's Inn? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Rudy bought it himself with saved service back-pay and odd-job money; the Skinwalker case made it affordable (the previous owner was the Skinwalker's victim, family sold cheap to be rid of it). See Rudy Williams.
  • What is Mickey building, and why? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): The Three Generations Ritual construct (see The Three Generations Ritual). Allen and Frank are the nursing-home body-swap — Allen is fleshcrafted into a copy of the real target, Frank, so Frank can be abducted without staff noticing. See Mickey Two-Dice.
  • Is the Dawes case truly unresolved? Executive Dawes's kidnapped son's fate is never shown — only recalled as backstory once Jeff Speno's son goes missing under an identical MO. Are Speno's case and Dawes's case the same perpetrator returning, a copycat, or something else? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Dawes's case is The Three Generations Ritual's ~20-years-ago case — Dawes is the father, and his son survived (the girlfriend/fiancée and unborn child didn't). That surviving son, now grown, is "the kid" — Mickey's present-day manipulated assistant with a vendetta against Rudy (see Mickey Two-Dice). Speno's case is a new, unrelated family Mickey chose to recreate the ritual with — a recurrence of the pattern, not the same bloodline.
  • What is "Second King County Chronicle (Untitled)" now, if The Prodigal Son is confirmed Book 2? Resolved: Dave confirmed they're the same book — the generational-trauma/repeating-ritual themes are meant to be true of The Prodigal Son, with how they connect to the drafted chapters (the Dawes/Speno recurring MO, Mickey's warehouse ritual) to be worked out in future chats. See The Prodigal Son (A King County Chronicle).
  • Who is "the kid"? Mickey references an unnamed young assistant twice (can't get into the nursing home, has "his own shit to do," expected back before the warehouse project's next phase) — never named or described. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session — identity confirmed same day): Executive Dawes's son — the now-grown survivor from The Three Generations Ritual's ~20-years-ago case, whose pregnant girlfriend/fiancée and unborn child died. Mickey is manipulating his grief, using it as fuel to help recreate the ritual and to go after Rudy — the kid genuinely wants revenge on Rudy for failing to save them; Mickey personally couldn't care less about Rudy at this point, he's just useful. Still unnamed on the page (only "Dawes's son"), but no longer a mystery whose son he is. This also resolves the previously-flagged "vendetta targets Mickey vs. Rudy" inconsistency on that page — it's Rudy.
  • What happened to Sarah? Jeff Speno's son's missing tutor — accomplice, second victim, or red herring entirely unrelated to the kidnapping? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): A Doll candidate — see Sarah.
  • LeGrande Station timeline conflict: Hale bought/renovated the station "three years" before this book's events, per the manuscript — in tension with the "roughly ten years" of feud established elsewhere. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Six years — splitting the difference, since the exact figure doesn't matter much. See LeGrande Station.
  • The unnamed rival hunter group in Stanton City — mentioned once, never developed. Allies, competitors, or a future complication for Rudy's own network? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Likely not part of this continuity at all — Dave suspects this is cross-contamination from a separate, unrelated in-person King County tabletop game he runs for friends, not this book series. If they're ever used here anyway, they're allies, not competitors.
  • A smartphone appears in the Prologue (the nursing home guard), conflicting with the established "no smartphones exist in King County" tech rule from "Ghosts of King County." Slip to fix, or does the rule need revisiting? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): No smartphones — a slip predating Dave's locked-down tech rules, fix in a future draft pass.
  • Do Allen and Frank resemble each other? The nurse who interrupts the Prologue scene addresses the sitting figure (Allen) as "Frank" before noticing Mickey — even though the real Frank is asleep in the bed. Could be a clue about why Mickey chose these two specific patients. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session — supersedes an earlier same-day "parked as a manuscript slip" pass, before "Bridging Story Gaps" was reviewed): Intentional — Mickey is fleshcrafting Allen into a copy of Frank at that exact moment, and the nurse's mistake is the disguise visibly working. See Mickey Two-Dice.
  • Prologue prose vs. the "Harv can't physically interact/isn't perceptible to others" rule: Harv's dialogue ("if you let me... I offered... All you had to do was ask") and Mickey's line about Harv/Missy being overheard both read as though Harv had a choice and could be perceived by others — in tension with Dave's direct clarification. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Harv and Missy have full free will and autonomy — they can move around, observe, and eavesdrop independently, just without any meaningful physical effect on the world. Harv's "I offered... all you had to do was ask" almost certainly refers to offering to scout/watch/follow (things he can do), not to physically help drag a body (which he genuinely can't) — he's confirmed lazy about volunteering for that kind of task even though he's capable of it. Mickey's "they're gonna hear us" line remains an unresolved small wrinkle — no other entity has ever actually perceived Harv or Missy as of this book's events, so that line reads as Mickey's own confusion/paranoia about their nature rather than a real risk. See Harv.

docs.title: GUMSHOE Sources — Prepped But Never Used In Play
docs.text: A larger batch of Sources was brainstormed for Rudy's One-2-One game alongside the ones with existing pages (Detective Marcus Cole, Reverend Tobias Gray, Avery Greene, Nina Caldwell, Old Man West). These three have never come up in an actual session and have no pages yet — worth building out properly if/when they're actually used, rather than guessing at placement now: Professor Lydia Hawthorne (Anthropology/Archaeology/History/Theology, originally suggested for Montrose), Dr. Fiona Blackwood (Biology/Medicine/Pharmacy/Chemistry, originally Grand Junction), Professor Milton Larkin (Astronomy/Physics/Cthulhu Mythos, originally Vail). A fourth, Eliot Johnson (Medicine/Biology/Chemistry, a biochemist/former paramedic in Delta, deliberately differentiated from Aaron "Doc" Mabey), was iterated on twice but still hasn't appeared in a session.

docs.title: The Disappearance of Clara McKenzie — Open Threads from the Actual-Play Sessions
docs.text: Note (2026-07-22): This is a paused actual-play campaign — these threads are open because play hasn't continued, not because they're forks needing a desk decision. Leave them as session cliffhangers until the game resumes. - Is Detective Marcus Cole compromised? He spent the day actively helping Rudy investigate (IDing Zack Sawyer, pulling Danny's file), then his handwriting turned up on a warning note at Zack's ransacked trailer telling Rudy to back off. Genuinely dirty, impersonated/framed, or something else? Unresolved cliffhanger. - Who is Deputy Humboldt, really? New to King County (transferred from Nevada), yet knew Rudy's name on sight with no explained connection. Also the prime (unconfirmed) suspect in the missing cassette tape. (Name itself resolved 2026-07-16 as "Humboldt" — the one-off "Holcomb" was a misremembering, not a real alternate.) - What is Bram, really? Flinched when called out for knowing Rudy's name unprompted, then left town in an unexplained high-end Audi rather than continuing to hitchhike. The "famous musician in disguise" idea from planning was never actually confirmed in play. - Who sent Rudy the "We know you're in town" text? Arrived by blocked number right after Amina Kahn's visit — could be her unnamed employers, or something entirely unconnected to her. - Who is the woman at Zack's trailer? Resolved as Amber — see her page. - Who or what is The Broker? Zack's mysterious contact, offering him a way "out" of something for cash, the night he disappeared/was taken. Confirmed to hold an "Old Deal" debt over Detective Marcus Cole, but true identity/nature still unknown. - What happened to Clara, and what happened to Zack? Neither has been found, dead or alive. The "sacrifice... ritual in the woods" Clara overheard is still just a rumor at this point — never confirmed as real supernatural activity. A ruin at Raven's Creek is the next lead, not yet investigated. - Who is the "goth girl"? An unnamed young woman who led Clara, Zack, and Ashley deeper into the woods the night of the party, with a crew that hung back at the party's edge. Distinct from Ashley. Zack's journal suggests he kept seeing her afterward ("Saw her again tonight. She looked… wrong.") — never named or identified. - What's Deputy Humboldt's real connection to Rudy? Per the GM's own framing during play (not yet dramatized in a scene): tied to Rudy's time in the Marines, implying Rudy did something that divided Humboldt's family. Also been loitering around the Brindle service station for weeks, apparently watching for someone or something. - Is Ashley involved, or just another witness? Guarded, cagey at Lucky's Place, part of the group that went into the woods with Clara — her interview was still in progress when the session log cut off.

docs.title: Rudy Williams — Would He Ever Tell Gil About the Discharge?
docs.text: • Per Dave (2026-07-16): Gil almost certainly doesn't know about Rudy's dishonorable discharge, since Rudy never talks about his own service. Dave's own read is that Gil would probably understand and look past it if Rudy actually explained — but whether Rudy would ever take that chance is left open, not decided.

docs.title: Rudy Williams — Height Discrepancy (resolved, reaffirmed 2026-07-16)
docs.text:
• 5'9" was floated early in "Ghosts of King County" during initial portrait description, but 5'7" is what actually got used once play started — confirmed explicitly within that same chat ("Rudy is only 5'7"", contrasted with Deputy Humboldt's 6'1") and echoed again in the separate "Escalating Tensions Rewrite" chat (shorter than Nathan Hale). 5'7" is settled, final — Dave reaffirmed this directly rather than leaving the two figures both sitting on the page, and added a real-world justification: Marine Scout Snipers are reportedly preferred shorter, since it's easier to stay concealed. See Rudy Williams.

### Nathan Hale — "Never Escalates" vs. Two Full Blowup Drafts Hale's page previously stated the Rudy/Hale feud is passive-aggressive and never escalates, per the Act Beat Refinement Help outlining pass (2025-05-29). The full "Escalating Tensions Rewrite" transcript (2025-06-04) is built entirely around justifying a real explosive shouting match ending in a slur, drafted twice in different settings. These directly conflict and haven't been reconciled — is the day-to-day feud really passive-aggressive with one big blowup as an exception, or does one of these framings need to be abandoned? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Mostly passive-aggressive. Rudy does let his temper flare during cases, but only because he's certain Hale will never physically retaliate — those flares (all three existing drafted blowup scenes, including The Prodigal Son's, which needs a rewrite pass to actually read smaller) are the safe, small kind. The one real explosion is a future beat, not yet written — it happens during a case that feels genuinely impossible, and afterward Jenn calls Rudy out on it, defending Hale as a good guy underneath. See Nathan Hale.

docs.title: Caleb / What Wasn't and What Was
docs.text:
• Something in the Woods: During his investigation, Rudy considers an animal-attack theory tied to local rumors about "something in the woods" near Caleb's home. Never developed beyond a passing lead — is there actually something out there, and if so, what? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): A dead end for the case — but there genuinely is something out there, almost certainly mundane (a local cougar, bobcat, or coyote), that's killed stray small dogs, cats, and chickens. Nothing larger. Not supernatural, not connected to Caleb's disappearance.
  • The Previous Case: Act 1.2 has Rudy wrapping up "a small, low-stakes case — perhaps a missing item or a petty theft for a neighbor" before Leanne arrives. Never defined beyond that placeholder — what was it, and should it thematically contrast with the Caleb case? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Deliberately left undefined. Dave likes the device of mentioning cases that never get fleshed out beyond the mention — this one might become something eventually, but right now it's nothing on purpose, not a placeholder waiting to be filled.

docs.title: Rudy Williams — Which Kid Does He Think Is His?
docs.text: Confirmed (per "Rudy Jenn Age Gap," full data export): Rudy briefly suspects a child in What Wasn't and What Was might be his own biological kid, disproven almost immediately. Never states which child. Given the book's plot, Caleb is the obvious candidate, but that's an inference, not a confirmed fact — worth deciding deliberately, especially given Caleb's ultimate reveal (never actually born) would make this an even heavier beat if it's him. Resolved (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session): Not Caleb — it's Tina's son, Ben, roughly the right age to make Rudy briefly wonder. Tina confirms Ben isn't his, and that if Rudy hadn't left her, she'd never have met Ben's actual father, who's still around and "a pretty awesome guy." See Tina.

docs.title: Rudy Williams — A Human He Killed (resolved)
docs.text:
Per "Vampire Population Ratio" (full data export), Rudy believes he once killed a human — not a monster — who wasn't an immediate active threat, though Rudy rationalized it at the time as a future threat. Dave's own words: "I've never figured out the specifics." A separate fresh-eyes pass flagged a possible connection to the Mickey/Passion-forced-trigger-finger hostage scene, unconfirmed. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Not the same event as the Mickey/Passion hostage scene — that connection is retired. This was most likely a young-generation vampire, a nomad or new King County resident, not yet an active threat when Rudy killed it (he hunts vampires proactively on principle — see his established motive tied to The California Incident). Young vampires don't rapidly decay/age on final death the way old ones do, so the body came out fresh and human-looking. Rudy knows it wasn't murder, but he won't reveal the truth — maintaining the "killed a human" impression protects the innocent rather than exposing the supernatural. See Rudy Williams.

### Possible ChatGPT Contamination — "Ben" - "Jane Doe Investigation Plausibility" (full data export) mentions a character named "Ben" exactly once, with zero elaboration, as someone who "meets Jamie and explains the kids' fear and rituals" during Rudy and Jamie's first trip into the Hedge. Doesn't match any established creek kid (Goblin/Juicebox/Claw/Lint) or any other named character in the vault. Could be a dropped character, an early/alternate name for one of the creek kids, or noise — flagged per standing practice, not assumed to be real. Resolved (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session): Not contamination — Ben is real, and he's Tina's son (see her page and the resolved "Which Kid Does He Think Is His?" item above). He's the creek kid who meets Jamie at the tree line, sneaking out early to nail his baby sister's baby tooth to the tree near the Hedge opening as a folk-charm against the Huntsman, whom the kids call "the Hollowman." Confirmed as a deliberate reuse of an existing character rather than a new NPC. See Ben.

### Possible ChatGPT Contamination — "Clara Jennings" A worked JSON-schema example in "Solo RPG Session JSON" (full data export) names the central missing-person case "The Disappearance of Clara Jennings," rather than the established Clara McKenzie. The user never introduced or engaged with this name — it only appears in the assistant's illustrative example — so per the standing skepticism about orphaned AI-generated names, treat this as likely contamination/hallucination, not a real alternate name, unless independently confirmed. Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Confirmed AI noise, not a real alternate name. Clara McKenzie stands.

### Mickey Two-Dice — Blood-Mage vs. Straightforward Malkavian The vault's existing framing (from "Bridging Story Gaps") describes Mickey as pursuing Golconda via a specific blood ritual, phrased loosely as "blood magic." "Vampire Population Ratio" describes him in purely Malkavian-vampire terms (diablerie, The Passion, fleshcraft) without ever using "Golconda" or "blood-mage." Not necessarily contradictory — the ritual/fleshcraft activities could just be what "blood magic" was describing all along — but worth deciding deliberately rather than assuming they're the same framing. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Malkavian, not a blood-mage — but not "straightforward" either. He'll dabble in anything he believes might grant true immortality (or get closer to it), which is why the ritual work reads like blood magic without him actually being a trained practitioner of it — in The Prodigal Son, he's working from manuscripts alone, past what he really understands. See Mickey Two-Dice.

docs.title: The Huntsman — Two Unreconciled Resolutions
docs.text:
The Detective Noir alternate outline has this Huntsman survive, its power merely cut away via a town-wide belief-in-Caleb mechanic ("you can't destroy doubt"). The main outline (per "Clue Evaluation Assistance," full data export) has Jamie genuinely kill it inside the Hedge after naming it "Steven." These can't both be the ending — not reconciled, may just reflect two different drafts of the climax rather than two facts about the same scene. Resolved (2026-07-15, decided live, grill-canon session — cascades directly from the outline-primacy decision above): The main outline is canon, so Jamie genuinely kills the Huntsman inside the Hedge after naming it "Steven" — that's the real ending. The "defanged, not destroyed" resolutions from the Detective Noir, Character-Driven, and Mystery Crime Thriller alternates are retired along with the outlines they came from. See The Huntsman.

### The Huntsman — Who actually lands the killing blow (new fork, flagged 2026-07-16, fresh-eyes pass on "Westernizing What Wasn't") The resolution directly above has Jamie deliver the kill, naming/taunting the Huntsman as "Steven" before and after ("Hi, Steven" ... "Bye, Steven"). But "Westernizing What Wasn't" — confirmed elsewhere as resolving the location (Arcadia) and timing (end of Act II) of this same confrontation — describes the actual killing blow as Rudy's, using a cold iron dagger, with Moss (Jamie) providing the opening/support rather than throwing the final strike (drafted line: Rudy — "No. But I understand what you did." — then "they end it"). These may not be strictly incompatible (Jamie could still deliver the "Bye, Steven" line while Rudy's the one who physically strikes), but who actually wields the weapon is a real, unresolved detail worth pinning down directly rather than assuming. Resolved (2026-07-18, decided live, grill-canon session): The integration Dave was already leaning toward — Jamie delivers the "Bye, Steven" taunt, Rudy actually drives the cold iron dagger home. This surfaced a real characterization split worth keeping: Jamie has never willingly killed anything and isn't about to start here, while Rudy already is a killer and carries real, unresolved doubt about whether that's already his definition of himself. See The Huntsman, Jamie, and Rudy Williams.

docs.title: Rudy Williams — "Passive Truth-Lure" (brainstormed, not adopted)
docs.text: • Floated during clue-structure brainstorming as a possible subtle supernatural quirk: strangers who aren't emotionally invested in Rudy (waitresses, gas station clerks) tend to over-share secrets and context around him without prompting, as if he draws truth out passively. Never confirmed as canon or given game-mechanical terms (Hunter: the Vigil / GUMSHOE), and no scene in the current outline explicitly demonstrates it — worth revisiting if it resurfaces.

docs.title: What Wasn't and What Was — "Older Kids Only" (resolved)
docs.text: The RV guy red herring includes an unexplained detail: kids dare each other to sneak up and touch his RV, but it's always the older kids who do it, never the younger ones. Introduced deliberately as a real clue (per the "a clue must lead somewhere" principle), but no payoff has been decided yet — what does it actually mean, and does it ever get addressed on the page? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): A minor clue, not important to the case itself. Younger kids are simply afraid of the RV; older kids feed that fear by daring each other to touch it, since younger kids don't have the same rites-of-passage dynamic. "Older" and "younger" stay deliberately undefined — no specific age or grade cutoff. See The Lawyer in the RV.

docs.title: Minor Characters Introduced, Not Yet Named
docs.text: Explicitly left unnamed for now (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Dave's call — not an oversight, just not needed yet. - Rudy's pregnant friend: An old Hunter-days contact who settled down, mentioned in Act 2.7 as the one whose offhand comment about birth stories cracks the case open for Rudy. Unnamed. - Caleb's dad: Not currently with Leanne, "maybe never more than a scared, fragile couple," possibly clean now or not. Unnamed — see the discrepancy note on Leanne's page about whether this is the same person as her established "husband." Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Same person as the husband — no "not his real dad" drama intended. Still unnamed. See Leanne. - The CPS worker: Had doubts about Leanne's paperwork, visited by Rudy in Act 3.1 while gathering evidence. Unnamed. - The car lender: The person who lent Leanne the unregistered, uninsured car — a recurring source across Acts 2 and 3. Unnamed. - The photo developer: Tentatively suggested (not confirmed) as a source for an undated ultrasound print from a kiosk. Unnamed, optional. - The "RV man": Mentioned in passing as one of the story's false leads, alongside "the Hedge near Jamie's exit" — not elaborated anywhere else found so far. - Sheriff Ostler as a future Slasher (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): That was purely Poker Night's own unresolved ending — the players never actually dealt with Ostler, and Dave's plan for him to return as a Slasher never got used there either. Not adopted into King County canon, but not ruled out — Dave's fine with him resurfacing eventually, just nothing important right now. Dormant thread, not an open question. See Boot Knife. - The drug-run town: The small, run-down pass-through town where Christie Reese operates and Moss's Near Miss happens. Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Red Bridge — based on Olathe, CO, per a Google Docs town-naming reference Dave rediscovered. See The Huntsman. - The asylum insider (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Someone on staff at Phoebe's institution knows she's a wild mage and wrote the chart-based drugging regimen that keeps her physically suppressed — the rest of the institution just follows the chart without understanding why. Unnamed; who they are and why they're doing it is a real open thread, not just an unnamed-for-flavor character. See Phoebe.

docs.title: Nathan Hale
docs.text: • Roots of the Feud: The animosity between Hale and Rudy is framed around roughly six years (see the resolved LeGrande Station timeline) of property disputes over Shepherd's Inn and LeGrande Station — but is that really all there is to it, or is there a deeper, unresolved history underneath the surface-level squabble over property values? Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): No deeper hidden history — property disputes really are mostly it, compounded by a genuine philosophical disagreement about what Shepherd Junction should even be (Hale wants a thriving I-70 tourist stop; Rudy wants a sleepy nowhere town) — deliberately mundane and boring on purpose, not a withheld secret. Plus a low-key layer of mutual jealousy over Jenn: nothing specific, just that each man is her friend and it quietly bothers the other. See Nathan Hale.

docs.title: California Werewolf Pack
docs.text:
• Presence in King County: Does the pack avoid King County altogether due to its tainted reputation, or are there certain areas where they’re known to operate? Could they have insights into the area’s supernatural properties?
  • Fate of the Blood-Bound Pack Member: What happened to the werewolf who was blood-bound to the vampire? Was this pack member ever freed, or could their fate still be intertwined with the vampire’s influence?
  • Potential Allies or Tense Encounters: If the pack and Rudy cross paths, would they be inclined to join forces against shared supernatural threats, or would they view him as a dangerous outsider due to his connections?
  • Knowledge of the No-Man’s-Land: Does the pack have any lore or myths explaining why King County is considered a no-man’s-land, particularly in relation to Wyrm-tainted energy or interdimensional threats? They may hold pieces of information that could be key to uncovering its mystery.

docs.title: King County, Colorado
docs.text:
• Source of the Wyrm Taint: Could the area's no-man's-land status be tied to a specific supernatural event, ritual, or curse that happened long ago? Is it possible that the energy from the vampire's portal incident somehow resonates with King County's taint? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Same ancient wound as the one behind the California portal — an old crack into the Abyss with multiple weak points, King County's being far older than 1986. See The Vampire.
  • Connection to Other Realms: The interdimensional qualities of King County may mean that it serves as a bridge to other realms, potentially the same one the vampire tried to access in California. Is there a portal or a weakened veil in the area that could explain its reputation? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Yes — same wound, same realm (the Abyss/Low Umbra). See King County, Colorado.
  • Unusual Influence on Residents: Could the residual supernatural energy of King County be affecting its human residents, even if they’re unaware of it? This influence might manifest as unexplained behaviors, lingering illnesses, or general unease among those who live close to certain landmarks.
  • Phoebe's Connection to King County: Was Phoebe's asylum placement in King County coincidental, or could her presence be linked to the supernatural properties of the area? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Not coincidental, and not conscious destiny either — resonance. Her Awakening put her in direct contact with the same wound that runs under King County; a place already thin like that is exactly the kind of place that can hold someone touched the way she was. See Phoebe.

docs.title: The California Incident
docs.text:
• Setting name conflict: The vault's plain framing is "a small town in California," but a separate "Lore post" brainstorm retconned the setting twice — first to San Bruno, CA (a Silent Hill homage), then to Baton Rouge, LA (the real 1987 film's rumored intended setting). None of these have been reconciled; not resolved here on purpose. A later chat ("Vampire Population Ratio") floats calling the event "The Louisiana Incident" to match, while also floating that "California Incident" and "Louisiana Incident" could just coexist as two different pieces of hunter-network slang for the same event — an option, not a decision. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Staying with California — plain "a small town in California." Reasoning: it makes more sense for Deputy Humboldt to know of a California incident than a Louisiana one, given proximity to Colorado. San Bruno, Baton Rouge, and "The Louisiana Incident" are all set aside. See The California Incident.
  • Phoebe's agency in sealing the portal: The core vault framing has Scary German Guy using Phoebe's latent power as a "battery" for his own ritual. A later chat ("Vampire Population Ratio") instead frames her as the one who sealed the breach herself via a spontaneous Awakening. Possibly compatible (her burst of power channeled through his ritual) but not explicitly reconciled anywhere. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): Spontaneous Awakening — she did the work herself; Scary German Guy was there and involved, but not the source of the power. See The California Incident.
  • The no-diary gap: Without the film's German-language diary, there's no confirmed hook explaining how the kids specifically end up needing Scary German Guy's help. Two options were brainstormed (German writing found directly in the Vampire's lair; the kids spotting him scouting the hideout himself as a possible Mage/Hunter) but neither was chosen. Resolved (2026-07-16, decided live, grill-canon session): He was already independently investigating the Vampire's house when the kids arrived on their own; they insisted they were part of it now, and either he begrudgingly worked with them or the two groups got in each other's way. See Scary German Guy.
  • True Purpose of the Portal: Was the vampire's goal simply to access another realm, or was he serving a greater supernatural entity? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Pure Abyss-obsession — he meant to drag the Abyss through into the town, not visit it or serve anyone else. See The Vampire. Still open: Could the portal's influence extend beyond California, potentially explaining King County's no-man's-land status? — see the still-open item below.
  • Scary German Guy’s Knowledge: Did Scary German Guy understand more about the portal’s influence than he shared with the Monster Squad? His role may have been more complex, with knowledge that Rudy could uncover if he finds him.
  • Effect on King County: Could the portal ritual resonate with King County's supernatural properties? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Yes — same ancient wound, two weak points. See King County, Colorado and The Vampire.
  • Unfinished Business: If any energy or spirit from the portal lingers, it may still be tied to the deaths of the other Monster Squad members, influencing Rudy’s life and driving his obsession with protecting Phoebe.

docs.title: The Vampire
docs.text:
• True Identity and Purpose: What drove the Vampire to open the portal? Was he seeking power, or was he bound to some greater supernatural force, perhaps connected to the Wyrm? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Pure Abyss-obsession, true Lasombra fashion — not power, not service to a greater entity. He meant to drag the Abyss through to engulf the town, not open a door and step through it himself. See The Vampire.
  • Lingering Influence Over the Blood-Bound Werewolf: If the werewolf bound to him wasn’t truly freed, there may be residual effects of the Vampire’s influence. It’s possible the werewolf remains affected, potentially posing a future threat to King County or even to Rudy.
  • Portal Connections Beyond California: Did the Vampire's portal magic have ramifications beyond the California town, possibly influencing places like King County? Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Not the ritual reaching outward — King County's wound was already there, ancient, the same crack the Vampire found a different weak point along. See King County, Colorado.
  • Final Fate of the Vampire: Is the Vampire truly gone, or could he have survived in some form? Resolved (pre-existing, cross-referenced 2026-07-22): Trapped in the Abyss roughly 30 years as of King County's "present day" — confirmed to return later in the series alongside The Promethean, at which point Mickey diablerizes him. See The Vampire.

docs.title: The Promethean
docs.text:
• Fate Beyond the Portal: If the Promethean survived his journey through the portal, could he be attempting to continue his Pilgrimage on the other side? Confirmed he and the Vampire do return together roughly 30 years later — the exact mechanism/circumstances are deliberately left open (2026-07-22, grill-canon session).
  • Connection to Phoebe's Condition: Given his bond with Phoebe, could her catatonic state be influenced by his disappearance? There may be a psychic or magical link between them that lingers. Resolved (2026-07-22, decided live, grill-canon session): Real, active link — Astral (High Umbra) and Abyss (Low Umbra) are adjacent Umbra layers, plus their trauma bond. See Phoebe and The Promethean.
  • Residual Wasteland Effect in King County: If the Wyrm-taint in King County has origins connected to The Vampire and Promethean, the Promethean’s Disquiet may resonate here. His journey toward humanity could be leaving subtle effects on the land, either amplifying or counteracting the area’s supernatural isolation.
  • Ongoing Pilgrimage and Redemption: If the Promethean returns, could he be further along in his Pilgrimage, perhaps possessing greater knowledge or even new supernatural abilities? His desire for humanity may make him an ally to Rudy, provided they can find common ground in their shared trauma.

docs.title: Supernatural Stuff
docs.text:
• Doc’s Purpose: What has brought Doc to King County seeking Rudy’s help? Is there a specific threat he believes only Rudy can handle, or is his presence tied to a broader supernatural concern?
  • Mickey’s Blood Hunt: How long can Mickey Two-Dice stay under the radar before the Blood Hunt catches up with him? Will he seek Rudy’s help or protection, or is his presence a liability to others?
  • Silas’s Role as a Seer: How much does Silas Freeman know, and what prophecies might he share with those in need? His visions could reveal hints about King County’s supernatural nature or offer warnings about incoming threats.
  • Further Unknown Entities: Are there other supernatural creatures that lie dormant or hidden, either avoiding Rudy’s attention or quietly influencing King County’s ecosystem?

docs.title: Out of Scope Ideas
docs.text: Running log of ideas surfaced in brainstorming chats that didn't get fully integrated elsewhere — discarded alternates, half-formed threads, or things that seemed tangential at the time. Kept here rather than dropped, since this vault is a brainstorming repo first and a source of truth second. Check this list against new chats to see if an idea resurfaces (a sign it's more load-bearing than it looked) or can finally be fleshed out.

docs.title: From "King County Chronicles 1 prewriting" (Google Docs dump, 2026-07-22) — name not adopted
docs.text: • Katelyn la Rouge — a Toreador vampire from Dave's separate Castle Dell, GA play-by-post game, Mickey's friend and possibly protégé there. Died in that game. Explicitly not being brought into these stories — flagged only because the name surfaced once with zero context, not because there's anything to build on.

docs.title: From "Jane Doe Investigation Plausibility" (full data export, 2025) — rejected alternate reveal
docs.text: • Caleb as a mannequin, Leanne as "a different kind of crazy" — a fully non-supernatural alternate version of the central reveal, floated and explicitly rejected by Dave in his own words: "More sad. WAY less fun." Kept the supernatural/belief-based reveal instead.

docs.title: From "Clue Evaluation Assistance" (full data export, 2025) — superseded pivot mechanism
docs.text: • Discarded early version of "what sends Rudy to the Hedge" — an earlier plan had clearing The Lawyer in the RV red herring directly reveal that the area is where Jamie originally came out of the Hedge, i.e. the RV-guy resolution itself was the pivot. This was superseded by the later, more developed "tall quiet man near the creek" bus-stop mechanism confirmed in "Jane Doe Investigation Plausibility" (see What Wasn't and What Was) — the RV-guy clearing and the Hedge pivot are now two separate beats, not one.

docs.title: From "Clue Evaluation Assistance" / "Jane Doe Investigation Plausibility" (full data export, 2025)
docs.text:
• Christmas-themed final epilogue photo — explicitly rejected by Dave ("I kept going back to 'christmas' or some bullshit like that, but the only real option is...") in favor of the confirmed "First day of school" sign. See What Wasn't and What Was.
  • A parent saying something like "some shadow monster down by the creek" directly, in Act 1 — rejected by Dave as "too big a nudge."
  • A batch of "one more supernatural clue" options offered before the bus-stop/creek-riprap mechanism was developed: a flickering missing-poster date, a library checkout log signed "Caleb" in eerie handwriting, a photo that changes shirt color, a drawing of a boy holding hands with an antlered figure, Jamie saying "You were there. At the barn. Before." Dave's response ("I need to think this over.
